 18 | 
 19 | def resolve_name(name, *additional_parts):
 20 |     """Resolve a name like ``module.object`` to an object and return it.
 21 | 
 22 |     This ends up working like ``from module import object`` but is easier
 23 |     to deal with than the `__import__` builtin and supports digging into
 24 |     submodules.
 25 | 
 26 |     Parameters
 27 |     ----------
 28 | 
 29 |     name : `str`
 30 |         A dotted path to a Python object--that is, the name of a function,
 31 |         class, or other object in a module with the full path to that module,
 32 |         including parent modules, separated by dots.  Also known as the fully
 33 |         qualified name of the object.
 34 | 
 35 |     additional_parts : iterable, optional
 36 |         If more than one positional arguments are given, those arguments are
 37 |         automatically dotted together with ``name``.
 38 | 
 39 |     Examples
 40 |     --------
 41 | 
 42 |     >>> resolve_name('astropy.utils.introspection.resolve_name')
 43 |     <function resolve_name at 0x...>
 44 |     >>> resolve_name('astropy', 'utils', 'introspection', 'resolve_name')
 45 |     <function resolve_name at 0x...>
 46 | 
 47 |     Raises
 48 |     ------
 49 |     `ImportError`
 50 |         If the module or named object is not found.
 51 |     """
 52 | 
 53 |     additional_parts = '.'.join(additional_parts)
 54 | 
 55 |     if additional_parts:
 56 |         name = name + '.' + additional_parts
 57 | 
 58 |     parts = name.split('.')
 59 | 
 60 |     if len(parts) == 1:
 61 |         # No dots in the name--just a straight up module import
 62 |         cursor = 1
 63 |         fromlist = []
 64 |     else:
 65 |         cursor = len(parts) - 1
 66 |         fromlist = [parts[-1]]
 67 | 
 68 |     module_name = parts[:cursor]
 69 | 
 70 |     while cursor > 0:
 71 |         try:
 72 |             ret = __import__(str('.'.join(module_name)), fromlist=fromlist)
 73 |             break
 74 |         except ImportError:
 75 |             if cursor == 0:
 76 |                 raise
 77 |             cursor -= 1
 78 |             module_name = parts[:cursor]
 79 |             fromlist = [parts[cursor]]
 80 |             ret = ''
 81 | 
 82 |     for part in parts[cursor:]:
 83 |         try:
 84 |             ret = getattr(ret, part)
 85 |         except AttributeError:
 86 |             raise ImportError(name)
 87 | 
 88 |     return ret
 89 | 
 90 | 
 91 | def minversion(module, version, inclusive=True, version_path='__version__'):
 92 |     """
 93 |     Returns `True` if the specified Python module satisfies a minimum version
 94 |     requirement, and `False` if not.
 95 | 
 96 |     Parameters
 97 |     ----------
 98 | 
 99 |     module : module or `str`
100 |         An imported module of which to check the version, or the name of
101 |         that module (in which case an import of that module is attempted--
102 |         if this fails `False` is returned).
103 | 
104 |     version : `str`
105 |         The version as a string that this module must have at a minimum (e.g.
106 |         ``'0.12'``).
107 | 
108 |     inclusive : `bool`
109 |         The specified version meets the requirement inclusively (i.e. ``>=``)
110 |         as opposed to strictly greater than (default: `True`).
111 | 
112 |     version_path : `str`
113 |         A dotted attribute path to follow in the module for the version.
114 |         Defaults to just ``'__version__'``, which should work for most Python
115 |         modules.
116 | 
117 |     Examples
118 |     --------
119 | 
120 |     >>> import astropy
121 |     >>> minversion(astropy, '0.4.4')
122 |     True
123 |     """
124 |     if isinstance(module, types.ModuleType):
125 |         module_name = module.__name__
126 |     elif isinstance(module, str):
127 |         module_name = module
128 |         try:
129 |             module = resolve_name(module_name)
130 |         except ImportError:
131 |             return False
132 |     else:
133 |         raise ValueError('module argument must be an actual imported '
134 |                          'module, or the import name of the module; '
135 |                          'got {0!r}'.format(module))
136 | 
137 |     if '.' not in version_path:
138 |         have_version = getattr(module, version_path)
139 |     else:
140 |         have_version = resolve_name(module.__name__, version_path)
141 | 
142 |     if inclusive:
143 |         return LooseVersion(have_version) >= LooseVersion(version)
144 |     else:
145 |         return LooseVersion(have_version) > LooseVersion(version)
146 | 
147 | 
148 | def find_current_module(depth=1, finddiff=False):
149 |     """
150 |     Determines the module/package from which this function is called.
151 | 
152 |     This function has two modes, determined by the ``finddiff`` option. it
153 |     will either simply go the requested number of frames up the call
154 |     stack (if ``finddiff`` is False), or it will go up the call stack until
155 |     it reaches a module that is *not* in a specified set.
156 | 
157 |     Parameters
158 |     ----------
159 |     depth : int
160 |         Specifies how far back to go in the call stack (0-indexed, so that
161 |         passing in 0 gives back `astropy.utils.misc`).
162 |     finddiff : bool or list
163 |         If False, the returned ``mod`` will just be ``depth`` frames up from
164 |         the current frame. Otherwise, the function will start at a frame
165 |         ``depth`` up from current, and continue up the call stack to the
166 |         first module that is *different* from those in the provided list.
167 |         In this case, ``finddiff`` can be a list of modules or modules
168 |         names. Alternatively, it can be True, which will use the module
169 |         ``depth`` call stack frames up as the module the returned module
170 |         most be different from.
171 | 
172 |     Returns
173 |     -------
174 |     mod : module or None
175 |         The module object or None if the package cannot be found. The name of
176 |         the module is available as the ``__name__`` attribute of the returned
177 |         object (if it isn't None).
178 | 
179 |     Raises
180 |     ------
181 |     ValueError
182 |         If ``finddiff`` is a list with an invalid entry.
183 | 
184 |     Examples
185 |     --------
186 |     The examples below assume that there are two modules in a package named
187 |     ``pkg``. ``mod1.py``::
188 | 
189 |         def find1():
190 |             from astropy.utils import find_current_module
191 |             print find_current_module(1).__name__
192 |         def find2():
193 |             from astropy.utils import find_current_module
194 |             cmod = find_current_module(2)
195 |             if cmod is None:
196 |                 print 'None'
197 |             else:
198 |                 print cmod.__name__
199 |         def find_diff():
200 |             from astropy.utils import find_current_module
201 |             print find_current_module(0,True).__name__
202 | 
203 |     ``mod2.py``::
204 | 
205 |         def find():
206 |             from .mod1 import find2
207 |             find2()
208 | 
209 |     With these modules in place, the following occurs::
210 | 
211 |         >>> from pkg import mod1, mod2
212 |         >>> from astropy.utils import find_current_module
213 |         >>> mod1.find1()
214 |         pkg.mod1
215 |         >>> mod1.find2()
216 |         None
217 |         >>> mod2.find()
218 |         pkg.mod2
219 |         >>> find_current_module(0)
220 |         <module 'astropy.utils.misc' from 'astropy/utils/misc.py'>
221 |         >>> mod1.find_diff()
222 |         pkg.mod1
223 | 
224 |     """
225 | 
226 |     frm = inspect.currentframe()
227 |     for i in range(depth):
228 |         frm = frm.f_back
229 |         if frm is None:
230 |             return None
231 | 
232 |     if finddiff:
233 |         currmod = inspect.getmodule(frm)
234 |         if finddiff is True:
235 |             diffmods = [currmod]
236 |         else:
237 |             diffmods = []
238 |             for fd in finddiff:
239 |                 if inspect.ismodule(fd):
240 |                     diffmods.append(fd)
241 |                 elif isinstance(fd, str):
242 |                     diffmods.append(importlib.import_module(fd))
243 |                 elif fd is True:
244 |                     diffmods.append(currmod)
245 |                 else:
246 |                     raise ValueError('invalid entry in finddiff')
247 | 
248 |         while frm:
249 |             frmb = frm.f_back
250 |             modb = inspect.getmodule(frmb)
251 |             if modb not in diffmods:
252 |                 return modb
253 |             frm = frmb
254 |     else:
255 |         return inspect.getmodule(frm)
256 | 
257 | 
258 | def find_mod_objs(modname, onlylocals=False):
259 |     """ Returns all the public attributes of a module referenced by name.
260 | 
261 |     .. note::
262 |         The returned list *not* include subpackages or modules of
263 |         ``modname``, nor does it include private attributes (those that
264 |         begin with '_' or are not in `__all__`).
265 | 
266 |     Parameters
267 |     ----------
268 |     modname : str
269 |         The name of the module to search.
270 |     onlylocals : bool or list of str
271 |         If `True`, only attributes that are either members of ``modname`` OR
272 |         one of its modules or subpackages will be included. If it is a list
273 |         of strings, those specify the possible packages that will be
274 |         considered "local".
275 | 
276 |     Returns
277 |     -------
278 |     localnames : list of str
279 |         A list of the names of the attributes as they are named in the
280 |         module ``modname`` .
281 |     fqnames : list of str
282 |         A list of the full qualified names of the attributes (e.g.,
283 |         ``astropy.utils.introspection.find_mod_objs``). For attributes that are
284 |         simple variables, this is based on the local name, but for functions or
285 |         classes it can be different if they are actually defined elsewhere and
286 |         just referenced in ``modname``.
287 |     objs : list of objects
288 |         A list of the actual attributes themselves (in the same order as
289 |         the other arguments)
290 | 
291 |     """
292 | 
293 |     mod = resolve_name(modname)
294 | 
295 |     if hasattr(mod, '__all__'):
296 |         pkgitems = [(k, mod.__dict__[k]) for k in mod.__all__]
297 |     else:
298 |         pkgitems = [(k, mod.__dict__[k]) for k in dir(mod) if k[0] != '_']
299 | 
300 |     # filter out modules and pull the names and objs out
301 |     ismodule = inspect.ismodule
302 |     localnames = [k for k, v in pkgitems if not ismodule(v)]
303 |     objs = [v for k, v in pkgitems if not ismodule(v)]
304 | 
305 |     # fully qualified names can be determined from the object's module
306 |     fqnames = []
307 |     for obj, lnm in zip(objs, localnames):
308 |         if hasattr(obj, '__module__') and hasattr(obj, '__name__'):
309 |             fqnames.append(obj.__module__ + '.' + obj.__name__)
310 |         else:
311 |             fqnames.append(modname + '.' + lnm)
312 | 
313 |     if onlylocals:
314 |         if onlylocals is True:
315 |             onlylocals = [modname]
316 |         valids = [any(fqn.startswith(nm) for nm in onlylocals) for fqn in fqnames]
317 |         localnames = [e for i, e in enumerate(localnames) if valids[i]]
318 |         fqnames = [e for i, e in enumerate(fqnames) if valids[i]]
319 |         objs = [e for i, e in enumerate(objs) if valids[i]]
320 | 
321 |     return localnames, fqnames, objs
322 | 
323 | 
324 | # Note: I would have preferred call this is_instancemethod, but this naming is
325 | # for consistency with other functions in the `inspect` module
326 | def isinstancemethod(cls, obj):
327 |     """
328 |     Returns `True` if the given object is an instance method of the class
329 |     it is defined on (as opposed to a `staticmethod` or a `classmethod`).
330 | 
331 |     This requires both the class the object is a member of as well as the
332 |     object itself in order to make this determination.
333 | 
334 |     Parameters
335 |     ----------
336 |     cls : `type`
337 |         The class on which this method was defined.
338 |     obj : `object`
339 |         A member of the provided class (the membership is not checked directly,
340 |         but this function will always return `False` if the given object is not
341 |         a member of the given class).
342 | 
343 |     Examples
344 |     --------
345 |     >>> class MetaClass(type):
346 |     ...     def a_classmethod(cls): pass
347 |     ...
348 |     >>> class MyClass(metaclass=MetaClass):
349 |     ...     def an_instancemethod(self): pass
350 |     ...
351 |     ...     @classmethod
352 |     ...     def another_classmethod(cls): pass
353 |     ...
354 |     ...     @staticmethod
355 |     ...     def a_staticmethod(): pass
356 |     ...
357 |     >>> isinstancemethod(MyClass, MyClass.a_classmethod)
358 |     False
359 |     >>> isinstancemethod(MyClass, MyClass.another_classmethod)
360 |     False
361 |     >>> isinstancemethod(MyClass, MyClass.a_staticmethod)
362 |     False
363 |     >>> isinstancemethod(MyClass, MyClass.an_instancemethod)
364 |     True
365 |     """
366 | 
367 |     return _isinstancemethod(cls, obj)
368 | 
369 | 
370 | def _isinstancemethod(cls, obj):
371 |     if not isinstance(obj, types.FunctionType):
372 |         return False
373 | 
374 |     # Unfortunately it seems the easiest way to get to the original
375 |     # staticmethod object is to look in the class's __dict__, though we
376 |     # also need to look up the MRO in case the method is not in the given
377 |     # class's dict
378 |     name = obj.__name__
379 |     for basecls in cls.mro():  # This includes cls
380 |         if name in basecls.__dict__:
381 |             return not isinstance(basecls.__dict__[name], staticmethod)
382 | 
383 |     # This shouldn't happen, though this is the most sensible response if
384 |     # it does.
385 |     raise AttributeError(name)

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `astropy/astropy` repository.

## Issue

Header.fromstring does not accept Python 3 bytes
According to [the docs](http://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/_modules/astropy/io/fits/header.html#Header.fromstring), the method `Header.fromstring` "...creates an HDU header from a byte string containing the entire header data."

By "byte string" here it really means the `str` type which on Python 2 could be raw binary data, but on Python 3 explicitly is not.   In fact it does work on Python 3's unicode `str`s, but here it assumes that the data can be ASCII-encoded.

Its counterpart, `Header.fromfile` will work with files opened in text or binary mode.  So probably the simplest solution for now (as opposed to adding new methods or something like that) is to change `Header.fromstring` to accept unicode or bytes string types.

`Card.fromstring` likely needs a similar treatment.

  21 | # This regular expression can match a *valid* END card which just consists of
  22 | # the string 'END' followed by all spaces, or an *invalid* end card which
  23 | # consists of END, followed by any character that is *not* a valid character
  24 | # for a valid FITS keyword (that is, this is not a keyword like 'ENDER' which
  25 | # starts with 'END' but is not 'END'), followed by any arbitrary bytes.  An
  26 | # invalid end card may also consist of just 'END' with no trailing bytes.
  27 | HEADER_END_RE = re.compile(encode_ascii(
  28 |     r'(?:(?P<valid>END {77}) *)|(?P<invalid>END$|END {0,76}[^A-Z0-9_-])'))
  29 | 
  30 | 
  31 | # According to the FITS standard the only characters that may appear in a
  32 | # header record are the restricted ASCII chars from 0x20 through 0x7E.
  33 | VALID_HEADER_CHARS = set(map(chr, range(0x20, 0x7F)))
  34 | END_CARD = 'END' + ' ' * 77
  35 | 
  36 | 
  37 | __doctest_skip__ = ['Header', 'Header.*']
  38 | 
  39 | 
  40 | class Header:
  41 |     """
  42 |     FITS header class.  This class exposes both a dict-like interface and a
  43 |     list-like interface to FITS headers.
  44 | 
  45 |     The header may be indexed by keyword and, like a dict, the associated value
  46 |     will be returned.  When the header contains cards with duplicate keywords,
  47 |     only the value of the first card with the given keyword will be returned.
  48 |     It is also possible to use a 2-tuple as the index in the form (keyword,
  49 |     n)--this returns the n-th value with that keyword, in the case where there
  50 |     are duplicate keywords.
  51 | 
  52 |     For example::
  53 | 
  54 |         >>> header['NAXIS']
  55 |         0
  56 |         >>> header[('FOO', 1)]  # Return the value of the second FOO keyword
  57 |         'foo'
  58 | 
  59 |     The header may also be indexed by card number::
  60 | 
  61 |         >>> header[0]  # Return the value of the first card in the header
  62 |         'T'
  63 | 
  64 |     Commentary keywords such as HISTORY and COMMENT are special cases: When
  65 |     indexing the Header object with either 'HISTORY' or 'COMMENT' a list of all
  66 |     the HISTORY/COMMENT values is returned::
  67 | 
  68 |         >>> header['HISTORY']
  69 |         This is the first history entry in this header.
  70 |         This is the second history entry in this header.
  71 |         ...
  72 | 
  73 |     See the Astropy documentation for more details on working with headers.
  74 |     """
  75 | 
  76 |     def __init__(self, cards=[], copy=False):
  77 |         """
  78 |         Construct a `Header` from an iterable and/or text file.
  79 | 
  80 |         Parameters
  81 |         ----------
  82 |         cards : A list of `Card` objects, optional
  83 |             The cards to initialize the header with. Also allowed are other
  84 |             `Header` (or `dict`-like) objects.
  85 | 
  86 |             .. versionchanged:: 1.2
  87 |                 Allowed ``cards`` to be a `dict`-like object.
  88 | 
  89 |         copy : bool, optional
  90 | 
  91 |             If ``True`` copies the ``cards`` if they were another `Header`
  92 |             instance.
  93 |             Default is ``False``.
  94 | 
  95 |             .. versionadded:: 1.3
  96 |         """
  97 |         self.clear()
  98 | 
  99 |         if isinstance(cards, Header):
 100 |             if copy:
 101 |                 cards = cards.copy()
 102 |             cards = cards.cards
 103 |         elif isinstance(cards, dict):
 104 |             cards = cards.items()
 105 | 
 106 |         for card in cards:
 107 |             self.append(card, end=True)
 108 | 
 109 |         self._modified = False
 110 | 
 111 |     def __len__(self):
 112 |         return len(self._cards)
 113 | 
 114 |     def __iter__(self):
 115 |         for card in self._cards:
 116 |             yield card.keyword
 117 | 
 118 |     def __contains__(self, keyword):
 119 |         if keyword in self._keyword_indices or keyword in self._rvkc_indices:
 120 |             # For the most common case (single, standard form keyword lookup)
 121 |             # this will work and is an O(1) check.  If it fails that doesn't
 122 |             # guarantee absence, just that we have to perform the full set of
 123 |             # checks in self._cardindex
 124 |             return True
 125 |         try:
 126 |             self._cardindex(keyword)
 127 |         except (KeyError, IndexError):
 128 |             return False
 129 |         return True
 130 | 
 131 |     def __getitem__(self, key):
 132 |         if isinstance(key, slice):
 133 |             return Header([copy.copy(c) for c in self._cards[key]])
 134 |         elif self._haswildcard(key):
 135 |             return Header([copy.copy(self._cards[idx])
 136 |                            for idx in self._wildcardmatch(key)])
 137 |         elif (isinstance(key, str) and
 138 |               key.upper() in Card._commentary_keywords):
 139 |             key = key.upper()
 140 |             # Special case for commentary cards
 141 |             return _HeaderCommentaryCards(self, key)
 142 |         if isinstance(key, tuple):
 143 |             keyword = key[0]
 144 |         else:
 145 |             keyword = key
 146 |         card = self._cards[self._cardindex(key)]
 147 |         if card.field_specifier is not None and keyword == card.rawkeyword:
 148 |             # This is RVKC; if only the top-level keyword was specified return
 149 |             # the raw value, not the parsed out float value
 150 |             return card.rawvalue
 151 | 
 152 |         value = card.value
 153 |         if value == UNDEFINED:
 154 |             return None
 155 |         return value
 156 | 
 157 |     def __setitem__(self, key, value):
 158 |         if self._set_slice(key, value, self):
 159 |             return
 160 | 
 161 |         if isinstance(value, tuple):
 162 |             if not (0 < len(value) <= 2):
 163 |                 raise ValueError(
 164 |                     'A Header item may be set with either a scalar value, '
 165 |                     'a 1-tuple containing a scalar value, or a 2-tuple '
 166 |                     'containing a scalar value and comment string.')
 167 |             if len(value) == 1:
 168 |                 value, comment = value[0], None
 169 |                 if value is None:
 170 |                     value = UNDEFINED
 171 |             elif len(value) == 2:
 172 |                 value, comment = value
 173 |                 if value is None:
 174 |                     value = UNDEFINED
 175 |                 if comment is None:
 176 |                     comment = ''
 177 |         else:
 178 |             comment = None
 179 | 
 180 |         card = None
 181 |         if isinstance(key, int):
 182 |             card = self._cards[key]
 183 |         elif isinstance(key, tuple):
 184 |             card = self._cards[self._cardindex(key)]
 185 |         if value is None:
 186 |             value = UNDEFINED
 187 |         if card:
 188 |             card.value = value
 189 |             if comment is not None:
 190 |                 card.comment = comment
 191 |             if card._modified:
 192 |                 self._modified = True
 193 |         else:
 194 |             # If we get an IndexError that should be raised; we don't allow
 195 |             # assignment to non-existing indices
 196 |             self._update((key, value, comment))
 197 | 
 198 |     def __delitem__(self, key):
 199 |         if isinstance(key, slice) or self._haswildcard(key):
 200 |             # This is very inefficient but it's not a commonly used feature.
 201 |             # If someone out there complains that they make heavy use of slice
 202 |             # deletions and it's too slow, well, we can worry about it then
 203 |             # [the solution is not too complicated--it would be wait 'til all
 204 |             # the cards are deleted before updating _keyword_indices rather
 205 |             # than updating it once for each card that gets deleted]
 206 |             if isinstance(key, slice):
 207 |                 indices = range(*key.indices(len(self)))
 208 |                 # If the slice step is backwards we want to reverse it, because
 209 |                 # it will be reversed in a few lines...
 210 |                 if key.step and key.step < 0:
 211 |                     indices = reversed(indices)
 212 |             else:
 213 |                 indices = self._wildcardmatch(key)
 214 |             for idx in reversed(indices):
 215 |                 del self[idx]
 216 |             return
 217 |         elif isinstance(key, str):
 218 |             # delete ALL cards with the same keyword name
 219 |             key = Card.normalize_keyword(key)
 220 |             indices = self._keyword_indices
 221 |             if key not in self._keyword_indices:
 222 |                 indices = self._rvkc_indices
 223 | 
 224 |             if key not in indices:
 225 |                 # if keyword is not present raise KeyError.
 226 |                 # To delete keyword without caring if they were present,
 227 |                 # Header.remove(Keyword) can be used with optional argument ignore_missing as True
 228 |                 raise KeyError("Keyword '{}' not found.".format(key))
 229 | 
 230 |             for idx in reversed(indices[key]):
 231 |                 # Have to copy the indices list since it will be modified below
 232 |                 del self[idx]
 233 |             return
 234 | 
 235 |         idx = self._cardindex(key)
 236 |         card = self._cards[idx]
 237 |         keyword = card.keyword
 238 |         del self._cards[idx]
 239 |         keyword = Card.normalize_keyword(keyword)
 240 |         indices = self._keyword_indices[keyword]
 241 |         indices.remove(idx)
 242 |         if not indices:
 243 |             del self._keyword_indices[keyword]
 244 | 
 245 |         # Also update RVKC indices if necessary :/
 246 |         if card.field_specifier is not None:
 247 |             indices = self._rvkc_indices[card.rawkeyword]
 248 |             indices.remove(idx)
 249 |             if not indices:
 250 |                 del self._rvkc_indices[card.rawkeyword]
 251 | 
 252 |         # We also need to update all other indices
 253 |         self._updateindices(idx, increment=False)
 254 |         self._modified = True
 255 | 
 256 |     def __repr__(self):
 257 |         return self.tostring(sep='\n', endcard=False, padding=False)
 258 | 
 259 |     def __str__(self):
 260 |         return self.tostring()
 261 | 
 262 |     def __eq__(self, other):
 263 |         """
 264 |         Two Headers are equal only if they have the exact same string
 265 |         representation.
 266 |         """
 267 | 
 268 |         return str(self) == str(other)
 269 | 
 270 |     def __add__(self, other):
 271 |         temp = self.copy(strip=False)

 275 |     def __iadd__(self, other):

 309 |     @property
 310 |     def _modified(self):

 312 |         Whether or not the header has been modified; this is a property so that
 313 |         it can also check each card for modifications--cards may have been
 314 |         modified directly without the header containing it otherwise knowing.

 317 |         modified_cards = any(c._modified for c in self._cards)
 318 |         if modified_cards:
 319 |             # If any cards were modified then by definition the header was
 320 |             # modified
 321 |             self.__dict__['_modified'] = True

 323 |         return self.__dict__['_modified']
 324 | 
 325 |     @_modified.setter
