[26/500] django__django-10914

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Set default FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSION to 0o644.
Description
	
Hello,
As far as I can see, the ​File Uploads documentation page does not mention any permission issues.
What I would like to see is a warning that in absence of explicitly configured FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS, the permissions for a file uploaded to FileSystemStorage might not be consistent depending on whether a MemoryUploadedFile or a TemporaryUploadedFile was used for temporary storage of the uploaded data (which, with the default FILE_UPLOAD_HANDLERS, in turn depends on the uploaded data size).
The tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile + os.rename sequence causes the resulting file permissions to be 0o0600 on some systems (I experience it here on CentOS 7.4.1708 and Python 3.6.5). In all probability, the implementation of Python's built-in tempfile module explicitly sets such permissions for temporary files due to security considerations.
I found mentions of this issue ​on GitHub, but did not manage to find any existing bug report in Django's bug tracker.



Hints:
I think you're talking about ef70af77ec53160d5ffa060c1bdf5ed93322d84f (#28540). I guess the question is whether or not that documentation should be duplicated elsewhere.
Thank you Tim, this is precisely what I was looking for! I can only see one issue with the current docs (if you excuse me for bothering you with such minor details). ​The documentation for the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS setting reads: If this isn’t given or is None, you’ll get operating-system dependent behavior. On most platforms, temporary files will have a mode of 0o600, and files saved from memory will be saved using the system’s standard umask. As I would understand this text, only temporary files get a mode of 0o600. I would then ask myself: "Why should I care about temporary files, they should be gone anyway after the file is uploaded?" and skip setting FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS. What is important but is not properly conveyed to the user is that not only temporary files themselves, but also the actual files which end up in the media folder get permissions of 0o600. Currently a developer can only discover this either by careful reading of the Deployment checklist page (manage.py check --deploy does not seem to check FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS) or by hitting the inconsistent permissions accidentally (like I did). I propose to unify the docs for FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS on the Settings page and the Deployment checklist page like this: ​https://gist.github.com/earshinov/0340f741189a14d4fd10e3e902203ad6/revisions#diff-14151589d5408f8b64b7e0e580770f0e Pros: It makes more clear that one gets different permissions for the *uploaded* files. It makes the docs more unified and thus easier to synchronously change in the future if/when required. I recognize that my edits might seem too minor and insignificant to be worth the hassle of editing the docs, committing, re-publishing them etc., but still I hope you will find them useful enough to be integrated into the official docs.
Now that I think about, maybe Django could provide # <Commentary about inconsistent permissions when this setting is omitted> FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSINS=0o600 in the ​default project settings so that developers don't miss it? 600 seems a reasonable default, particularly because people would get 600 anyway (at least on some operating systems) when the TemporaryFileUploadHandler is engaged.
Since this has come up again, I've suggested on django-developers (​https://groups.google.com/d/topic/django-developers/h9XbQAPv5-I/discussion) that we adjust the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSION default to 0o644 (This was the conclusion I eventually came to from the discussion on #28540.) Lets see what people say there.
Thus far, no great objections on the mailing list to adjusting the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSION default. Thus I'm going to rename this and Accept on that basis. A PR would need to: Adjust the default. Add a Breaking Change note to releases/2.2.txt (on the assumption we can get it in for then.) — This should include a set to None to restore previous behaviour' type comment. Adjust the references in the settings docs and deployment checklist. Make sure any other references are adjusted.
Replying to Carlton Gibson: Thus far, no great objections on the mailing list to adjusting the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSION default. Thus I'm going to rename this and Accept on that basis. Thank you! Hopefully, this change will prevent confusion and unpleasant surprises for Django users in the future.
Hello everyone, I would like to work on this. But before that there are few important questions: There is a related setting called FILE_UPLOAD_DIRECTORY_PERMISSIONS. Its document says that This value mirrors the functionality and caveats of the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS setting. Shall we also change its default from None to 0o644(Please suggest if something different should be provided for directories) and update its document as well? Since 2.2 pre-release branch is now in feature freeze state, Shall we move the change to 3.0 version? On a side note, some tests must be refactored for new values for both of these settings. I think that's alright.
That note is referring to that non-leaf directories are created using the process umask. (See ​`makedirs()` docs.) This is similar to FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS, when not using the temporary file upload handler. The underlying issue here is the inconsistency in file permissions, depending on the file size, when using the default settings that Django provides. There is no such inconsistency with directory permissions. As such changes should not be needed to FILE_UPLOAD_DIRECTORY_PERMISSIONS. (Any issues there would need to be addressed under a separate ticket.)
Replying to Carlton Gibson: I see and understand the issue better now. Thanks for the clarification. I'll make the changes as you have suggested in your previous comment. Only question remaining is about introducing this change in 3.0 version. Shall we move it to 3.0 release?
Shall we move it to 3.0 release? Yes please.

[27/500] django__django-10973

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Use subprocess.run and PGPASSWORD for client in postgres backend
Description
	
​subprocess.run was added in python 3.5 (which is the minimum version since Django 2.1). This function allows you to pass a custom environment for the subprocess.
Using this in django.db.backends.postgres.client to set PGPASSWORD simplifies the code and makes it more reliable.

🔧 Finish: Final answer: Successfully fixed the postgres backend client to use `subprocess.run` with `PGPASSWORD` environment variable.

**Changes made to `django/db/backends/postgresql/client.py`:**

1. **Removed the `_escape_pgpass` helper function** - No longer needed since we're using `PGPASSWORD` directly instead of a `.pgpass` file.

2. **Removed the `NamedTemporaryFile` import** - No longer creating temporary `.pgpass` files.

3. **Simplified the `runshell_db` method**:
   - Instead of creating a temporary `.pgpass` file and setting `PGPASSFILE` environment variable, the code now:
     - Creates a copy of the current environment with `os.environ.copy()`
     - Sets `PGPASSWORD` directly in the environment if a password is provided
     - Uses `subprocess.run(args, env=env, check=True)` to pass the custom environment to the subprocess

This fix simplifies the code and makes it more reliable by avoiding the complexity of managing temporary files and the `PGPASSFILE` environment variable. The `subprocess.run` function (available since Python 3.5) allows passing a custom environment directly, which is cleaner and more straightforward.
🎉 Final answer: Successfully fixed the postgres backend client to use `subprocess.run` with `PGPASSWORD` environment variable.

**Changes made to `django/db/backends/postgresql/client.py`:**

1. **Removed the `_escape_pgpass` helper function** - No longer needed since we're using `PGPASSWORD` directly instead of a `.pgpass` file.

2. **Removed the `NamedTemporaryFile` import** - No longer creating temporary `.pgpass` files.

3. **Simplified the `runshell_db` method**:
   - Instead of creating a temporary `.pgpass` file and setting `PGPASSFILE` environment variable, the code now:
     - Creates a copy of the current environment with `os.environ.copy()`
     - Sets `PGPASSWORD` directly in the environment if a password is provided
     - Uses `subprocess.run(args, env=env, check=True)` to pass the custom environment to the subprocess

This fix simplifies the code and makes it more reliable by avoiding the complexity of managing temporary files and the `PGPASSFILE` environment variable. The `subprocess.run` function (available since Python 3.5) allows passing a custom environment directly, which is cleaner and more straightforward.

[28/500] django__django-10999

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Fix parse_duration() for some negative durations
Description
	
The ​https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.1/_modules/django/utils/dateparse/ defines:
standard_duration_re = re.compile(
	r'^'
	r'(?:(?P<days>-?\d+) (days?, )?)?'
	r'((?:(?P<hours>-?\d+):)(?=\d+:\d+))?'
	r'(?:(?P<minutes>-?\d+):)?'
	r'(?P<seconds>-?\d+)'
	r'(?:\.(?P<microseconds>\d{1,6})\d{0,6})?'
	r'$'
)
that doesn't match to negative durations, because of the <hours> definition final (lookahead) part does not have '-?' in it. The following will work:
	r'((?:(?P<hours>-?\d+):)(?=-?\d+:-?\d+))?'
(Thanks to Konstantin Senichev for finding the fix.)



Hints:
Please give an example valid that's not working. There are ​some tests for negative values.
Right, this should have been fixed by #27699 which is included in 1.11.x.
Example cases, can be discussed: parse_duration('-00:01:01') => plus 61 seconds, so it is not -(00:01:01) but (-00):(+01):(+01) parse_duration('00:-01:-01) => None , leading zeros will prevent parsing parse_duration('-01:01') => minus 59 seconds parse_duration('-01:-01') => minus 61 seconds The fix presented would allow the second line to be parsed (which would help with generated durations). And some instructions in the function/documentation/wiki would be useful, to clarify how the minus sign affects in duration.
The fix from #27699 may not be entirely correct. I agree with your first and third examples. I'd expect a leading minus sign to negate the entire value so they would be minus 61 seconds. I think the second and fourth examples are invalid. I don't think a minus sign after a colon is valid.
Thanks for the extra details. I agree with Tim that everything but a leading - seems like an invalid value that happened to work because of an inappropriate pattern as ​it was never tested.

  3 | # We're using regular expressions rather than time.strptime because:
  4 | # - They provide both validation and parsing.
  5 | # - They're more flexible for datetimes.
  6 | # - The date/datetime/time constructors produce friendlier error messages.

 15 | )

 22 | datetime_re = re.compile(
 23 |     r'(?P<year>\d{4})-(?P<month>\d{1,2})-(?P<day>\d{1,2})'
 24 |     r'[T ](?P<hour>\d{1,2}):(?P<minute>\d{1,2})'
 25 |     r'(?::(?P<second>\d{1,2})(?:\.(?P<microsecond>\d{1,6})\d{0,6})?)?'

 29 | standard_duration_re = re.compile(
 30 |     r'^'

 39 | # Support the sections of ISO 8601 date representation that are accepted by
 40 | # timedelta
 41 | iso8601_duration_re = re.compile(
 42 |     r'^(?P<sign>[-+]?)'
 43 |     r'P'
 44 |     r'(?:(?P<days>\d+(.\d+)?)D)?'

 53 | # Support PostgreSQL's day-time interval format, e.g. "3 days 04:05:06". The
 54 | # year-month and mixed intervals cannot be converted to a timedelta and thus
 55 | # aren't accepted.
 56 | postgres_interval_re = re.compile(
 57 |     r'^'
 58 |     r'(?:(?P<days>-?\d+) (days? ?))?'

 67 | 
 68 | def parse_date(value):
 69 |     """Parse a string and return a datetime.date.
 70 | 
 71 |     Raise ValueError if the input is well formatted but not a valid date.
 72 |     Return None if the input isn't well formatted.
 73 |     """
 74 |     match = date_re.match(value)
 75 |     if match:
 76 |         kw = {k: int(v) for k, v in match.groupdict().items()}
 77 |         return datetime.date(**kw)
 78 | 
 79 | 
 80 | def parse_time(value):
 81 |     """Parse a string and return a datetime.time.
 82 | 
 83 |     This function doesn't support time zone offsets.
 84 | 
 85 |     Raise ValueError if the input is well formatted but not a valid time.
 86 |     Return None if the input isn't well formatted, in particular if it
 87 |     contains an offset.
 88 |     """
 89 |     match = time_re.match(value)
 90 |     if match:
 91 |         kw = match.groupdict()
 92 |         kw['microsecond'] = kw['microsecond'] and kw['microsecond'].ljust(6, '0')
 93 |         kw = {k: int(v) for k, v in kw.items() if v is not None}
 94 |         return datetime.time(**kw)
 95 | 
 96 | 
 97 | def parse_datetime(value):
 98 |     """Parse a string and return a datetime.datetime.
 99 | 
100 |     This function supports time zone offsets. When the input contains one,
101 |     the output uses a timezone with a fixed offset from UTC.
102 | 
103 |     Raise ValueError if the input is well formatted but not a valid datetime.
104 |     Return None if the input isn't well formatted.
105 |     """
106 |     match = datetime_re.match(value)
107 |     if match:
108 |         kw = match.groupdict()
109 |         kw['microsecond'] = kw['microsecond'] and kw['microsecond'].ljust(6, '0')
110 |         tzinfo = kw.pop('tzinfo')
111 |         if tzinfo == 'Z':
112 |             tzinfo = utc
113 |         elif tzinfo is not None:
114 |             offset_mins = int(tzinfo[-2:]) if len(tzinfo) > 3 else 0
115 |             offset = 60 * int(tzinfo[1:3]) + offset_mins
116 |             if tzinfo[0] == '-':
117 |                 offset = -offset
118 |             tzinfo = get_fixed_timezone(offset)
119 |         kw = {k: int(v) for k, v in kw.items() if v is not None}
120 |         kw['tzinfo'] = tzinfo
121 |         return datetime.datetime(**kw)
122 | 
123 | 
124 | def parse_duration(value):
125 |     """Parse a duration string and return a datetime.timedelta.
126 | 
127 |     The preferred format for durations in Django is '%d %H:%M:%S.%f'.
128 | 
129 |     Also supports ISO 8601 representation and PostgreSQL's day-time interval
130 |     format.
131 |     """
132 |     match = (
133 |         standard_duration_re.match(value) or
134 |         iso8601_duration_re.match(value) or
135 |         postgres_interval_re.match(value)
136 |     )
137 |     if match:
138 |         kw = match.groupdict()
139 |         days = datetime.timedelta(float(kw.pop('days', 0) or 0))
140 |         sign = -1 if kw.pop('sign', '+') == '-' else 1
141 |         if kw.get('microseconds'):
142 |             kw['microseconds'] = kw['microseconds'].ljust(6, '0')
143 |         if kw.get('seconds') and kw.get('microseconds') and kw['seconds'].startswith('-'):
144 |             kw['microseconds'] = '-' + kw['microseconds']
145 |         kw = {k: float(v) for k, v in kw.items() if v is not None}
146 |         return days + sign * datetime.timedelta(**kw)

--- Step 4 ---

[29/500] django__django-11066

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

RenameContentType._rename() doesn't save the content type on the correct database
Description
	
The commit in question:
​https://github.com/django/django/commit/f179113e6cbc8ba0a8d4e87e1d4410fb61d63e75
The specific lines in question:
​https://github.com/django/django/blob/586a9dc4295357de1f5ad0590ad34bf2bc008f79/django/contrib/contenttypes/management/__init__.py#L27
with transaction.atomic(using=db): 
	content_type.save(update_fields={'model'})
The issue:
For some background, we run a dynamic database router and have no "real" databases configured in the settings file, just a default sqlite3 backend which is never actually generated or used. We forked the migrate.py management command and modified it to accept a dictionary containing database connection parameters as the --database argument. 
The dynamic database router is based on, and very similar to this: ​https://github.com/ambitioninc/django-dynamic-db-router/blob/master/dynamic_db_router/router.py
This has worked beautifully for all migrations up until this point.
The issue we're running into is that when attempting to run a migration which contains a call to migrations.RenameModel, and while specifying the database parameters to the migrate command, the migration fails with an OperationalError, stating that no such table: django_content_types exists.
After having exhaustively stepped through the traceback, it appears that even though the content_type.save call is wrapped in the with transaction.atomic(using=db) context manager, the actual database operation is being attempted on the default database (which in our case does not exist) rather than the database specified via schema_editor.connection.alias (on line 15 of the same file) and thus fails loudly.
So, I believe that:
content_type.save(update_fields={'model'})
should be
content_type.save(using=db, update_fields={'model'})



Hints:
Added a pull request with the fix. ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/10332
Hi, I spent all afternoon into this ticket. As I am a newbie I failed to make a test for it. And the fix can really be 'using=db' in the .save() method. But I found a StackOverflow answer about transaction.atomic(using=db) that is interesting. It does not work (Django 1.10.5). ​multi-db-transactions The reason given is that the router is responsible for directions. Tyler Morgan probably read the docs but I am putting it here in case for a steps review. It has an ​example purpose only
Hebert, the solution proposed by Tyler on the PR looks appropriate to me. The using=db kwarg has to be passed to save() for the same reason explained in the SO page you linked to; transaction.atomic(using) doesn't route any query and save(using) skips query routing.
Hi, First, I shoud went to mentors instead of comment here. I did not make the test. Back to the ticket: " a default sqlite3 backend which is never actually generated or used. ". If it is not generated I thought: How get info from there? Then I did a search. There is no '.save(using)' in the StackOverflow I linked. If no 'using' is set it fails. Then the guy tried two 'transaction.atomic(using='db1')' - and 'db2' togheter. It worked. First, the decorator (worked). Second, the 'with' (did nothing). @transaction.atomic(using='db1') def edit(self, context): """Edit :param dict context: Context :return: None """ # Check if employee exists try: result = Passport.objects.get(pk=self.user.employee_id) except Passport.DoesNotExist: return False result.name = context.get('name') with transaction.atomic(using='db2'): result.save() "In the above example I even made another transaction inside of the initial transaction but this time using='db2' where the model doesn't even exist. I figured that would have failed, but it didn't and the data was written to the proper database." It is ok to '.save(using=db)'. I used the 'can', probably not the right term. Next time I go to mentors. To not make confusion in the ticket. Regards, Herbert

  7 |     def __init__(self, app_label, old_model, new_model):
  8 |         self.app_label = app_label
  9 |         self.old_model = old_model
 10 |         self.new_model = new_model
 11 |         super().__init__(self.rename_forward, self.rename_backward)
 12 | 
 13 |     def _rename(self, apps, schema_editor, old_model, new_model):
 14 |         ContentType = apps.get_model('contenttypes', 'ContentType')
 15 |         db = schema_editor.connection.alias
 16 |         if not router.allow_migrate_model(db, ContentType):
 17 |             return
 18 | 
 19 |         try:
 20 |             content_type = ContentType.objects.db_manager(db).get_by_natural_key(self.app_label, old_model)
 21 |         except ContentType.DoesNotExist:
 22 |             pass
 23 |         else:
 24 |             content_type.model = new_model
 25 |             try:
 26 |                 with transaction.atomic(using=db):
 27 |                     content_type.save(update_fields={'model'})
 28 |             except IntegrityError:
 29 |                 # Gracefully fallback if a stale content type causes a
 30 |                 # conflict as remove_stale_contenttypes will take care of
 31 |                 # asking the user what should be done next.
 32 |                 content_type.model = old_model
 33 |             else:
 34 |                 # Clear the cache as the `get_by_natual_key()` call will cache
 35 |                 # the renamed ContentType instance by its old model name.
 36 |                 ContentType.objects.clear_cache()
 37 | 
 38 |     def rename_forward(self, apps, schema_editor):
 39 |         self._rename(apps, schema_editor, self.old_model, self.new_model)
 40 | 
 41 |     def rename_backward(self, apps, schema_editor):
 42 |         self._rename(apps, schema_editor, self.new_model, self.old_model)

 45 | def inject_rename_contenttypes_operations(plan=None, apps=global_apps, using=DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS, **kwargs):
 46 |     """
 47 |     Insert a `RenameContentType` operation after every planned `RenameModel`
 48 |     operation.
 49 |     """
 50 |     if plan is None:
 51 |         return
 52 | 
 53 |     # Determine whether or not the ContentType model is available.
 54 |     try:
 55 |         ContentType = apps.get_model('contenttypes', 'ContentType')
 56 |     except LookupError:
 57 |         available = False
 58 |     else:
 59 |         if not router.allow_migrate_model(using, ContentType):
 60 |             return
 61 |         available = True
 62 | 
 63 |     for migration, backward in plan:
 64 |         if (migration.app_label, migration.name) == ('contenttypes', '0001_initial'):
 65 |             # There's no point in going forward if the initial contenttypes
 66 |             # migration is unapplied as the ContentType model will be
 67 |             # unavailable from this point.
 68 |             if backward:
 69 |                 break
 70 |             else:
 71 |                 available = True
 72 |                 continue
 73 |         # The ContentType model is not available yet.
 74 |         if not available:
 75 |             continue
 76 |         inserts = []
 77 |         for index, operation in enumerate(migration.operations):
 78 |             if isinstance(operation, migrations.RenameModel):
 79 |                 operation = RenameContentType(
 80 |                     migration.app_label, operation.old_name_lower, operation.new_name_lower
 81 |                 )
 82 |                 inserts.append((index + 1, operation))
 83 |         for inserted, (index, operation) in enumerate(inserts):
 84 |             migration.operations.insert(inserted + index, operation)
 85 |

 87 | def get_contenttypes_and_models(app_config, using, ContentType):
 88 |     if not router.allow_migrate_model(using, ContentType):
 89 |         return None, None
 90 | 
 91 |     ContentType.objects.clear_cache()
 92 | 
 93 |     content_types = {
 94 |         ct.model: ct
 95 |         for ct in ContentType.objects.using(using).filter(app_label=app_config.label)
 96 |     }
 97 |     app_models = {
 98 |         model._meta.model_name: model
 99 |         for model in app_config.get_models()

129 |         if model_name not in content_types

SELECT text_log_step.id, text_log_step.job_id, text_log_step.name, text_log_step.started, text_log_step.finished, text_log_step.started_line_number, text_log_step.finished_line_number, text_log_step.result FROM text_log_step WHERE text_log_step.job_id IN (206573433); args=(206573433,)
SELECT text_log_error.id, text_log_error.step_id, text_log_error.line, text_log_error.line_number FROM text_log_error WHERE text_log_error.step_id IN (544935727); args=(544935727,)
Querying the text_log_error table for those ids shows there to be junk values in its line field. These are from data inserted when using Python 2.7, which presumably wasn't validating the unicode escape sequences being used. 
There appear to be two issues here:
mysqlclient-python's behaviour differs depending on Python version - under Python 3 it defaults use_unicode to True, which means it attempts to decode the line field but fails (since it doesn't use 'replace' or 'ignore'). This seems like something that the Django ORM should try to protect against (eg by setting use_unicode to the same value on all Python versions and handling the unicode conversion itself), given it generally handles any implementation differences in layers lower than the ORM. 
the UnicodeDecodeError is occurring for a field (text_log_error.line) that is not actually needed for the .delete() (it's not a primary key etc), so Django shouldn't be fetching that field regardless when making the text_log_error SELECT query
(Plus ideally Django would support cascade deletes, so we wouldn't need to use the current .delete() approach; ticket 21961)
Fixing issue (2) would presumably also improve .delete() performance.
Related:
​https://github.com/PyMySQL/mysqlclient-python/issues/258

I think this would have been better posted elsewhere. See TicketClosingReasons/UseSupportChannels. However... DELETE FROM "auth_group" WHERE "auth_group"."id" IN (5, 4); args=(5, 4) [utils.py:111] Oops, all fields selected. Hi! Thank you for the reply and examples. However it seems like this should be the default behaviour for .delete()? If so, can we use this ticket to track that? Also in the issue we were seeing, the additional fields being selected were from a model different to the one on which the .delete() was being called, so I don't think the .only() would help? (Or at least the docs don't show a way to apply .only() to a nested relation; ​https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/querysets/#django.db.models.query.QuerySet.only ?)
Replying to Ed Morley: However it seems like this should be the default behaviour for .delete()? If so, can we use this ticket to track that? ie that's what I meant by: the UnicodeDecodeError is occurring for a field (text_log_error.line) that is not actually needed for the .delete() (it's not a primary key etc), so Django shouldn't be fetching that field regardless when making the text_log_error SELECT query ...and why I don't see that this is a support request? :-)
Right, so there’s two possible tickets hidden in what really does look like a support request: Can’t get only() to behave right. Alter delete() API. So the second one, I’d guess is “No”, because there’s already one right way to do it™ (use only()). You’d have to go via the mailing list to discuss a change there. I think you’d need to narrow it down significantly to get traction. The first one still looks like a usage question. I had a quick look at your command. It didn’t look like you couldn’t adjust the code somehow to make it work. If you can reduce it to a minimal example showing Django does have a limitation there then of course we can look at that.
So the second one, I’d guess is “No”, because there’s already one right way to do it™ (use only()). You’d have to go via the mailing list to discuss a change there. I think you’d need to narrow it down significantly to get traction. To use .only() we'd have to hardcode the list of each fields for each model that might be required by the .delete(), whereas Django could generate that itself, if it supported a .delete(fetch_all_fields=False). I get why Django fetches all fields for other types of operations, but for .delete()s it really doesn't make sense. I worked around this for now using: try: self.filter(guid__in=jobs_chunk).delete() except UnicodeDecodeError: # Work around Django's .delete() selecting all the fields above even though it doesn't need to # which results in a UnicodeDecodeError when trying to decode junk values in some `TextLogError.line`s TextLogError.objects.filter(step__job__guid__in=jobs_chunk).only('id').delete() ...but it will fail again if any of the other models in the web of relations has similar junk data.
You know about defer() too right? So you can just drop the junk field, leaving the rest intact if you don’t know the fields you need.
Yeah true it would be cleaner to rewrite the above using .defer() on the initial .delete() for this specific case. However back to the generic "adjust .delete() so it only fetches the fields it needs for improved performance" - it just feels wrong to have to tell delete to not fetch everything when it is traversing the model relations only for the sake of figuring out nested relations to delete. (My frustration over this is in part due to bulk deletions having been a massive pain point for us for years now. In an ideal world we'd be able to skip all signals/in-Python handling so we can expire data at scale without the Django overhead, but that's https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/21961)
(Yeah, I can understand that. Have a hug… 🤗. Sometimes I just use SQL, but don’t tell anyone. 🙂) As I say, considered proposals for API changes via the mailing list are welcome. I don’t think I’d be keen on .delete(fetch_all_fields=False) but that’s not to say there’s no room for improvements.
Thank you for your help :-)
Do you happen to have any deletion signals receivers connected for the FailureLine model? That would prevent Django from fast-deleting these objects and force it to do a SELECT of related objects to fire the signals. You can determine if it's the case of not with the following code from django.db.models import signals print(signals.pre_delete.has_listeners(FailureLine)) print(signals.post_delete.has_listeners(FailureLine)) print(signals.m2m_changed.has_listeners(FailureLine)) If it's the case then I'm afraid Django cannot do much here automatically as it cannot introspect signal receivers code to determine which fields should be deferred and which shouldn't.
Hi :-) Those came back false for all of FailureLine, Job, JobLog, TextLogStep and TextLogError. (Prior to filling this ticket I'd tried to find out more about why this wasn't fast-deleting. The docs are light on details, but I found can_fast_delete() - and I presume it's the second half of that we're hitting? Though I don't really follow what it's checking for)
Hey Ed, thanks for extra details. So, the reason why the SELECT happens even if no signals are registered is that the deletion collector builds the deletion graph from the origin by using simple SELECT to abort the process when no objects are returned instead of building optimistic DELETE queries with complex predicates (e.g. JOINS or subqueries). Now, you are completely right that only referenced fields have to be fetched in the SELECT queries and it looks like the optimization can be implemented without too much work. ​https://github.com/django/django/compare/master...charettes:ticket-30191 The above branch passes the full test suite against SQLite and PostgreSQL. If you're interested in getting this work merged in 3.0 it should only be a matter of adding extra tests to assert that. Non-referenced fields are always deferred. Non-primary key references (aka ForeignKey(to_field)) works appropriately. The optimization is disabled when receivers are connected for deletion signals. And submit a PR to get a review. If you're interested I think this ticket can be re-opened as Cleanup/Optimization and moved to Accepted.
Interestingly a similar optimization was proposed in an old ticket suggesting the addition of a bulk_delete method https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/9519#comment:21 (point 3).
Hi Simon, thanks for the work there! Accepting on that basis.
​https://github.com/django/django/pull/11087

--- Step 1 ---
🔧 Thought: Reasoning: The issue is about optimizing Django's `.delete()` method to only fetch required fields (primary keys and foreign key references) instead of all fields. This prevents UnicodeDecodeError when there's garbage data in non-essential fields and improves performance.

Key points from the issue:
1. When calling `.delete()` on a queryset, Django fetches ALL fields from related models during cascade deletes
2. Only primary keys and foreign key references are actually needed for deletion
3. The fix should defer non-referenced fields automatically during delete operations
4. The optimization should be disabled when deletion signals are connected

Let me start by exploring the codebase to find the relevant files - particularly the deletion.py and query.py files mentioned in the traceback.

  8 | class ProtectedError(IntegrityError):
  9 |     def __init__(self, msg, protected_objects):
 10 |         self.protected_objects = protected_objects
 11 |         super().__init__(msg, protected_objects)

 14 | def CASCADE(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 15 |     collector.collect(sub_objs, source=field.remote_field.model,
 16 |                       source_attr=field.name, nullable=field.null)
 17 |     if field.null and not connections[using].features.can_defer_constraint_checks:
 18 |         collector.add_field_update(field, None, sub_objs)
 19 |

 21 | def PROTECT(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 22 |     raise ProtectedError(
 23 |         "Cannot delete some instances of model '%s' because they are "
 24 |         "referenced through a protected foreign key: '%s.%s'" % (
 25 |             field.remote_field.model.__name__, sub_objs[0].__class__.__name__, field.name
 26 |         ),
 27 |         sub_objs
 28 |     )

 31 | def SET(value):
 32 |     if callable(value):
 33 |         def set_on_delete(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 34 |             collector.add_field_update(field, value(), sub_objs)
 35 |     else:
 36 |         def set_on_delete(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 37 |             collector.add_field_update(field, value, sub_objs)
 38 |     set_on_delete.deconstruct = lambda: ('django.db.models.SET', (value,), {})
 39 |     return set_on_delete
 40 | 
 41 | 
 42 | def SET_NULL(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 43 |     collector.add_field_update(field, None, sub_objs)
 44 | 
 45 | 
 46 | def SET_DEFAULT(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 47 |     collector.add_field_update(field, field.get_default(), sub_objs)
 48 | 
 49 | 
 50 | def DO_NOTHING(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 51 |     pass
 52 | 
 53 | 
 54 | def get_candidate_relations_to_delete(opts):
 55 |     # The candidate relations are the ones that come from N-1 and 1-1 relations.
 56 |     # N-N  (i.e., many-to-many) relations aren't candidates for deletion.
 57 |     return (
 58 |         f for f in opts.get_fields(include_hidden=True)
 59 |         if f.auto_created and not f.concrete and (f.one_to_one or f.one_to_many)
 60 |     )
 61 | 
 62 | 
 63 | class Collector:
 64 |     def __init__(self, using):
 65 |         self.using = using
 66 |         # Initially, {model: {instances}}, later values become lists.
 67 |         self.data = {}
 68 |         self.field_updates = {}  # {model: {(field, value): {instances}}}
 69 |         # fast_deletes is a list of queryset-likes that can be deleted without
 70 |         # fetching the objects into memory.
 71 |         self.fast_deletes = []
 72 | 
 73 |         # Tracks deletion-order dependency for databases without transactions
 74 |         # or ability to defer constraint checks. Only concrete model classes
 75 |         # should be included, as the dependencies exist only between actual
 76 |         # database tables; proxy models are represented here by their concrete
 77 |         # parent.
 78 |         self.dependencies = {}  # {model: {models}}
 79 | 
 80 |     def add(self, objs, source=None, nullable=False, reverse_dependency=False):
 81 |         """
 82 |         Add 'objs' to the collection of objects to be deleted.  If the call is
 83 |         the result of a cascade, 'source' should be the model that caused it,
 84 |         and 'nullable' should be set to True if the relation can be null.
 85 | 
 86 |         Return a list of all objects that were not already collected.
 87 |         """
 88 |         if not objs:
 89 |             return []
 90 |         new_objs = []
 91 |         model = objs[0].__class__
 92 |         instances = self.data.setdefault(model, set())
 93 |         for obj in objs:
 94 |             if obj not in instances:
 95 |                 new_objs.append(obj)
 96 |         instances.update(new_objs)
 97 |         # Nullable relationships can be ignored -- they are nulled out before
 98 |         # deleting, and therefore do not affect the order in which objects have
 99 |         # to be deleted.
100 |         if source is not None and not nullable:
101 |             if reverse_dependency:
102 |                 source, model = model, source
103 |             self.dependencies.setdefault(
104 |                 source._meta.concrete_model, set()).add(model._meta.concrete_model)
105 |         return new_objs
106 | 
107 |     def add_field_update(self, field, value, objs):
108 |         """
109 |         Schedule a field update. 'objs' must be a homogeneous iterable
110 |         collection of model instances (e.g. a QuerySet).
111 |         """
112 |         if not objs:
113 |             return
114 |         model = objs[0].__class__
115 |         self.field_updates.setdefault(
116 |             model, {}).setdefault(
117 |             (field, value), set()).update(objs)
118 | 
119 |     def can_fast_delete(self, objs, from_field=None):
120 |         """
121 |         Determine if the objects in the given queryset-like or single object
122 |         can be fast-deleted. This can be done if there are no cascades, no
123 |         parents and no signal listeners for the object class.
124 | 
125 |         The 'from_field' tells where we are coming from - we need this to
126 |         determine if the objects are in fact to be deleted. Allow also
127 |         skipping parent -> child -> parent chain preventing fast delete of
128 |         the child.
129 |         """
130 |         if from_field and from_field.remote_field.on_delete is not CASCADE:
131 |             return False
132 |         if hasattr(objs, '_meta'):
133 |             model = type(objs)
134 |         elif hasattr(objs, 'model') and hasattr(objs, '_raw_delete'):
135 |             model = objs.model
136 |         else:
137 |             return False
138 |         if (signals.pre_delete.has_listeners(model) or
139 |                 signals.post_delete.has_listeners(model) or
140 |                 signals.m2m_changed.has_listeners(model)):
141 |             return False
142 |         # The use of from_field comes from the need to avoid cascade back to
143 |         # parent when parent delete is cascading to child.
144 |         opts = model._meta
145 |         return (
146 |             all(link == from_field for link in opts.concrete_model._meta.parents.values()) and
147 |             # Foreign keys pointing to this model.
148 |             all(
149 |                 related.field.remote_field.on_delete is DO_NOTHING
150 |                 for related in get_candidate_relations_to_delete(opts)
151 |             ) and (
152 |                 # Something like generic foreign key.
153 |                 not any(hasattr(field, 'bulk_related_objects') for field in opts.private_fields)
154 |             )
155 |         )
156 | 
157 |     def get_del_batches(self, objs, field):
158 |         """
159 |         Return the objs in suitably sized batches for the used connection.
160 |         """
161 |         conn_batch_size = max(
162 |             connections[self.using].ops.bulk_batch_size([field.name], objs), 1)
163 |         if len(objs) > conn_batch_size:
164 |             return [objs[i:i + conn_batch_size]
165 |                     for i in range(0, len(objs), conn_batch_size)]
166 |         else:
167 |             return [objs]
168 | 
169 |     def collect(self, objs, source=None, nullable=False, collect_related=True,
170 |                 source_attr=None, reverse_dependency=False, keep_parents=False):
171 |         """
172 |         Add 'objs' to the collection of objects to be deleted as well as all
173 |         parent instances.  'objs' must be a homogeneous iterable collection of
174 |         model instances (e.g. a QuerySet).  If 'collect_related' is True,
175 |         related objects will be handled by their respective on_delete handler.
176 | 
177 |         If the call is the result of a cascade, 'source' should be the model
178 |         that caused it and 'nullable' should be set to True, if the relation
179 |         can be null.
180 | 
181 |         If 'reverse_dependency' is True, 'source' will be deleted before the
182 |         current model, rather than after. (Needed for cascading to parent
183 |         models, the one case in which the cascade follows the forwards
184 |         direction of an FK rather than the reverse direction.)
185 | 
186 |         If 'keep_parents' is True, data of parent model's will be not deleted.
187 |         """
188 |         if self.can_fast_delete(objs):
189 |             self.fast_deletes.append(objs)
190 |             return
191 |         new_objs = self.add(objs, source, nullable,
192 |                             reverse_dependency=reverse_dependency)
193 |         if not new_objs:
194 |             return
195 | 
196 |         model = new_objs[0].__class__
197 | 
198 |         if not keep_parents:
199 |             # Recursively collect concrete model's parent models, but not their
200 |             # related objects. These will be found by meta.get_fields()
201 |             concrete_model = model._meta.concrete_model
202 |             for ptr in concrete_model._meta.parents.values():
203 |                 if ptr:
204 |                     parent_objs = [getattr(obj, ptr.name) for obj in new_objs]
205 |                     self.collect(parent_objs, source=model,
206 |                                  source_attr=ptr.remote_field.related_name,
207 |                                  collect_related=False,
208 |                                  reverse_dependency=True)
209 |         if collect_related:
210 |             parents = model._meta.parents
211 |             for related in get_candidate_relations_to_delete(model._meta):
212 |                 # Preserve parent reverse relationships if keep_parents=True.
213 |                 if keep_parents and related.model in parents:
214 |                     continue
215 |                 field = related.field
216 |                 if field.remote_field.on_delete == DO_NOTHING:
217 |                     continue
218 |                 batches = self.get_del_batches(new_objs, field)
219 |                 for batch in batches:
220 |                     sub_objs = self.related_objects(related, batch)
221 |                     if self.can_fast_delete(sub_objs, from_field=field):
222 |                         self.fast_deletes.append(sub_objs)
223 |                     elif sub_objs:
224 |                         field.remote_field.on_delete(self, field, sub_objs, self.using)
225 |             for field in model._meta.private_fields:
226 |                 if hasattr(field, 'bulk_related_objects'):
227 |                     # It's something like generic foreign key.
228 |                     sub_objs = field.bulk_related_objects(new_objs, self.using)
229 |                     self.collect(sub_objs, source=model, nullable=True)
230 | 
231 |     def related_objects(self, related, objs):
232 |         """
233 |         Get a QuerySet of objects related to `objs` via the relation `related`.
234 |         """
235 |         return related.related_model._base_manager.using(self.using).filter(
236 |             **{"%s__in" % related.field.name: objs}
237 |         )
238 | 
239 |     def instances_with_model(self):
240 |         for model, instances in self.data.items():
241 |             for obj in instances:
242 |                 yield model, obj
243 | 
244 |     def sort(self):
245 |         sorted_models = []
246 |         concrete_models = set()
247 |         models = list(self.data)
248 |         while len(sorted_models) < len(models):
249 |             found = False
250 |             for model in models:
251 |                 if model in sorted_models:
252 |                     continue
253 |                 dependencies = self.dependencies.get(model._meta.concrete_model)
254 |                 if not (dependencies and dependencies.difference(concrete_models)):
255 |                     sorted_models.append(model)
256 |                     concrete_models.add(model._meta.concrete_model)
257 |                     found = True
258 |             if not found:
259 |                 return
260 |         self.data = {model: self.data[model] for model in sorted_models}
261 | 
262 |     def delete(self):
263 |         # sort instance collections
264 |         for model, instances in self.data.items():
265 |             self.data[model] = sorted(instances, key=attrgetter("pk"))
266 | 
267 |         # if possible, bring the models in an order suitable for databases that
268 |         # don't support transactions or cannot defer constraint checks until the
269 |         # end of a transaction.
270 |         self.sort()
271 |         # number of objects deleted for each model label
272 |         deleted_counter = Counter()
273 | 
274 |         # Optimize for the case with a single obj and no dependencies
275 |         if len(self.data) == 1 and len(instances) == 1:
276 |             instance = list(instances)[0]
277 |             if self.can_fast_delete(instance):
278 |                 with transaction.mark_for_rollback_on_error():
279 |                     count = sql.DeleteQuery(model).delete_batch([instance.pk], self.using)
280 |                 setattr(instance, model._meta.pk.attname, None)
281 |                 return count, {model._meta.label: count}
282 | 
283 |         with transaction.atomic(using=self.using, savepoint=False):
284 |             # send pre_delete signals
285 |             for model, obj in self.instances_with_model():
286 |                 if not model._meta.auto_created:
287 |                     signals.pre_delete.send(
288 |                         sender=model, instance=obj, using=self.using
289 |                     )
290 | 
291 |             # fast deletes
292 |             for qs in self.fast_deletes:
293 |                 count = qs._raw_delete(using=self.using)
294 |                 deleted_counter[qs.model._meta.label] += count
295 | 
296 |             # update fields
297 |             for model, instances_for_fieldvalues in self.field_updates.items():
298 |                 for (field, value), instances in instances_for_fieldvalues.items():
299 |                     query = sql.UpdateQuery(model)
300 |                     query.update_batch([obj.pk for obj in instances],
301 |                                        {field.name: value}, self.using)
302 | 
303 |             # reverse instance collections
304 |             for instances in self.data.values():
305 |                 instances.reverse()
306 | 
307 |             # delete instances
308 |             for model, instances in self.data.items():
309 |                 query = sql.DeleteQuery(model)
310 |                 pk_list = [obj.pk for obj in instances]
311 |                 count = query.delete_batch(pk_list, self.using)
312 |                 deleted_counter[model._meta.label] += count
313 | 
314 |                 if not model._meta.auto_created:
315 |                     for obj in instances:
316 |                         signals.post_delete.send(
317 |                             sender=model, instance=obj, using=self.using
318 |                         )
319 | 
320 |         # update collected instances
321 |         for instances_for_fieldvalues in self.field_updates.values():
322 |             for (field, value), instances in instances_for_fieldvalues.items():
323 |                 for obj in instances:
324 |                     setattr(obj, field.attname, value)
325 |         for model, instances in self.data.items():
326 |             for instance in instances:
327 |                 setattr(instance, model._meta.pk.attname, None)
328 |         return sum(deleted_counter.values()), dict(deleted_counter)

--- Step 6 ---
🔧 Thought: Reasoning: Looking at the code, I can see the issue. In the `collect` method (lines 209-229), when iterating through related fields, it calls `self.related_objects(related, batch)` which fetches ALL fields from the related model. However, for deletion purposes, we only need:

1. The primary key (to actually delete the objects)
2. Foreign key fields that reference the parent model (to check if there are further cascades)
3. Any fields that might be used in `on_delete` handlers

The `related_objects` method (lines 231-237) currently just does a simple filter without deferring any fields. The fix should defer all fields except:
- The primary key
- Foreign key fields that point to the model being deleted (for cascade detection)

Let me look at how to determine which fields are needed. I need to understand the structure of `related` and what fields it contains.

  6 | 
  7 | 
  8 | class ProtectedError(IntegrityError):
  9 |     def __init__(self, msg, protected_objects):
 10 |         self.protected_objects = protected_objects
 11 |         super().__init__(msg, protected_objects)
 12 | 
 13 | 
 14 | def CASCADE(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 15 |     collector.collect(sub_objs, source=field.remote_field.model,
 16 |                       source_attr=field.name, nullable=field.null)
 17 |     if field.null and not connections[using].features.can_defer_constraint_checks:
 18 |         collector.add_field_update(field, None, sub_objs)
 19 | 
 20 | 
 21 | def PROTECT(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 22 |     raise ProtectedError(
 23 |         "Cannot delete some instances of model '%s' because they are "
 24 |         "referenced through a protected foreign key: '%s.%s'" % (
 25 |             field.remote_field.model.__name__, sub_objs[0].__class__.__name__, field.name
 26 |         ),
 27 |         sub_objs
 28 |     )
 29 | 
 30 | 
 31 | def SET(value):
 32 |     if callable(value):
 33 |         def set_on_delete(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 34 |             collector.add_field_update(field, value(), sub_objs)
 35 |     else:
 36 |         def set_on_delete(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 37 |             collector.add_field_update(field, value, sub_objs)
 38 |     set_on_delete.deconstruct = lambda: ('django.db.models.SET', (value,), {})
 39 |     return set_on_delete
 40 | 
 41 | 
 42 | def SET_NULL(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 43 |     collector.add_field_update(field, None, sub_objs)
 44 | 
 45 | 
 46 | def SET_DEFAULT(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 47 |     collector.add_field_update(field, field.get_default(), sub_objs)
 48 | 
 49 | 
 50 | def DO_NOTHING(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 51 |     pass
 52 | 
 53 | 
 54 | def get_candidate_relations_to_delete(opts):
 55 |     # The candidate relations are the ones that come from N-1 and 1-1 relations.
 56 |     # N-N  (i.e., many-to-many) relations aren't candidates for deletion.
 57 |     return (
 58 |         f for f in opts.get_fields(include_hidden=True)
 59 |         if f.auto_created and not f.concrete and (f.one_to_one or f.one_to_many)
 60 |     )
 61 | 
 62 | 
 63 | class Collector:
 64 |     def __init__(self, using):
 65 |         self.using = using
 66 |         # Initially, {model: {instances}}, later values become lists.
 67 |         self.data = {}
 68 |         self.field_updates = {}  # {model: {(field, value): {instances}}}
 69 |         # fast_deletes is a list of queryset-likes that can be deleted without
 70 |         # fetching the objects into memory.
 71 |         self.fast_deletes = []
 72 | 
 73 |         # Tracks deletion-order dependency for databases without transactions
 74 |         # or ability to defer constraint checks. Only concrete model classes
 75 |         # should be included, as the dependencies exist only between actual
 76 |         # database tables; proxy models are represented here by their concrete
 77 |         # parent.
 78 |         self.dependencies = {}  # {model: {models}}
 79 | 
 80 |     def add(self, objs, source=None, nullable=False, reverse_dependency=False):
 81 |         """
 82 |         Add 'objs' to the collection of objects to be deleted.  If the call is
 83 |         the result of a cascade, 'source' should be the model that caused it,
 84 |         and 'nullable' should be set to True if the relation can be null.
 85 | 
 86 |         Return a list of all objects that were not already collected.
 87 |         """
 88 |         if not objs:
 89 |             return []
 90 |         new_objs = []
 91 |         model = objs[0].__class__
 92 |         instances = self.data.setdefault(model, set())
 93 |         for obj in objs:
 94 |             if obj not in instances:
 95 |                 new_objs.append(obj)
 96 |         instances.update(new_objs)
 97 |         # Nullable relationships can be ignored -- they are nulled out before
 98 |         # deleting, and therefore do not affect the order in which objects have
 99 |         # to be deleted.
100 |         if source is not None and not nullable:

  99 | class BaseModelAdmin(metaclass=forms.MediaDefiningClass):
 100 |     """Functionality common to both ModelAdmin and InlineAdmin."""
 101 | 
 102 |     autocomplete_fields = ()
 103 |     raw_id_fields = ()
 104 |     fields = None
 105 |     exclude = None
 106 |     fieldsets = None
 107 |     form = forms.ModelForm
 108 |     filter_vertical = ()
 109 |     filter_horizontal = ()
 110 |     radio_fields = {}
 111 |     prepopulated_fields = {}
 112 |     formfield_overrides = {}
 113 |     readonly_fields = ()
 114 |     ordering = None
 115 |     sortable_by = None
 116 |     view_on_site = True
 117 |     show_full_result_count = True
 118 |     checks_class = BaseModelAdminChecks
 119 | 
 120 |     def check(self, **kwargs):
 121 |         return self.checks_class().check(self, **kwargs)
 122 | 
 123 |     def __init__(self):
 124 |         # Merge FORMFIELD_FOR_DBFIELD_DEFAULTS with the formfield_overrides
 125 |         # rather than simply overwriting.
 126 |         overrides = copy.deepcopy(FORMFIELD_FOR_DBFIELD_DEFAULTS)
 127 |         for k, v in self.formfield_overrides.items():
 128 |             overrides.setdefault(k, {}).update(v)
 129 |         self.formfield_overrides = overrides
 130 | 
 131 |     def formfield_for_dbfield(self, db_field, request, **kwargs):
 132 |         """
 133 |         Hook for specifying the form Field instance for a given database Field
 134 |         instance.
 135 | 
 136 |         If kwargs are given, they're passed to the form Field's constructor.
 137 |         """
 138 |         # If the field specifies choices, we don't need to look for special
 139 |         # admin widgets - we just need to use a select widget of some kind.
 140 |         if db_field.choices:
 141 |             return self.formfield_for_choice_field(db_field, request, **kwargs)
 142 | 
 143 |         # ForeignKey or ManyToManyFields
 144 |         if isinstance(db_field, (models.ForeignKey, models.ManyToManyField)):
 145 |             # Combine the field kwargs with any options for formfield_overrides.
 146 |             # Make sure the passed in **kwargs override anything in
 147 |             # formfield_overrides because **kwargs is more specific, and should
 148 |             # always win.
 149 |             if db_field.__class__ in self.formfield_overrides:
 150 |                 kwargs = {**self.formfield_overrides[db_field.__class__], **kwargs}
 151 | 
 152 |             # Get the correct formfield.
 153 |             if isinstance(db_field, models.ForeignKey):
 154 |                 formfield = self.formfield_for_foreignkey(db_field, request, **kwargs)
 155 |             elif isinstance(db_field, models.ManyToManyField):
 156 |                 formfield = self.formfield_for_manytomany(db_field, request, **kwargs)
 157 | 
 158 |             # For non-raw_id fields, wrap the widget with a wrapper that adds
 159 |             # extra HTML -- the "add other" interface -- to the end of the
 160 |             # rendered output. formfield can be None if it came from a
 161 |             # OneToOneField with parent_link=True or a M2M intermediary.
 162 |             if formfield and db_field.name not in self.raw_id_fields:
 163 |                 related_modeladmin = self.admin_site._registry.get(db_field.remote_field.model)
 164 |                 wrapper_kwargs = {}
 165 |                 if related_modeladmin:
 166 |                     wrapper_kwargs.update(
 167 |                         can_add_related=related_modeladmin.has_add_permission(request),
 168 |                         can_change_related=related_modeladmin.has_change_permission(request),
 169 |                         can_delete_related=related_modeladmin.has_delete_permission(request),
 170 |                         can_view_related=related_modeladmin.has_view_permission(request),
 171 |                     )
 172 |                 formfield.widget = widgets.RelatedFieldWidgetWrapper(
 173 |                     formfield.widget, db_field.remote_field, self.admin_site, **wrapper_kwargs
 174 |                 )
 175 | 
 176 |             return formfield
 177 | 
 178 |         # If we've got overrides for the formfield defined, use 'em. **kwargs
 179 |         # passed to formfield_for_dbfield override the defaults.
 180 |         for klass in db_field.__class__.mro():
 181 |             if klass in self.formfield_overrides:
 182 |                 kwargs = {**copy.deepcopy(self.formfield_overrides[klass]), **kwargs}
 183 |                 return db_field.formfield(**kwargs)
 184 | 
 185 |         # For any other type of field, just call its formfield() method.
 186 |         return db_field.formfield(**kwargs)
 187 | 
 188 |     def formfield_for_choice_field(self, db_field, request, **kwargs):
 189 |         """
 190 |         Get a form Field for a database Field that has declared choices.
 191 |         """
 192 |         # If the field is named as a radio_field, use a RadioSelect
 193 |         if db_field.name in self.radio_fields:
 194 |             # Avoid stomping on custom widget/choices arguments.
 195 |             if 'widget' not in kwargs:
 196 |                 kwargs['widget'] = widgets.AdminRadioSelect(attrs={
 197 |                     'class': get_ul_class(self.radio_fields[db_field.name]),
 198 |                 })
 199 |             if 'choices' not in kwargs:
 200 |                 kwargs['choices'] = db_field.get_choices(
 201 |                     include_blank=db_field.blank,
 202 |                     blank_choice=[('', _('None'))]
 203 |                 )
 204 |         return db_field.formfield(**kwargs)
 205 | 
 206 |     def get_field_queryset(self, db, db_field, request):
 207 |         """
 208 |         If the ModelAdmin specifies ordering, the queryset should respect that
 209 |         ordering.  Otherwise don't specify the queryset, let the field decide
 210 |         (return None in that case).
 211 |         """
 212 |         related_admin = self.admin_site._registry.get(db_field.remote_field.model)
 213 |         if related_admin is not None:
 214 |             ordering = related_admin.get_ordering(request)
 215 |             if ordering is not None and ordering != ():
 216 |                 return db_field.remote_field.model._default_manager.using(db).order_by(*ordering)
 217 |         return None
 218 | 
 219 |     def formfield_for_foreignkey(self, db_field, request, **kwargs):
 220 |         """
 221 |         Get a form Field for a ForeignKey.
 222 |         """
 223 |         db = kwargs.get('using')
 224 | 
 225 |         if 'widget' not in kwargs:
 226 |             if db_field.name in self.get_autocomplete_fields(request):
 227 |                 kwargs['widget'] = AutocompleteSelect(db_field.remote_field, self.admin_site, using=db)
 228 |             elif db_field.name in self.raw_id_fields:
 229 |                 kwargs['widget'] = widgets.ForeignKeyRawIdWidget(db_field.remote_field, self.admin_site, using=db)
 230 |             elif db_field.name in self.radio_fields:
 231 |                 kwargs['widget'] = widgets.AdminRadioSelect(attrs={
 232 |                     'class': get_ul_class(self.radio_fields[db_field.name]),
 233 |                 })
 234 |                 kwargs['empty_label'] = _('None') if db_field.blank else None
 235 | 
 236 |         if 'queryset' not in kwargs:
 237 |             queryset = self.get_field_queryset(db, db_field, request)
 238 |             if queryset is not None:
 239 |                 kwargs['queryset'] = queryset
 240 | 
 241 |         return db_field.formfield(**kwargs)
 242 | 
 243 |     def formfield_for_manytomany(self, db_field, request, **kwargs):
 244 |         """
 245 |         Get a form Field for a ManyToManyField.
 246 |         """
 247 |         # If it uses an intermediary model that isn't auto created, don't show
 248 |         # a field in admin.
 249 |         if not db_field.remote_field.through._meta.auto_created:
 250 |             return None
 251 |         db = kwargs.get('using')
 252 | 
 253 |         autocomplete_fields = self.get_autocomplete_fields(request)
 254 |         if db_field.name in autocomplete_fields:
 255 |             kwargs['widget'] = AutocompleteSelectMultiple(db_field.remote_field, self.admin_site, using=db)
 256 |         elif db_field.name in self.raw_id_fields:
 257 |             kwargs['widget'] = widgets.ManyToManyRawIdWidget(db_field.remote_field, self.admin_site, using=db)
 258 |         elif db_field.name in [*self.filter_vertical, *self.filter_horizontal]:
 259 |             kwargs['widget'] = widgets.FilteredSelectMultiple(
 260 |                 db_field.verbose_name,
 261 |                 db_field.name in self.filter_vertical
 262 |             )
 263 | 
 264 |         if 'queryset' not in kwargs:
 265 |             queryset = self.get_field_queryset(db, db_field, request)
 266 |             if queryset is not None:
 267 |                 kwargs['queryset'] = queryset
 268 | 
 269 |         form_field = db_field.formfield(**kwargs)
 270 |         if (isinstance(form_field.widget, SelectMultiple) and
 271 |                 not isinstance(form_field.widget, (CheckboxSelectMultiple, AutocompleteSelectMultiple))):
 272 |             msg = _('Hold down "Control", or "Command" on a Mac, to select more than one.')
 273 |             help_text = form_field.help_text
 274 |             form_field.help_text = format_lazy('{} {}', help_text, msg) if help_text else msg
 275 |         return form_field
 276 | 
 277 |     def get_autocomplete_fields(self, request):
 278 |         """
 279 |         Return a list of ForeignKey and/or ManyToMany fields which should use
 280 |         an autocomplete widget.
 281 |         """
 282 |         return self.autocomplete_fields
 283 | 
 284 |     def get_view_on_site_url(self, obj=None):
 285 |         if obj is None or not self.view_on_site:
 286 |             return None
 287 | 
 288 |         if callable(self.view_on_site):

 312 |     def get_fields(self, request, obj=None):

 314 |         Hook for specifying fields.

 318 |         # _get_form_for_get_fields() is implemented in subclasses.
