 17 | 
 18 | class GenericForeignKey(FieldCacheMixin):
 19 |     """
 20 |     Provide a generic many-to-one relation through the ``content_type`` and
 21 |     ``object_id`` fields.
 22 | 
 23 |     This class also doubles as an accessor to the related object (similar to
 24 |     ForwardManyToOneDescriptor) by adding itself as a model attribute.
 25 |     """
 26 | 
 27 |     # Field flags
 28 |     auto_created = False
 29 |     concrete = False
 30 |     editable = False
 31 |     hidden = False
 32 | 
 33 |     is_relation = True
 34 |     many_to_many = False
 35 |     many_to_one = True
 36 |     one_to_many = False
 37 |     one_to_one = False
 38 |     related_model = None
 39 |     remote_field = None
 40 | 
 41 |     def __init__(self, ct_field='content_type', fk_field='object_id', for_concrete_model=True):
 42 |         self.ct_field = ct_field
 43 |         self.fk_field = fk_field
 44 |         self.for_concrete_model = for_concrete_model
 45 |         self.editable = False
 46 |         self.rel = None
 47 |         self.column = None
 48 | 
 49 |     def contribute_to_class(self, cls, name, **kwargs):
 50 |         self.name = name
 51 |         self.model = cls
 52 |         cls._meta.add_field(self, private=True)
 53 |         setattr(cls, name, self)
 54 | 
 55 |     def get_filter_kwargs_for_object(self, obj):
 56 |         """See corresponding method on Field"""
 57 |         return {
 58 |             self.fk_field: getattr(obj, self.fk_field),
 59 |             self.ct_field: getattr(obj, self.ct_field),
 60 |         }
 61 | 
 62 |     def get_forward_related_filter(self, obj):
 63 |         """See corresponding method on RelatedField"""
 64 |         return {
 65 |             self.fk_field: obj.pk,
 66 |             self.ct_field: ContentType.objects.get_for_model(obj).pk,
 67 |         }
 68 | 
 69 |     def __str__(self):
 70 |         model = self.model
 71 |         app = model._meta.app_label
 72 |         return '%s.%s.%s' % (app, model._meta.object_name, self.name)
 73 | 
 74 |     def check(self, **kwargs):
 75 |         return [
 76 |             *self._check_field_name(),
 77 |             *self._check_object_id_field(),
 78 |             *self._check_content_type_field(),
 79 |         ]
 80 | 
 81 |     def _check_field_name(self):
 82 |         if self.name.endswith("_"):
 83 |             return [
 84 |                 checks.Error(
 85 |                     'Field names must not end with an underscore.',
 86 |                     obj=self,
 87 |                     id='fields.E001',
 88 |                 )
 89 |             ]
 90 |         else:
 91 |             return []
 92 | 
 93 |     def _check_object_id_field(self):
 94 |         try:
 95 |             self.model._meta.get_field(self.fk_field)
 96 |         except FieldDoesNotExist:
 97 |             return [
 98 |                 checks.Error(
 99 |                     "The GenericForeignKey object ID references the "
100 |                     "nonexistent field '%s'." % self.fk_field,
101 |                     obj=self,
102 |                     id='contenttypes.E001',
103 |                 )
104 |             ]
105 |         else:
106 |             return []
107 | 
108 |     def _check_content_type_field(self):
109 |         """
110 |         Check if field named `field_name` in model `model` exists and is a
111 |         valid content_type field (is a ForeignKey to ContentType).
112 |         """
113 |         try:
114 |             field = self.model._meta.get_field(self.ct_field)
115 |         except FieldDoesNotExist:
116 |             return [
117 |                 checks.Error(
118 |                     "The GenericForeignKey content type references the "
119 |                     "nonexistent field '%s.%s'." % (
120 |                         self.model._meta.object_name, self.ct_field
121 |                     ),
122 |                     obj=self,
123 |                     id='contenttypes.E002',
124 |                 )
125 |             ]
126 |         else:
127 |             if not isinstance(field, models.ForeignKey):
128 |                 return [
129 |                     checks.Error(
130 |                         "'%s.%s' is not a ForeignKey." % (
131 |                             self.model._meta.object_name, self.ct_field
132 |                         ),
133 |                         hint=(
134 |                             "GenericForeignKeys must use a ForeignKey to "
135 |                             "'contenttypes.ContentType' as the 'content_type' field."
136 |                         ),
137 |                         obj=self,
138 |                         id='contenttypes.E003',
139 |                     )
140 |                 ]
141 |             elif field.remote_field.model != ContentType:
142 |                 return [
143 |                     checks.Error(
144 |                         "'%s.%s' is not a ForeignKey to 'contenttypes.ContentType'." % (
145 |                             self.model._meta.object_name, self.ct_field
146 |                         ),
147 |                         hint=(
148 |                             "GenericForeignKeys must use a ForeignKey to "
149 |                             "'contenttypes.ContentType' as the 'content_type' field."
150 |                         ),
151 |                         obj=self,
152 |                         id='contenttypes.E004',
153 |                     )
154 |                 ]
155 |             else:
156 |                 return []
157 | 
158 |     def get_cache_name(self):
159 |         return self.name
160 | 
161 |     def get_content_type(self, obj=None, id=None, using=None):
162 |         if obj is not None:
163 |             return ContentType.objects.db_manager(obj._state.db).get_for_model(
164 |                 obj, for_concrete_model=self.for_concrete_model)
165 |         elif id is not None:
166 |             return ContentType.objects.db_manager(using).get_for_id(id)
167 |         else:
168 |             # This should never happen. I love comments like this, don't you?
169 |             raise Exception("Impossible arguments to GFK.get_content_type!")
170 | 
171 |     def get_prefetch_queryset(self, instances, queryset=None):
172 |         if queryset is not None:
173 |             raise ValueError("Custom queryset can't be used for this lookup.")
174 | 
175 |         # For efficiency, group the instances by content type and then do one
176 |         # query per model
177 |         fk_dict = defaultdict(set)
178 |         # We need one instance for each group in order to get the right db:
179 |         instance_dict = {}
180 |         ct_attname = self.model._meta.get_field(self.ct_field).get_attname()
181 |         for instance in instances:
182 |             # We avoid looking for values if either ct_id or fkey value is None
183 |             ct_id = getattr(instance, ct_attname)
184 |             if ct_id is not None:
185 |                 fk_val = getattr(instance, self.fk_field)
186 |                 if fk_val is not None:
187 |                     fk_dict[ct_id].add(fk_val)
188 |                     instance_dict[ct_id] = instance
189 | 
190 |         ret_val = []
191 |         for ct_id, fkeys in fk_dict.items():
192 |             instance = instance_dict[ct_id]
193 |             ct = self.get_content_type(id=ct_id, using=instance._state.db)
194 |             ret_val.extend(ct.get_all_objects_for_this_type(pk__in=fkeys))
195 | 
196 |         # For doing the join in Python, we have to match both the FK val and the
197 |         # content type, so we use a callable that returns a (fk, class) pair.
198 |         def gfk_key(obj):
199 |             ct_id = getattr(obj, ct_attname)
200 |             if ct_id is None:
201 |                 return None
202 |             else:
203 |                 model = self.get_content_type(id=ct_id,
204 |                                               using=obj._state.db).model_class()
205 |                 return (model._meta.pk.get_prep_value(getattr(obj, self.fk_field)),
206 |                         model)
207 | 
208 |         return (
209 |             ret_val,
210 |             lambda obj: (obj.pk, obj.__class__),
211 |             gfk_key,
212 |             True,
213 |             self.name,
214 |             True,
215 |         )
216 | 
217 |     def __get__(self, instance, cls=None):
218 |         if instance is None:
219 |             return self
220 | 
221 |         # Don't use getattr(instance, self.ct_field) here because that might
222 |         # reload the same ContentType over and over (#5570). Instead, get the
223 |         # content type ID here, and later when the actual instance is needed,
224 |         # use ContentType.objects.get_for_id(), which has a global cache.
225 |         f = self.model._meta.get_field(self.ct_field)
226 |         ct_id = getattr(instance, f.get_attname(), None)
227 |         pk_val = getattr(instance, self.fk_field)
228 | 
229 |         rel_obj = self.get_cached_value(instance, default=None)
230 |         if rel_obj is not None:
231 |             ct_match = ct_id == self.get_content_type(obj=rel_obj, using=instance._state.db).id
232 |             pk_match = rel_obj._meta.pk.to_python(pk_val) == rel_obj.pk
233 |             if ct_match and pk_match:
234 |                 return rel_obj
235 |             else:
236 |                 rel_obj = None
237 |         if ct_id is not None:
238 |             ct = self.get_content_type(id=ct_id, using=instance._state.db)
239 |             try:
240 |                 rel_obj = ct.get_object_for_this_type(pk=pk_val)
241 |             except ObjectDoesNotExist:
242 |                 pass
243 |         self.set_cached_value(instance, rel_obj)
244 |         return rel_obj
245 | 
246 |     def __set__(self, instance, value):
247 |         ct = None
248 |         fk = None
249 |         if value is not None:
250 |             ct = self.get_content_type(obj=value)
251 |             fk = value.pk
252 | 
253 |         setattr(instance, self.ct_field, ct)
254 |         setattr(instance, self.fk_field, fk)
255 |         self.set_cached_value(instance, value)
256 | 
257 | 
258 | class GenericRel(ForeignObjectRel):
259 |     """
260 |     Used by GenericRelation to store information about the relation.
261 |     """
262 | 
263 |     def __init__(self, field, to, related_name=None, related_query_name=None, limit_choices_to=None):
264 |         super().__init__(
265 |             field, to, related_name=related_query_name or '+',
266 |             related_query_name=related_query_name,
267 |             limit_choices_to=limit_choices_to, on_delete=DO_NOTHING,
268 |         )
269 | 
270 | 
271 | class GenericRelation(ForeignObject):
272 |     """
273 |     Provide a reverse to a relation created by a GenericForeignKey.
274 |     """
275 | 
276 |     # Field flags
277 |     auto_created = False
278 | 
279 |     many_to_many = False
280 |     many_to_one = False
281 |     one_to_many = True
282 |     one_to_one = False
283 | 
284 |     rel_class = GenericRel
285 | 
286 |     mti_inherited = False
287 | 
288 |     def __init__(self, to, object_id_field='object_id', content_type_field='content_type',
289 |                  for_concrete_model=True, related_query_name=None, limit_choices_to=None, **kwargs):
290 |         kwargs['rel'] = self.rel_class(
291 |             self, to,
292 |             related_query_name=related_query_name,
293 |             limit_choices_to=limit_choices_to,
294 |         )
295 | 
296 |         kwargs['blank'] = True
297 |         kwargs['on_delete'] = models.CASCADE
298 |         kwargs['editable'] = False
299 |         kwargs['serialize'] = False
300 | 
301 |         # This construct is somewhat of an abuse of ForeignObject. This field
302 |         # represents a relation from pk to object_id field. But, this relation
303 |         # isn't direct, the join is generated reverse along foreign key. So,
304 |         # the from_field is object_id field, to_field is pk because of the
305 |         # reverse join.
306 |         super().__init__(to, from_fields=[object_id_field], to_fields=[], **kwargs)
307 | 
308 |         self.object_id_field_name = object_id_field
309 |         self.content_type_field_name = content_type_field
310 |         self.for_concrete_model = for_concrete_model
311 | 
312 |     def check(self, **kwargs):
313 |         return [
314 |             *super().check(**kwargs),
315 |             *self._check_generic_foreign_key_existence(),
316 |         ]
317 | 
318 |     def _is_matching_generic_foreign_key(self, field):
319 |         """
320 |         Return True if field is a GenericForeignKey whose content type and
321 |         object id fields correspond to the equivalent attributes on this
322 |         GenericRelation.
323 |         """
324 |         return (
325 |             isinstance(field, GenericForeignKey) and
326 |             field.ct_field == self.content_type_field_name and
327 |             field.fk_field == self.object_id_field_name
328 |         )
329 | 
330 |     def _check_generic_foreign_key_existence(self):
331 |         target = self.remote_field.model
332 |         if isinstance(target, ModelBase):
333 |             fields = target._meta.private_fields
334 |             if any(self._is_matching_generic_foreign_key(field) for field in fields):
335 |                 return []
336 |             else:
337 |                 return [
338 |                     checks.Error(
339 |                         "The GenericRelation defines a relation with the model "
340 |                         "'%s.%s', but that model does not have a GenericForeignKey." % (
341 |                             target._meta.app_label, target._meta.object_name
342 |                         ),
343 |                         obj=self,
344 |                         id='contenttypes.E004',
345 |                     )
346 |                 ]
347 |         else:
348 |             return []
349 | 
350 |     def resolve_related_fields(self):
351 |         self.to_fields = [self.model._meta.pk.name]
352 |         return [(self.remote_field.model._meta.get_field(self.object_id_field_name), self.model._meta.pk)]
353 | 
354 |     def _get_path_info_with_parent(self, filtered_relation):
355 |         """
356 |         Return the path that joins the current model through any parent models.
357 |         The idea is that if you have a GFK defined on a parent model then we
358 |         need to join the parent model first, then the child model.
359 |         """
360 |         # With an inheritance chain ChildTag -> Tag and Tag defines the
361 |         # GenericForeignKey, and a TaggedItem model has a GenericRelation to
362 |         # ChildTag, then we need to generate a join from TaggedItem to Tag
363 |         # (as Tag.object_id == TaggedItem.pk), and another join from Tag to
364 |         # ChildTag (as that is where the relation is to). Do this by first
365 |         # generating a join to the parent model, then generating joins to the
366 |         # child models.
367 |         path = []
368 |         opts = self.remote_field.model._meta.concrete_model._meta
369 |         parent_opts = opts.get_field(self.object_id_field_name).model._meta
370 |         target = parent_opts.pk
371 |         path.append(PathInfo(
372 |             from_opts=self.model._meta,
373 |             to_opts=parent_opts,
374 |             target_fields=(target,),
375 |             join_field=self.remote_field,
376 |             m2m=True,
377 |             direct=False,
378 |             filtered_relation=filtered_relation,
379 |         ))
380 |         # Collect joins needed for the parent -> child chain. This is easiest
381 |         # to do if we collect joins for the child -> parent chain and then
382 |         # reverse the direction (call to reverse() and use of
383 |         # field.remote_field.get_path_info()).
384 |         parent_field_chain = []
385 |         while parent_opts != opts:
386 |             field = opts.get_ancestor_link(parent_opts.model)
387 |             parent_field_chain.append(field)
388 |             opts = field.remote_field.model._meta
389 |         parent_field_chain.reverse()
390 |         for field in parent_field_chain:
391 |             path.extend(field.remote_field.get_path_info())
392 |         return path
393 | 
394 |     def get_path_info(self, filtered_relation=None):
395 |         opts = self.remote_field.model._meta
396 |         object_id_field = opts.get_field(self.object_id_field_name)
397 |         if object_id_field.model != opts.model:
398 |             return self._get_path_info_with_parent(filtered_relation)
399 |         else:
400 |             target = opts.pk
401 |             return [PathInfo(
402 |                 from_opts=self.model._meta,
403 |                 to_opts=opts,
404 |                 target_fields=(target,),
405 |                 join_field=self.remote_field,
406 |                 m2m=True,
407 |                 direct=False,
408 |                 filtered_relation=filtered_relation,
409 |             )]
410 | 
411 |     def get_reverse_path_info(self, filtered_relation=None):
412 |         opts = self.model._meta
413 |         from_opts = self.remote_field.model._meta
414 |         return [PathInfo(
415 |             from_opts=from_opts,
416 |             to_opts=opts,
417 |             target_fields=(opts.pk,),
418 |             join_field=self,
419 |             m2m=not self.unique,
420 |             direct=False,
421 |             filtered_relation=filtered_relation,
422 |         )]
423 | 
424 |     def value_to_string(self, obj):
425 |         qs = getattr(obj, self.name).all()
426 |         return str([instance.pk for instance in qs])
427 | 
428 |     def contribute_to_class(self, cls, name, **kwargs):
429 |         kwargs['private_only'] = True
430 |         super().contribute_to_class(cls, name, **kwargs)
431 |         self.model = cls
432 |         # Disable the reverse relation for fields inherited by subclasses of a
433 |         # model in multi-table inheritance. The reverse relation points to the
434 |         # field of the base model.
435 |         if self.mti_inherited:
436 |             self.remote_field.related_name = '+'
437 |             self.remote_field.related_query_name = None
438 |         setattr(cls, self.name, ReverseGenericManyToOneDescriptor(self.remote_field))
439 | 
440 |         # Add get_RELATED_order() and set_RELATED_order() to the model this
441 |         # field belongs to, if the model on the other end of this relation
442 |         # is ordered with respect to its corresponding GenericForeignKey.
443 |         if not cls._meta.abstract:
444 | 
445 |             def make_generic_foreign_order_accessors(related_model, model):
446 |                 if self._is_matching_generic_foreign_key(model._meta.order_with_respect_to):
447 |                     make_foreign_order_accessors(model, related_model)
448 | 
449 |             lazy_related_operation(make_generic_foreign_order_accessors, self.model, self.remote_field.model)
450 | 
451 |     def set_attributes_from_rel(self):
452 |         pass
453 | 
454 |     def get_internal_type(self):
455 |         return "ManyToManyField"
456 | 
457 |     def get_content_type(self):
458 |         """
459 |         Return the content type associated with this field's model.
460 |         """
461 |         return ContentType.objects.get_for_model(self.model,
462 |                                                  for_concrete_model=self.for_concrete_model)
463 | 
464 |     def get_extra_restriction(self, where_class, alias, remote_alias):
465 |         field = self.remote_field.model._meta.get_field(self.content_type_field_name)
466 |         contenttype_pk = self.get_content_type().pk
467 |         cond = where_class()
468 |         lookup = field.get_lookup('exact')(field.get_col(remote_alias), contenttype_pk)
469 |         cond.add(lookup, 'AND')
470 |         return cond
471 | 
472 |     def bulk_related_objects(self, objs, using=DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS):
473 |         """
474 |         Return all objects related to ``objs`` via this ``GenericRelation``.
475 |         """
476 |         return self.remote_field.model._base_manager.db_manager(using).filter(**{
477 |             "%s__pk" % self.content_type_field_name: ContentType.objects.db_manager(using).get_for_model(
478 |                 self.model, for_concrete_model=self.for_concrete_model).pk,
479 |             "%s__in" % self.object_id_field_name: [obj.pk for obj in objs]
480 |         })
481 | 
482 | 
483 | class ReverseGenericManyToOneDescriptor(ReverseManyToOneDescriptor):
484 |     """
485 |     Accessor to the related objects manager on the one-to-many relation created
486 |     by GenericRelation.
487 | 
488 |     In the example::
489 | 
490 |         class Post(Model):
491 |             comments = GenericRelation(Comment)
492 | 
493 |     ``post.comments`` is a ReverseGenericManyToOneDescriptor instance.
494 |     """
495 | 
496 |     @cached_property
497 |     def related_manager_cls(self):
498 |         return create_generic_related_manager(
499 |             self.rel.model._default_manager.__class__,
500 |             self.rel,
501 |         )
502 | 
503 | 
504 | def create_generic_related_manager(superclass, rel):
505 |     """
506 |     Factory function to create a manager that subclasses another manager
507 |     (generally the default manager of a given model) and adds behaviors
508 |     specific to generic relations.
509 |     """
510 | 
511 |     class GenericRelatedObjectManager(superclass):
512 |         def __init__(self, instance=None):
513 |             super().__init__()
514 | 
515 |             self.instance = instance
516 | 
517 |             self.model = rel.model
518 | 
519 |             content_type = ContentType.objects.db_manager(instance._state.db).get_for_model(
520 |                 instance, for_concrete_model=rel.field.for_concrete_model)
521 |             self.content_type = content_type
522 |             self.content_type_field_name = rel.field.content_type_field_name
523 |             self.object_id_field_name = rel.field.object_id_field_name
524 |             self.prefetch_cache_name = rel.field.attname
525 |             self.pk_val = instance.pk
526 | 
527 |             self.core_filters = {
528 |                 '%s__pk' % self.content_type_field_name: content_type.id,
529 |                 self.object_id_field_name: self.pk_val,
530 |             }
531 | 
532 |         def __call__(self, *, manager):
533 |             manager = getattr(self.model, manager)
534 |             manager_class = create_generic_related_manager(manager.__class__, rel)
535 |             return manager_class(instance=self.instance)
536 |         do_not_call_in_templates = True
537 | 
538 |         def __str__(self):
539 |             return repr(self)
540 | 
541 |         def _apply_rel_filters(self, queryset):
542 |             """
543 |             Filter the queryset for the instance this manager is bound to.
544 |             """
545 |             db = self._db or router.db_for_read(self.model, instance=self.instance)
546 |             return queryset.using(db).filter(**self.core_filters)
547 | 
548 |         def _remove_prefetched_objects(self):
549 |             try:
550 |                 self.instance._prefetched_objects_cache.pop(self.prefetch_cache_name)
551 |             except (AttributeError, KeyError):
552 |                 pass  # nothing to clear from cache
553 | 
554 |         def get_queryset(self):
555 |             try:
556 |                 return self.instance._prefetched_objects_cache[self.prefetch_cache_name]
557 |             except (AttributeError, KeyError):
558 |                 queryset = super().get_queryset()
559 |                 return self._apply_rel_filters(queryset)
560 | 
561 |         def get_prefetch_queryset(self, instances, queryset=None):
562 |             if queryset is None:
563 |                 queryset = super().get_queryset()
564 | 
565 |             queryset._add_hints(instance=instances[0])
566 |             queryset = queryset.using(queryset._db or self._db)
567 | 
568 |             query = {
569 |                 '%s__pk' % self.content_type_field_name: self.content_type.id,
570 |                 '%s__in' % self.object_id_field_name: {obj.pk for obj in instances}
571 |             }
572 | 
573 |             # We (possibly) need to convert object IDs to the type of the
574 |             # instances' PK in order to match up instances:
575 |             object_id_converter = instances[0]._meta.pk.to_python
576 |             return (
577 |                 queryset.filter(**query),
578 |                 lambda relobj: object_id_converter(getattr(relobj, self.object_id_field_name)),
579 |                 lambda obj: obj.pk,
580 |                 False,
581 |                 self.prefetch_cache_name,
582 |                 False,
583 |             )
584 | 
585 |         def add(self, *objs, bulk=True):
586 |             self._remove_prefetched_objects()
587 |             db = router.db_for_write(self.model, instance=self.instance)
588 | 
589 |             def check_and_update_obj(obj):
590 |                 if not isinstance(obj, self.model):
591 |                     raise TypeError("'%s' instance expected, got %r" % (
592 |                         self.model._meta.object_name, obj
593 |                     ))
594 |                 setattr(obj, self.content_type_field_name, self.content_type)
595 |                 setattr(obj, self.object_id_field_name, self.pk_val)
596 | 
597 |             if bulk:
598 |                 pks = []
599 |                 for obj in objs:
600 |                     if obj._state.adding or obj._state.db != db:
601 |                         raise ValueError(
602 |                             "%r instance isn't saved. Use bulk=False or save "
603 |                             "the object first." % obj
604 |                         )
605 |                     check_and_update_obj(obj)
606 |                     pks.append(obj.pk)
607 | 
608 |                 self.model._base_manager.using(db).filter(pk__in=pks).update(**{
609 |                     self.content_type_field_name: self.content_type,
610 |                     self.object_id_field_name: self.pk_val,
611 |                 })
612 |             else:
613 |                 with transaction.atomic(using=db, savepoint=False):
614 |                     for obj in objs:
615 |                         check_and_update_obj(obj)
616 |                         obj.save()
617 |         add.alters_data = True
618 | 
619 |         def remove(self, *objs, bulk=True):
620 |             if not objs:
621 |                 return
622 |             self._clear(self.filter(pk__in=[o.pk for o in objs]), bulk)
623 |         remove.alters_data = True
624 | 
625 |         def clear(self, *, bulk=True):
626 |             self._clear(self, bulk)
627 |         clear.alters_data = True
628 | 
629 |         def _clear(self, queryset, bulk):
630 |             self._remove_prefetched_objects()
631 |             db = router.db_for_write(self.model, instance=self.instance)
632 |             queryset = queryset.using(db)
633 |             if bulk:
634 |                 # `QuerySet.delete()` creates its own atomic block which
635 |                 # contains the `pre_delete` and `post_delete` signal handlers.
636 |                 queryset.delete()
637 |             else:
638 |                 with transaction.atomic(using=db, savepoint=False):
639 |                     for obj in queryset:
640 |                         obj.delete()
641 |         _clear.alters_data = True
642 | 
643 |         def set(self, objs, *, bulk=True, clear=False):
644 |             # Force evaluation of `objs` in case it's a queryset whose value
645 |             # could be affected by `manager.clear()`. Refs #19816.
646 |             objs = tuple(objs)
647 | 
648 |             db = router.db_for_write(self.model, instance=self.instance)
649 |             with transaction.atomic(using=db, savepoint=False):
650 |                 if clear:
651 |                     self.clear()
652 |                     self.add(*objs, bulk=bulk)
653 |                 else:
654 |                     old_objs = set(self.using(db).all())
655 |                     new_objs = []
656 |                     for obj in objs:
657 |                         if obj in old_objs:
658 |                             old_objs.remove(obj)
659 |                         else:
660 |                             new_objs.append(obj)
661 | 
662 |                     self.remove(*old_objs)
663 |                     self.add(*new_objs, bulk=bulk)
664 |         set.alters_data = True
665 | 
666 |         def create(self, **kwargs):
667 |             self._remove_prefetched_objects()
668 |             kwargs[self.content_type_field_name] = self.content_type
669 |             kwargs[self.object_id_field_name] = self.pk_val
670 |             db = router.db_for_write(self.model, instance=self.instance)
671 |             return super().using(db).create(**kwargs)
672 |         create.alters_data = True
673 | 
674 |         def get_or_create(self, **kwargs):
675 |             kwargs[self.content_type_field_name] = self.content_type
676 |             kwargs[self.object_id_field_name] = self.pk_val
677 |             db = router.db_for_write(self.model, instance=self.instance)
678 |             return super().using(db).get_or_create(**kwargs)
679 |         get_or_create.alters_data = True
680 | 
681 |         def update_or_create(self, **kwargs):
682 |             kwargs[self.content_type_field_name] = self.content_type
683 |             kwargs[self.object_id_field_name] = self.pk_val
684 |             db = router.db_for_write(self.model, instance=self.instance)
685 |             return super().using(db).update_or_create(**kwargs)
686 |         update_or_create.alters_data = True
687 | 
688 |     return GenericRelatedObjectManager

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Use Python stdlib html.escape() to in django.utils.html.escape()
Description
	
The function django.utils.html.escape() partially duplicates the Python stdlib function html.escape(). We can replace this duplication with wider community developed version.
html.escape() has been available since Python 3.2:
​https://docs.python.org/3/library/html.html#html.escape
This function is also faster than Django's. As Python bug ​https://bugs.python.org/issue18020 concludes, using .replace() can be faster than .translate(). This function gets called numerous times when rendering templates. After making the change locally, I saw the following improvement:
master:
$ python -m timeit -s 'from django.utils.html import escape' 'escape(copyright)'
50000 loops, best of 5: 4.03 usec per loop
branch:
$ python -m timeit -s 'from django.utils.html import escape' 'escape(copyright)'
100000 loops, best of 5: 2.45 usec per loop
One small concern, html.escape() converts ' to &#x27 rather than &#39. These values are functionally equivalent HTML, but I'll mention it as a backwards incompatible change as the literal text has changed

 27 | _html_escapes = {
 28 |     ord('&'): '&amp;',

 30 |     ord('>'): '&gt;',

 36 | @keep_lazy(str, SafeString)
 37 | def escape(text):
 38 |     """
 39 |     Return the given text with ampersands, quotes and angle brackets encoded
 40 |     for use in HTML.
 41 | 
 42 |     Always escape input, even if it's already escaped and marked as such.
 43 |     This may result in double-escaping. If this is a concern, use
 44 |     conditional_escape() instead.
 45 |     """
 46 |     return mark_safe(str(text).translate(_html_escapes))

 49 | _js_escapes = {
 50 |     ord('\\'): '\\u005C',
 51 |     ord('\''): '\\u0027',
 52 |     ord('"'): '\\u0022',
 53 |     ord('>'): '\\u003E',
 54 |     ord('<'): '\\u003C',
 55 |     ord('&'): '\\u0026',
 56 |     ord('='): '\\u003D',
 57 |     ord('-'): '\\u002D',
 58 |     ord(';'): '\\u003B',
 59 |     ord('`'): '\\u0060',
 60 |     ord('\u2028'): '\\u2028',
 61 |     ord('\u2029'): '\\u2029'
 62 | }
 63 | 
 64 | # Escape every ASCII character with a value less than 32.
 65 | _js_escapes.update((ord('%c' % z), '\\u%04X' % z) for z in range(32))

 68 | @keep_lazy(str, SafeString)
 69 | def escapejs(value):
 70 |     """Hex encode characters for use in JavaScript strings."""
 71 |     return mark_safe(str(value).translate(_js_escapes))

 73 | 
 74 | _json_script_escapes = {
 75 |     ord('>'): '\\u003E',
 76 |     ord('<'): '\\u003C',
 77 |     ord('&'): '\\u0026',
 78 | }

 80 | 
 81 | def json_script(value, element_id):
 82 |     """
 83 |     Escape all the HTML/XML special characters with their unicode escapes, so
 84 |     value is safe to be output anywhere except for inside a tag attribute. Wrap
 85 |     the escaped JSON in a script tag.
 86 |     """
 87 |     from django.core.serializers.json import DjangoJSONEncoder
 88 |     json_str = json.dumps(value, cls=DjangoJSONEncoder).translate(_json_script_escapes)
 89 |     return format_html(
 90 |         '<script id="{}" type="application/json">{}</script>',
 91 |         element_id, mark_safe(json_str)
 92 |     )

 94 | 
 95 | def conditional_escape(text):
 96 |     """
 97 |     Similar to escape(), except that it doesn't operate on pre-escaped strings.
 98 | 
 99 |     This function relies on the __html__ convention used both by Django's
100 |     SafeData class and by third-party libraries like markupsafe.
101 |     """
102 |     if isinstance(text, Promise):
103 |         text = str(text)
104 |     if hasattr(text, '__html__'):
105 |         return text.__html__()
106 |     else:
107 |         return escape(text)
108 | 
109 | 
110 | def format_html(format_string, *args, **kwargs):
111 |     """
112 |     Similar to str.format, but pass all arguments through conditional_escape(),
113 |     and call mark_safe() on the result. This function should be used instead
114 |     of str.format or % interpolation to build up small HTML fragments.
115 |     """
116 |     args_safe = map(conditional_escape, args)
117 |     kwargs_safe = {k: conditional_escape(v) for (k, v) in kwargs.items()}
118 |     return mark_safe(format_string.format(*args_safe, **kwargs_safe))
119 | 
120 | 
121 | def format_html_join(sep, format_string, args_generator):
122 |     """
123 |     A wrapper of format_html, for the common case of a group of arguments that
124 |     need to be formatted using the same format string, and then joined using
125 |     'sep'. 'sep' is also passed through conditional_escape.
126 | 
127 |     'args_generator' should be an iterator that returns the sequence of 'args'
128 |     that will be passed to format_html.
129 | 
130 |     Example:
131 | 
132 |       format_html_join('\n', "<li>{} {}</li>", ((u.first_name, u.last_name)
133 |                                                   for u in users))
134 |     """
135 |     return mark_safe(conditional_escape(sep).join(
136 |         format_html(format_string, *args)
137 |         for args in args_generator
138 |     ))
139 | 
140 | 
141 | @keep_lazy_text
142 | def linebreaks(value, autoescape=False):
143 |     """Convert newlines into <p> and <br>s."""
144 |     value = normalize_newlines(value)
145 |     paras = re.split('\n{2,}', str(value))
146 |     if autoescape:
147 |         paras = ['<p>%s</p>' % escape(p).replace('\n', '<br>') for p in paras]
148 |     else:
149 |         paras = ['<p>%s</p>' % p.replace('\n', '<br>') for p in paras]
150 |     return '\n\n'.join(paras)

153 | class MLStripper(HTMLParser):
154 |     def __init__(self):
155 |         super().__init__(convert_charrefs=False)
156 |         self.reset()
157 |         self.fed = []
158 | 
159 |     def handle_data(self, d):
160 |         self.fed.append(d)
161 | 
162 |     def handle_entityref(self, name):
163 |         self.fed.append('&%s;' % name)
164 | 
165 |     def handle_charref(self, name):
166 |         self.fed.append('&#%s;' % name)
167 | 
168 |     def get_data(self):
169 |         return ''.join(self.fed)
170 | 
171 | 
172 | def _strip_once(value):
173 |     """
174 |     Internal tag stripping utility used by strip_tags.
175 |     """
176 |     s = MLStripper()
177 |     s.feed(value)
178 |     s.close()
179 |     return s.get_data()
180 | 
181 | 
182 | @keep_lazy_text
183 | def strip_tags(value):
184 |     """Return the given HTML with all tags stripped."""
185 |     # Note: in typical case this loop executes _strip_once once. Loop condition
186 |     # is redundant, but helps to reduce number of executions of _strip_once.
187 |     value = str(value)
188 |     while '<' in value and '>' in value:
189 |         new_value = _strip_once(value)
190 |         if len(new_value) >= len(value):
191 |             # _strip_once was not able to detect more tags
192 |             break
193 |         value = new_value
194 |     return value
195 | 
196 | 
197 | @keep_lazy_text
198 | def strip_spaces_between_tags(value):
199 |     """Return the given HTML with spaces between tags removed."""
200 |     return re.sub(r'>\s+<', '><', str(value))
201 | 
202 | 
203 | def smart_urlquote(url):
204 |     """Quote a URL if it isn't already quoted."""
205 |     def unquote_quote(segment):
206 |         segment = unquote(segment)
207 |         # Tilde is part of RFC3986 Unreserved Characters
208 |         # https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3986#section-2.3
209 |         # See also https://bugs.python.org/issue16285
210 |         return quote(segment, safe=RFC3986_SUBDELIMS + RFC3986_GENDELIMS + '~')
211 | 
212 |     # Handle IDN before quoting.
213 |     try:
214 |         scheme, netloc, path, query, fragment = urlsplit(url)
215 |     except ValueError:
216 |         # invalid IPv6 URL (normally square brackets in hostname part).
217 |         return unquote_quote(url)
218 | 
219 |     try:
220 |         netloc = netloc.encode('idna').decode('ascii')  # IDN -> ACE
221 |     except UnicodeError:  # invalid domain part
222 |         return unquote_quote(url)
223 | 
224 |     if query:
225 |         # Separately unquoting key/value, so as to not mix querystring separators
226 |         # included in query values. See #22267.
227 |         query_parts = [(unquote(q[0]), unquote(q[1]))
228 |                        for q in parse_qsl(query, keep_blank_values=True)]
229 |         # urlencode will take care of quoting
230 |         query = urlencode(query_parts)
231 | 
232 |     path = unquote_quote(path)
233 |     fragment = unquote_quote(fragment)
234 | 
235 |     return urlunsplit((scheme, netloc, path, query, fragment))
236 | 
237 | 
238 | @keep_lazy_text
239 | def urlize(text, trim_url_limit=None, nofollow=False, autoescape=False):
240 |     """
241 |     Convert any URLs in text into clickable links.
242 | 
243 |     Works on http://, https://, www. links, and also on links ending in one of
244 |     the original seven gTLDs (.com, .edu, .gov, .int, .mil, .net, and .org).
245 |     Links can have trailing punctuation (periods, commas, close-parens) and
246 |     leading punctuation (opening parens) and it'll still do the right thing.
247 | 
248 |     If trim_url_limit is not None, truncate the URLs in the link text longer
249 |     than this limit to trim_url_limit - 1 characters and append an ellipsis.
250 | 
251 |     If nofollow is True, give the links a rel="nofollow" attribute.
252 | 
253 |     If autoescape is True, autoescape the link text and URLs.
254 |     """
255 |     safe_input = isinstance(text, SafeData)
256 | 
257 |     def trim_url(x, limit=trim_url_limit):
258 |         if limit is None or len(x) <= limit:
259 |             return x
260 |         return '%s…' % x[:max(0, limit - 1)]
261 | 
262 |     def unescape(text):
263 |         """
264 |         If input URL is HTML-escaped, unescape it so that it can be safely fed
265 |         to smart_urlquote. For example:
266 |         http://example.com?x=1&amp;y=&lt;2&gt; => http://example.com?x=1&y=<2>
267 |         """
268 |         return text.replace('&amp;', '&').replace('&lt;', '<').replace(
269 |             '&gt;', '>').replace('&quot;', '"').replace('&#39;', "'")
270 | 
271 |     def trim_punctuation(lead, middle, trail):
272 |         """
273 |         Trim trailing and wrapping punctuation from `middle`. Return the items
274 |         of the new state.
275 |         """
276 |         # Continue trimming until middle remains unchanged.
277 |         trimmed_something = True
278 |         while trimmed_something:
279 |             trimmed_something = False
280 |             # Trim wrapping punctuation.
281 |             for opening, closing in WRAPPING_PUNCTUATION:
282 |                 if middle.startswith(opening):
283 |                     middle = middle[len(opening):]
284 |                     lead += opening
285 |                     trimmed_something = True
286 |                 # Keep parentheses at the end only if they're balanced.
287 |                 if (middle.endswith(closing) and
288 |                         middle.count(closing) == middle.count(opening) + 1):
289 |                     middle = middle[:-len(closing)]
290 |                     trail = closing + trail
291 |                     trimmed_something = True
292 |             # Trim trailing punctuation (after trimming wrapping punctuation,
293 |             # as encoded entities contain ';'). Unescape entites to avoid
294 |             # breaking them by removing ';'.
295 |             middle_unescaped = unescape(middle)
296 |             stripped = middle_unescaped.rstrip(TRAILING_PUNCTUATION_CHARS)
297 |             if middle_unescaped != stripped:
298 |                 trail = middle[len(stripped):] + trail
299 |                 middle = middle[:len(stripped) - len(middle_unescaped)]
300 |                 trimmed_something = True
301 |         return lead, middle, trail
302 | 
303 |     def is_email_simple(value):
304 |         """Return True if value looks like an email address."""
305 |         # An @ must be in the middle of the value.
306 |         if '@' not in value or value.startswith('@') or value.endswith('@'):
307 |             return False
308 |         try:
309 |             p1, p2 = value.split('@')
310 |         except ValueError:
311 |             # value contains more than one @.
312 |             return False
313 |         # Dot must be in p2 (e.g. example.com)
314 |         if '.' not in p2 or p2.startswith('.'):
315 |             return False
316 |         return True
317 | 
318 |     words = word_split_re.split(str(text))
319 |     for i, word in enumerate(words):
320 |         if '.' in word or '@' in word or ':' in word:
321 |             # lead: Current punctuation trimmed from the beginning of the word.
322 |             # middle: Current state of the word.
323 |             # trail: Current punctuation trimmed from the end of the word.
324 |             lead, middle, trail = '', word, ''
325 |             # Deal with punctuation.
326 |             lead, middle, trail = trim_punctuation(lead, middle, trail)
327 | 
328 |             # Make URL we want to point to.
329 |             url = None
330 |             nofollow_attr = ' rel="nofollow"' if nofollow else ''
331 |             if simple_url_re.match(middle):
332 |                 url = smart_urlquote(unescape(middle))
333 |             elif simple_url_2_re.match(middle):
334 |                 url = smart_urlquote('http://%s' % unescape(middle))
335 |             elif ':' not in middle and is_email_simple(middle):
336 |                 local, domain = middle.rsplit('@', 1)
337 |                 try:
338 |                     domain = domain.encode('idna').decode('ascii')
339 |                 except UnicodeError:
340 |                     continue
341 |                 url = 'mailto:%s@%s' % (local, domain)
342 |                 nofollow_attr = ''
343 | 
344 |             # Make link.
345 |             if url:
346 |                 trimmed = trim_url(middle)
347 |                 if autoescape and not safe_input:
348 |                     lead, trail = escape(lead), escape(trail)
349 |                     trimmed = escape(trimmed)
350 |                 middle = '<a href="%s"%s>%s</a>' % (escape(url), nofollow_attr, trimmed)
351 |                 words[i] = mark_safe('%s%s%s' % (lead, middle, trail))
352 |             else:
353 |                 if safe_input:
354 |                     words[i] = mark_safe(word)
355 |                 elif autoescape:
356 |                     words[i] = escape(word)
357 |         elif safe_input:
358 |             words[i] = mark_safe(word)
359 |         elif autoescape:
360 |             words[i] = escape(word)
361 |     return ''.join(words)
362 | 
363 | 
364 | def avoid_wrapping(value):
365 |     """
366 |     Avoid text wrapping in the middle of a phrase by adding non-breaking
367 |     spaces where there previously were normal spaces.
368 |     """
369 |     return value.replace(" ", "\xa0")
370 | 
371 | 
372 | def html_safe(klass):
373 |     """
374 |     A decorator that defines the __html__ method. This helps non-Django
375 |     templates to detect classes whose __str__ methods return SafeString.
376 |     """
377 |     if '__html__' in klass.__dict__:
378 |         raise ValueError(
379 |             "can't apply @html_safe to %s because it defines "
380 |             "__html__()." % klass.__name__
381 |         )
382 |     if '__str__' not in klass.__dict__:
383 |         raise ValueError(
384 |             "can't apply @html_safe to %s because it doesn't "
385 |             "define __str__()." % klass.__name__
386 |         )
387 |     klass_str = klass.__str__
388 |     klass.__str__ = lambda self: mark_safe(klass_str(self))
389 |     klass.__html__ = lambda self: str(self)
390 |     return klass

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Add --skip-checks option to management commands.
Description
	
Management commands already have skip_checks stealth option. I propose exposing this option on the command line. This would allow users to skip checks when running a command from the command line. Sometimes in a development environment, it is nice to move ahead with a task at hand rather than getting side tracked fixing a system check.



## Instructions

1. Use `Grep` and `Glob` to locate the relevant source files. Start by searching for key class names, function names, or error messages mentioned in the issue.
2. Use `Read` to understand the code around the identified location. Read enough context (surrounding functions, class definitions) to fully understand the logic.
3. Reason about the root cause based on the issue description and the code you read. Explain your understanding before making changes.
4. Use `Edit` to implement a minimal, targeted fix. Change only what is necessary to resolve the issue.
5. Use `Read` to verify your edit was applied correctly.

## Rules

- Do NOT run `pip install`, `python setup.py`, or any installation commands. The environment is pre-configured.
- Do NOT run test commands (`pytest`, `python -m pytest`, etc.). Testing is handled separately after your changes.
- Do NOT modify any test files (files under `tests/` or `test_*.py`).
- Make the smallest possible change that fixes the issue.
- Preserve existing code style, indentation, and conventions.
- If the fix requires changes in multiple files, edit each one.

 17 | class CommandError(Exception):
 18 |     """
 19 |     Exception class indicating a problem while executing a management
 20 |     command.
 21 | 
 22 |     If this exception is raised during the execution of a management
 23 |     command, it will be caught and turned into a nicely-printed error
 24 |     message to the appropriate output stream (i.e., stderr); as a
 25 |     result, raising this exception (with a sensible description of the
 26 |     error) is the preferred way to indicate that something has gone
 27 |     wrong in the execution of a command.
 28 |     """
 29 |     pass
 30 | 
 31 | 
 32 | class SystemCheckError(CommandError):
 33 |     """
 34 |     The system check framework detected unrecoverable errors.
 35 |     """
 36 |     pass

 38 | 
 39 | class CommandParser(ArgumentParser):
 40 |     """
 41 |     Customized ArgumentParser class to improve some error messages and prevent
 42 |     SystemExit in several occasions, as SystemExit is unacceptable when a
 43 |     command is called programmatically.
 44 |     """
 45 |     def __init__(self, *, missing_args_message=None, called_from_command_line=None, **kwargs):
 46 |         self.missing_args_message = missing_args_message
 47 |         self.called_from_command_line = called_from_command_line
 48 |         super().__init__(**kwargs)
 49 | 
 50 |     def parse_args(self, args=None, namespace=None):
 51 |         # Catch missing argument for a better error message
 52 |         if (self.missing_args_message and
 53 |                 not (args or any(not arg.startswith('-') for arg in args))):
 54 |             self.error(self.missing_args_message)
 55 |         return super().parse_args(args, namespace)
 56 | 
 57 |     def error(self, message):
 58 |         if self.called_from_command_line:
 59 |             super().error(message)
 60 |         else:
 61 |             raise CommandError("Error: %s" % message)
 62 | 
 63 | 
 64 | def handle_default_options(options):
 65 |     """
 66 |     Include any default options that all commands should accept here
 67 |     so that ManagementUtility can handle them before searching for
 68 |     user commands.
 69 |     """
 70 |     if options.settings:
 71 |         os.environ['DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE'] = options.settings
 72 |     if options.pythonpath:
 73 |         sys.path.insert(0, options.pythonpath)
 74 | 
 75 | 
 76 | def no_translations(handle_func):
 77 |     """Decorator that forces a command to run with translations deactivated."""
 78 |     def wrapped(*args, **kwargs):
 79 |         from django.utils import translation
 80 |         saved_locale = translation.get_language()
 81 |         translation.deactivate_all()
 82 |         try:
 83 |             res = handle_func(*args, **kwargs)
 84 |         finally:
 85 |             if saved_locale is not None:
 86 |                 translation.activate(saved_locale)
 87 |         return res
 88 |     return wrapped
 89 | 
 90 | 
 91 | class DjangoHelpFormatter(HelpFormatter):
 92 |     """
 93 |     Customized formatter so that command-specific arguments appear in the
 94 |     --help output before arguments common to all commands.
 95 |     """
 96 |     show_last = {
 97 |         '--version', '--verbosity', '--traceback', '--settings', '--pythonpath',
 98 |         '--no-color', '--force-color',
 99 |     }
100 | 
101 |     def _reordered_actions(self, actions):
102 |         return sorted(
103 |             actions,
104 |             key=lambda a: set(a.option_strings) & self.show_last != set()
105 |         )
106 | 
107 |     def add_usage(self, usage, actions, *args, **kwargs):
108 |         super().add_usage(usage, self._reordered_actions(actions), *args, **kwargs)
109 | 
110 |     def add_arguments(self, actions):
111 |         super().add_arguments(self._reordered_actions(actions))
112 | 
113 | 
114 | class OutputWrapper(TextIOBase):
115 |     """
116 |     Wrapper around stdout/stderr
117 |     """
118 |     @property
119 |     def style_func(self):
120 |         return self._style_func
121 | 
122 |     @style_func.setter
123 |     def style_func(self, style_func):
124 |         if style_func and self.isatty():
125 |             self._style_func = style_func
126 |         else:
127 |             self._style_func = lambda x: x
128 | 
129 |     def __init__(self, out, ending='\n'):
130 |         self._out = out
131 |         self.style_func = None
132 |         self.ending = ending
133 | 
134 |     def __getattr__(self, name):
135 |         return getattr(self._out, name)
136 | 
137 |     def isatty(self):
138 |         return hasattr(self._out, 'isatty') and self._out.isatty()
139 | 
140 |     def write(self, msg, style_func=None, ending=None):
141 |         ending = self.ending if ending is None else ending
142 |         if ending and not msg.endswith(ending):
143 |             msg += ending
144 |         style_func = style_func or self.style_func
145 |         self._out.write(style_func(msg))
146 | 
147 | 
148 | class BaseCommand:
149 |     """
150 |     The base class from which all management commands ultimately
151 |     derive.
152 | 
153 |     Use this class if you want access to all of the mechanisms which
154 |     parse the command-line arguments and work out what code to call in
155 |     response; if you don't need to change any of that behavior,
156 |     consider using one of the subclasses defined in this file.
157 | 
158 |     If you are interested in overriding/customizing various aspects of
159 |     the command-parsing and -execution behavior, the normal flow works
160 |     as follows:
161 | 
162 |     1. ``django-admin`` or ``manage.py`` loads the command class
163 |        and calls its ``run_from_argv()`` method.
164 | 
165 |     2. The ``run_from_argv()`` method calls ``create_parser()`` to get
166 |        an ``ArgumentParser`` for the arguments, parses them, performs
167 |        any environment changes requested by options like
168 |        ``pythonpath``, and then calls the ``execute()`` method,
169 |        passing the parsed arguments.
170 | 
171 |     3. The ``execute()`` method attempts to carry out the command by
172 |        calling the ``handle()`` method with the parsed arguments; any
173 |        output produced by ``handle()`` will be printed to standard
174 |        output and, if the command is intended to produce a block of
175 |        SQL statements, will be wrapped in ``BEGIN`` and ``COMMIT``.
176 | 
177 |     4. If ``handle()`` or ``execute()`` raised any exception (e.g.
178 |        ``CommandError``), ``run_from_argv()`` will  instead print an error
179 |        message to ``stderr``.
180 | 
181 |     Thus, the ``handle()`` method is typically the starting point for
182 |     subclasses; many built-in commands and command types either place
183 |     all of their logic in ``handle()``, or perform some additional
184 |     parsing work in ``handle()`` and then delegate from it to more
185 |     specialized methods as needed.
186 | 
187 |     Several attributes affect behavior at various steps along the way:
188 | 
189 |     ``help``
190 |         A short description of the command, which will be printed in
191 |         help messages.
192 | 
193 |     ``output_transaction``
194 |         A boolean indicating whether the command outputs SQL
195 |         statements; if ``True``, the output will automatically be
196 |         wrapped with ``BEGIN;`` and ``COMMIT;``. Default value is
197 |         ``False``.
198 | 
199 |     ``requires_migrations_checks``
200 |         A boolean; if ``True``, the command prints a warning if the set of
201 |         migrations on disk don't match the migrations in the database.
202 | 
203 |     ``requires_system_checks``
204 |         A boolean; if ``True``, entire Django project will be checked for errors
205 |         prior to executing the command. Default value is ``True``.
206 |         To validate an individual application's models
207 |         rather than all applications' models, call
208 |         ``self.check(app_configs)`` from ``handle()``, where ``app_configs``
209 |         is the list of application's configuration provided by the
210 |         app registry.
211 | 
212 |     ``stealth_options``
213 |         A tuple of any options the command uses which aren't defined by the
214 |         argument parser.
215 |     """
216 |     # Metadata about this command.
217 |     help = ''
218 | 
219 |     # Configuration shortcuts that alter various logic.
220 |     _called_from_command_line = False
221 |     output_transaction = False  # Whether to wrap the output in a "BEGIN; COMMIT;"
222 |     requires_migrations_checks = False
223 |     requires_system_checks = True
224 |     # Arguments, common to all commands, which aren't defined by the argument
225 |     # parser.
226 |     base_stealth_options = ('skip_checks', 'stderr', 'stdout')
227 |     # Command-specific options not defined by the argument parser.
228 |     stealth_options = ()
229 | 
230 |     def __init__(self, stdout=None, stderr=None, no_color=False, force_color=False):
231 |         self.stdout = OutputWrapper(stdout or sys.stdout)
232 |         self.stderr = OutputWrapper(stderr or sys.stderr)
233 |         if no_color and force_color:
234 |             raise CommandError("'no_color' and 'force_color' can't be used together.")
235 |         if no_color:
236 |             self.style = no_style()
237 |         else:
238 |             self.style = color_style(force_color)
239 |             self.stderr.style_func = self.style.ERROR
240 | 
241 |     def get_version(self):
242 |         """
243 |         Return the Django version, which should be correct for all built-in
244 |         Django commands. User-supplied commands can override this method to
245 |         return their own version.
246 |         """
247 |         return django.get_version()
248 | 
249 |     def create_parser(self, prog_name, subcommand, **kwargs):
250 |         """
251 |         Create and return the ``ArgumentParser`` which will be used to
252 |         parse the arguments to this command.
253 |         """
254 |         parser = CommandParser(
255 |             prog='%s %s' % (os.path.basename(prog_name), subcommand),
256 |             description=self.help or None,
257 |             formatter_class=DjangoHelpFormatter,
258 |             missing_args_message=getattr(self, 'missing_args_message', None),
259 |             called_from_command_line=getattr(self, '_called_from_command_line', None),
260 |             **kwargs
261 |         )
262 |         parser.add_argument('--version', action='version', version=self.get_version())
263 |         parser.add_argument(
264 |             '-v', '--verbosity', default=1,
265 |             type=int, choices=[0, 1, 2, 3],
266 |             help='Verbosity level; 0=minimal output, 1=normal output, 2=verbose output, 3=very verbose output',
267 |         )
268 |         parser.add_argument(
269 |             '--settings',
270 |             help=(
271 |                 'The Python path to a settings module, e.g. '
272 |                 '"myproject.settings.main". If this isn\'t provided, the '
273 |                 'DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE environment variable will be used.'
274 |             ),
275 |         )
276 |         parser.add_argument(
277 |             '--pythonpath',
278 |             help='A directory to add to the Python path, e.g. "/home/djangoprojects/myproject".',
279 |         )
280 |         parser.add_argument('--traceback', action='store_true', help='Raise on CommandError exceptions')
281 |         parser.add_argument(
282 |             '--no-color', action='store_true',
283 |             help="Don't colorize the command output.",
284 |         )
285 |         parser.add_argument(
286 |             '--force-color', action='store_true',
287 |             help='Force colorization of the command output.',
288 |         )
289 |         self.add_arguments(parser)
290 |         return parser
291 | 
292 |     def add_arguments(self, parser):
293 |         """
294 |         Entry point for subclassed commands to add custom arguments.
295 |         """
296 |         pass
297 | 
298 |     def print_help(self, prog_name, subcommand):
299 |         """
300 |         Print the help message for this command, derived from
301 |         ``self.usage()``.
302 |         """
303 |         parser = self.create_parser(prog_name, subcommand)
304 |         parser.print_help()
305 | 
306 |     def run_from_argv(self, argv):
307 |         """
308 |         Set up any environment changes requested (e.g., Python path
309 |         and Django settings), then run this command. If the
310 |         command raises a ``CommandError``, intercept it and print it sensibly
311 |         to stderr. If the ``--traceback`` option is present or the raised
312 |         ``Exception`` is not ``CommandError``, raise it.
313 |         """
314 |         self._called_from_command_line = True
315 |         parser = self.create_parser(argv[0], argv[1])
316 | 
317 |         options = parser.parse_args(argv[2:])
318 |         cmd_options = vars(options)
319 |         # Move positional args out of options to mimic legacy optparse
320 |         args = cmd_options.pop('args', ())
321 |         handle_default_options(options)
322 |         try:
323 |             self.execute(*args, **cmd_options)
324 |         except Exception as e:
325 |             if options.traceback or not isinstance(e, CommandError):
326 |                 raise
327 | 
328 |             # SystemCheckError takes care of its own formatting.
329 |             if isinstance(e, SystemCheckError):
330 |                 self.stderr.write(str(e), lambda x: x)
331 |             else:
332 |                 self.stderr.write('%s: %s' % (e.__class__.__name__, e))
333 |             sys.exit(1)
334 |         finally:
335 |             try:
336 |                 connections.close_all()
337 |             except ImproperlyConfigured:
338 |                 # Ignore if connections aren't setup at this point (e.g. no
339 |                 # configured settings).
340 |                 pass
341 | 
342 |     def execute(self, *args, **options):
343 |         """
344 |         Try to execute this command, performing system checks if needed (as
345 |         controlled by the ``requires_system_checks`` attribute, except if
346 |         force-skipped).
347 |         """
348 |         if options['force_color'] and options['no_color']:
349 |             raise CommandError("The --no-color and --force-color options can't be used together.")
350 |         if options['force_color']:
351 |             self.style = color_style(force_color=True)
352 |         elif options['no_color']:
353 |             self.style = no_style()
354 |             self.stderr.style_func = None
355 |         if options.get('stdout'):
356 |             self.stdout = OutputWrapper(options['stdout'])
357 |         if options.get('stderr'):
358 |             self.stderr = OutputWrapper(options['stderr'])
359 | 
360 |         if self.requires_system_checks and not options.get('skip_checks'):
361 |             self.check()
362 |         if self.requires_migrations_checks:
363 |             self.check_migrations()
364 |         output = self.handle(*args, **options)
365 |         if output:
366 |             if self.output_transaction:
367 |                 connection = connections[options.get('database', DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS)]
368 |                 output = '%s\n%s\n%s' % (
369 |                     self.style.SQL_KEYWORD(connection.ops.start_transaction_sql()),
370 |                     output,
371 |                     self.style.SQL_KEYWORD(connection.ops.end_transaction_sql()),
372 |                 )
373 |             self.stdout.write(output)
374 |         return output
375 | 
376 |     def _run_checks(self, **kwargs):
377 |         return checks.run_checks(**kwargs)
378 | 
379 |     def check(self, app_configs=None, tags=None, display_num_errors=False,
380 |               include_deployment_checks=False, fail_level=checks.ERROR):
381 |         """
382 |         Use the system check framework to validate entire Django project.
383 |         Raise CommandError for any serious message (error or critical errors).
384 |         If there are only light messages (like warnings), print them to stderr
385 |         and don't raise an exception.
386 |         """
387 |         all_issues = self._run_checks(
388 |             app_configs=app_configs,
389 |             tags=tags,
390 |             include_deployment_checks=include_deployment_checks,
391 |         )
392 | 
393 |         header, body, footer = "", "", ""
394 |         visible_issue_count = 0  # excludes silenced warnings
395 | 
396 |         if all_issues:
397 |             debugs = [e for e in all_issues if e.level < checks.INFO and not e.is_silenced()]
398 |             infos = [e for e in all_issues if checks.INFO <= e.level < checks.WARNING and not e.is_silenced()]
399 |             warnings = [e for e in all_issues if checks.WARNING <= e.level < checks.ERROR and not e.is_silenced()]
400 |             errors = [e for e in all_issues if checks.ERROR <= e.level < checks.CRITICAL and not e.is_silenced()]
401 |             criticals = [e for e in all_issues if checks.CRITICAL <= e.level and not e.is_silenced()]
402 |             sorted_issues = [
403 |                 (criticals, 'CRITICALS'),
404 |                 (errors, 'ERRORS'),
405 |                 (warnings, 'WARNINGS'),
406 |                 (infos, 'INFOS'),
407 |                 (debugs, 'DEBUGS'),
408 |             ]
409 | 
410 |             for issues, group_name in sorted_issues:
411 |                 if issues:
412 |                     visible_issue_count += len(issues)
413 |                     formatted = (
414 |                         self.style.ERROR(str(e))
415 |                         if e.is_serious()
416 |                         else self.style.WARNING(str(e))
417 |                         for e in issues)
418 |                     formatted = "\n".join(sorted(formatted))
419 |                     body += '\n%s:\n%s\n' % (group_name, formatted)
420 | 
421 |         if visible_issue_count:
422 |             header = "System check identified some issues:\n"
423 | 
424 |         if display_num_errors:
425 |             if visible_issue_count:
426 |                 footer += '\n'
427 |             footer += "System check identified %s (%s silenced)." % (
428 |                 "no issues" if visible_issue_count == 0 else
429 |                 "1 issue" if visible_issue_count == 1 else
430 |                 "%s issues" % visible_issue_count,
431 |                 len(all_issues) - visible_issue_count,
432 |             )
433 | 
434 |         if any(e.is_serious(fail_level) and not e.is_silenced() for e in all_issues):
435 |             msg = self.style.ERROR("SystemCheckError: %s" % header) + body + footer
436 |             raise SystemCheckError(msg)
437 |         else:
438 |             msg = header + body + footer
439 | 
440 |         if msg:
441 |             if visible_issue_count:
442 |                 self.stderr.write(msg, lambda x: x)
443 |             else:
444 |                 self.stdout.write(msg)
445 | 
446 |     def check_migrations(self):
447 |         """
448 |         Print a warning if the set of migrations on disk don't match the
449 |         migrations in the database.
450 |         """
451 |         from django.db.migrations.executor import MigrationExecutor
452 |         try:
453 |             executor = MigrationExecutor(connections[DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS])
454 |         except ImproperlyConfigured:
455 |             # No databases are configured (or the dummy one)
456 |             return
457 | 
458 |         plan = executor.migration_plan(executor.loader.graph.leaf_nodes())
459 |         if plan:
460 |             apps_waiting_migration = sorted({migration.app_label for migration, backwards in plan})
461 |             self.stdout.write(
462 |                 self.style.NOTICE(
463 |                     "\nYou have %(unpplied_migration_count)s unapplied migration(s). "
464 |                     "Your project may not work properly until you apply the "
465 |                     "migrations for app(s): %(apps_waiting_migration)s." % {
466 |                         "unpplied_migration_count": len(plan),
467 |                         "apps_waiting_migration": ", ".join(apps_waiting_migration),
468 |                     }
469 |                 )
470 |             )
471 |             self.stdout.write(self.style.NOTICE("Run 'python manage.py migrate' to apply them.\n"))
472 | 
473 |     def handle(self, *args, **options):
474 |         """
475 |         The actual logic of the command. Subclasses must implement
476 |         this method.
477 |         """
478 |         raise NotImplementedError('subclasses of BaseCommand must provide a handle() method')
479 | 
480 | 
481 | class AppCommand(BaseCommand):
482 |     """
483 |     A management command which takes one or more installed application labels
484 |     as arguments, and does something with each of them.
485 | 
486 |     Rather than implementing ``handle()``, subclasses must implement
487 |     ``handle_app_config()``, which will be called once for each application.
488 |     """
489 |     missing_args_message = "Enter at least one application label."
490 | 
491 |     def add_arguments(self, parser):
492 |         parser.add_argument('args', metavar='app_label', nargs='+', help='One or more application label.')
493 | 
494 |     def handle(self, *app_labels, **options):
495 |         from django.apps import apps
496 |         try:
497 |             app_configs = [apps.get_app_config(app_label) for app_label in app_labels]
498 |         except (LookupError, ImportError) as e:
499 |             raise CommandError("%s. Are you sure your INSTALLED_APPS setting is correct?" % e)
500 |         output = []
501 |         for app_config in app_configs:
502 |             app_output = self.handle_app_config(app_config, **options)
503 |             if app_output:
504 |                 output.append(app_output)
505 |         return '\n'.join(output)
506 | 
507 |     def handle_app_config(self, app_config, **options):
508 |         """
509 |         Perform the command's actions for app_config, an AppConfig instance
510 |         corresponding to an application label given on the command line.
511 |         """
512 |         raise NotImplementedError(
513 |             "Subclasses of AppCommand must provide"
514 |             "a handle_app_config() method.")
515 | 
516 | 
517 | class LabelCommand(BaseCommand):
518 |     """
519 |     A management command which takes one or more arbitrary arguments
520 |     (labels) on the command line, and does something with each of
521 |     them.
522 | 
523 |     Rather than implementing ``handle()``, subclasses must implement
524 |     ``handle_label()``, which will be called once for each label.
525 | 
526 |     If the arguments should be names of installed applications, use
527 |     ``AppCommand`` instead.
528 |     """
529 |     label = 'label'
530 |     missing_args_message = "Enter at least one %s." % label
531 | 
532 |     def add_arguments(self, parser):
533 |         parser.add_argument('args', metavar=self.label, nargs='+')
534 | 
535 |     def handle(self, *labels, **options):
536 |         output = []
537 |         for label in labels:
538 |             label_output = self.handle_label(label, **options)
539 |             if label_output:
540 |                 output.append(label_output)
541 |         return '\n'.join(output)
542 | 
543 |     def handle_label(self, label, **options):
544 |         """
545 |         Perform the command's actions for ``label``, which will be the
546 |         string as given on the command line.
547 |         """
548 |         raise NotImplementedError('subclasses of LabelCommand must provide a handle_label() method')
