133 | class RemoteUserBackend(ModelBackend):
134 |     """
135 |     This backend is to be used in conjunction with the ``RemoteUserMiddleware``
136 |     found in the middleware module of this package, and is used when the server
137 |     is handling authentication outside of Django.
138 | 
139 |     By default, the ``authenticate`` method creates ``User`` objects for
140 |     usernames that don't already exist in the database.  Subclasses can disable
141 |     this behavior by setting the ``create_unknown_user`` attribute to
142 |     ``False``.
143 |     """
144 | 
145 |     # Create a User object if not already in the database?
146 |     create_unknown_user = True
147 | 
148 |     def authenticate(self, request, remote_user):
149 |         """
150 |         The username passed as ``remote_user`` is considered trusted. Return
151 |         the ``User`` object with the given username. Create a new ``User``
152 |         object if ``create_unknown_user`` is ``True``.
153 | 
154 |         Return None if ``create_unknown_user`` is ``False`` and a ``User``
155 |         object with the given username is not found in the database.
156 |         """
157 |         if not remote_user:
158 |             return
159 |         user = None
160 |         username = self.clean_username(remote_user)
161 | 
162 |         # Note that this could be accomplished in one try-except clause, but
163 |         # instead we use get_or_create when creating unknown users since it has
164 |         # built-in safeguards for multiple threads.
165 |         if self.create_unknown_user:
166 |             user, created = UserModel._default_manager.get_or_create(**{
167 |                 UserModel.USERNAME_FIELD: username
168 |             })
169 |             if created:
170 |                 args = (request, user)
171 |                 try:
172 |                     inspect.getcallargs(self.configure_user, request, user)
173 |                 except TypeError:
174 |                     args = (user,)
175 |                     warnings.warn(
176 |                         'Update %s.configure_user() to accept `request` as '
177 |                         'the first argument.'
178 |                         % self.__class__.__name__, RemovedInDjango31Warning
179 |                     )
180 |                 user = self.configure_user(*args)
181 |         else:
182 |             try:
183 |                 user = UserModel._default_manager.get_by_natural_key(username)
184 |             except UserModel.DoesNotExist:
185 |                 pass

--- Step 6 ---
🔧 Finish: Final answer: Fixed the bug in `django/contrib/auth/backends.py` by adding an early return in `ModelBackend.authenticate()` when either `username` or `password` is `None`. This prevents unnecessary database queries (like `WHERE username IS NULL`) and avoids running the expensive password hasher when credentials are incomplete. The fix adds 2 lines after extracting the username from kwargs:

```python
if username is None or password is None:
    return
```
🎉 Final answer: Fixed the bug in `django/contrib/auth/backends.py` by adding an early return in `ModelBackend.authenticate()` when either `username` or `password` is `None`. This prevents unnecessary database queries (like `WHERE username IS NULL`) and avoids running the expensive password hasher when credentials are incomplete. The fix adds 2 lines after extracting the username from kwargs:

```python
if username is None or password is None:
    return
```

[53/500] django__django-11532

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Email messages crash on non-ASCII domain when email encoding is non-unicode.
Description
	
When the computer hostname is set in unicode (in my case "正宗"), the following test fails: ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/tests/mail/tests.py#L368
Specifically, since the encoding is set to iso-8859-1, Python attempts to convert all of the headers to that encoding, including the Message-ID header which has been set here: ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/core/mail/message.py#L260
This is not just a problem in the tests, Django should be handling the encoding of the message properly
Steps to recreate:
Set hostname to non iso-8859-1 value (i.e. hostname 正宗)
run the mail tests
Fix:
have django.core.mail.utils or django.core.mail.message convert domain name to punycode before using
Test:
from unittest.mock import patch
from django.core.mail import EmailMessage
with patch("django.core.mailmessage.DNS_NAME", "漢字"):
	email = EmailMessage('subject', '', 'from@example.com', ['to@example.com'])
	email.encoding = 'iso-8859-1'
	message = email.message()
	self.assertIn('xn--p8s937b', message['Message-ID'])

 35 | class BadHeaderError(ValueError):
 36 |     pass
 37 | 
 38 | 
 39 | # Header names that contain structured address data (RFC #5322)
 40 | ADDRESS_HEADERS = {
 41 |     'from',
 42 |     'sender',
 43 |     'reply-to',
 44 |     'to',
 45 |     'cc',
 46 |     'bcc',
 47 |     'resent-from',
 48 |     'resent-sender',
 49 |     'resent-to',
 50 |     'resent-cc',
 51 |     'resent-bcc',
 52 | }
 53 | 
 54 | 
 55 | def forbid_multi_line_headers(name, val, encoding):
 56 |     """Forbid multi-line headers to prevent header injection."""
 57 |     encoding = encoding or settings.DEFAULT_CHARSET
 58 |     val = str(val)  # val may be lazy
 59 |     if '\n' in val or '\r' in val:
 60 |         raise BadHeaderError("Header values can't contain newlines (got %r for header %r)" % (val, name))
 61 |     try:
 62 |         val.encode('ascii')
 63 |     except UnicodeEncodeError:
 64 |         if name.lower() in ADDRESS_HEADERS:
 65 |             val = ', '.join(sanitize_address(addr, encoding) for addr in getaddresses((val,)))
 66 |         else:
 67 |             val = Header(val, encoding).encode()
 68 |     else:
 69 |         if name.lower() == 'subject':
 70 |             val = Header(val).encode()
 71 |     return name, val
 72 | 
 73 | 
 74 | def sanitize_address(addr, encoding):
 75 |     """
 76 |     Format a pair of (name, address) or an email address string.
 77 |     """
 78 |     address = None
 79 |     if not isinstance(addr, tuple):
 80 |         addr = force_str(addr)
 81 |         try:
 82 |             token, rest = parser.get_mailbox(addr)
 83 |         except (HeaderParseError, ValueError, IndexError):
 84 |             raise ValueError('Invalid address "%s"' % addr)
 85 |         else:
 86 |             if rest:
 87 |                 # The entire email address must be parsed.
 88 |                 raise ValueError(
 89 |                     'Invalid adddress; only %s could be parsed from "%s"'
 90 |                     % (token, addr)
 91 |                 )
 92 |             nm = token.display_name or ''
 93 |             localpart = token.local_part
 94 |             domain = token.domain or ''
 95 |     else:
 96 |         nm, address = addr
 97 |         localpart, domain = address.rsplit('@', 1)
 98 | 
 99 |     nm = Header(nm, encoding).encode()
100 |     # Avoid UTF-8 encode, if it's possible.
101 |     try:
102 |         localpart.encode('ascii')
103 |     except UnicodeEncodeError:
104 |         localpart = Header(localpart, encoding).encode()
105 |     try:
106 |         domain.encode('ascii')
107 |     except UnicodeEncodeError:
108 |         domain = domain.encode('idna').decode('ascii')
109 | 
110 |     parsed_address = Address(nm, username=localpart, domain=domain)
111 |     return str(parsed_address)
112 | 
113 | 
114 | class MIMEMixin:
115 |     def as_string(self, unixfrom=False, linesep='\n'):
116 |         """Return the entire formatted message as a string.
117 |         Optional `unixfrom' when True, means include the Unix From_ envelope
118 |         header.
119 | 
120 |         This overrides the default as_string() implementation to not mangle
121 |         lines that begin with 'From '. See bug #13433 for details.
122 |         """
123 |         fp = StringIO()
124 |         g = generator.Generator(fp, mangle_from_=False)
125 |         g.flatten(self, unixfrom=unixfrom, linesep=linesep)
126 |         return fp.getvalue()
127 | 
128 |     def as_bytes(self, unixfrom=False, linesep='\n'):
129 |         """Return the entire formatted message as bytes.
130 |         Optional `unixfrom' when True, means include the Unix From_ envelope
131 |         header.
132 | 
133 |         This overrides the default as_bytes() implementation to not mangle
134 |         lines that begin with 'From '. See bug #13433 for details.
135 |         """
136 |         fp = BytesIO()
137 |         g = generator.BytesGenerator(fp, mangle_from_=False)
138 |         g.flatten(self, unixfrom=unixfrom, linesep=linesep)
139 |         return fp.getvalue()

154 |         MIMEText.__init__(self, _text, _subtype=_subtype, _charset=_charset)

160 |     def set_payload(self, payload, charset=None):
161 |         if charset == 'utf-8' and not isinstance(charset, Charset.Charset):
162 |             has_long_lines = any(
163 |                 len(l.encode()) > RFC5322_EMAIL_LINE_LENGTH_LIMIT
164 |                 for l in payload.splitlines()
165 |             )
166 |             # Quoted-Printable encoding has the side effect of shortening long
167 |             # lines, if any (#22561).

183 | class EmailMessage:
184 |     """A container for email information."""
185 |     content_subtype = 'plain'
186 |     mixed_subtype = 'mixed'
187 |     encoding = None     # None => use settings default
188 | 
189 |     def __init__(self, subject='', body='', from_email=None, to=None, bcc=None,
190 |                  connection=None, attachments=None, headers=None, cc=None,
191 |                  reply_to=None):
192 |         """
193 |         Initialize a single email message (which can be sent to multiple
194 |         recipients).
195 |         """
196 |         if to:
197 |             if isinstance(to, str):
198 |                 raise TypeError('"to" argument must be a list or tuple')
199 |             self.to = list(to)
200 |         else:
201 |             self.to = []
202 |         if cc:
203 |             if isinstance(cc, str):
204 |                 raise TypeError('"cc" argument must be a list or tuple')
205 |             self.cc = list(cc)
206 |         else:
207 |             self.cc = []
208 |         if bcc:
209 |             if isinstance(bcc, str):
210 |                 raise TypeError('"bcc" argument must be a list or tuple')
211 |             self.bcc = list(bcc)
212 |         else:
213 |             self.bcc = []
214 |         if reply_to:
215 |             if isinstance(reply_to, str):
216 |                 raise TypeError('"reply_to" argument must be a list or tuple')
217 |             self.reply_to = list(reply_to)
218 |         else:
219 |             self.reply_to = []
220 |         self.from_email = from_email or settings.DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL
221 |         self.subject = subject
222 |         self.body = body or ''
223 |         self.attachments = []
224 |         if attachments:
225 |             for attachment in attachments:
226 |                 if isinstance(attachment, MIMEBase):
227 |                     self.attach(attachment)
228 |                 else:
229 |                     self.attach(*attachment)
230 |         self.extra_headers = headers or {}
231 |         self.connection = connection
232 | 
233 |     def get_connection(self, fail_silently=False):
234 |         from django.core.mail import get_connection
235 |         if not self.connection:
236 |             self.connection = get_connection(fail_silently=fail_silently)
237 |         return self.connection
238 | 
239 |     def message(self):
240 |         encoding = self.encoding or settings.DEFAULT_CHARSET
241 |         msg = SafeMIMEText(self.body, self.content_subtype, encoding)
242 |         msg = self._create_message(msg)
243 |         msg['Subject'] = self.subject
244 |         msg['From'] = self.extra_headers.get('From', self.from_email)
245 |         self._set_list_header_if_not_empty(msg, 'To', self.to)
246 |         self._set_list_header_if_not_empty(msg, 'Cc', self.cc)
247 |         self._set_list_header_if_not_empty(msg, 'Reply-To', self.reply_to)
248 | 
249 |         # Email header names are case-insensitive (RFC 2045), so we have to
250 |         # accommodate that when doing comparisons.
251 |         header_names = [key.lower() for key in self.extra_headers]
252 |         if 'date' not in header_names:
253 |             # formatdate() uses stdlib methods to format the date, which use
254 |             # the stdlib/OS concept of a timezone, however, Django sets the
255 |             # TZ environment variable based on the TIME_ZONE setting which
256 |             # will get picked up by formatdate().
257 |             msg['Date'] = formatdate(localtime=settings.EMAIL_USE_LOCALTIME)
258 |         if 'message-id' not in header_names:
259 |             # Use cached DNS_NAME for performance
260 |             msg['Message-ID'] = make_msgid(domain=DNS_NAME)
261 |         for name, value in self.extra_headers.items():
262 |             if name.lower() != 'from':  # From is already handled
263 |                 msg[name] = value
264 |         return msg
265 | 
266 |     def recipients(self):
267 |         """
268 |         Return a list of all recipients of the email (includes direct
269 |         addressees as well as Cc and Bcc entries).
270 |         """
271 |         return [email for email in (self.to + self.cc + self.bcc) if email]
272 | 
273 |     def send(self, fail_silently=False):
274 |         """Send the email message."""
275 |         if not self.recipients():
276 |             # Don't bother creating the network connection if there's nobody to
277 |             # send to.
278 |             return 0
279 |         return self.get_connection(fail_silently).send_messages([self])
280 | 
281 |     def attach(self, filename=None, content=None, mimetype=None):
282 |         """
283 |         Attach a file with the given filename and content. The filename can
284 |         be omitted and the mimetype is guessed, if not provided.
285 | 
286 |         If the first parameter is a MIMEBase subclass, insert it directly
287 |         into the resulting message attachments.
288 | 
289 |         For a text/* mimetype (guessed or specified), when a bytes object is
290 |         specified as content, decode it as UTF-8. If that fails, set the
291 |         mimetype to DEFAULT_ATTACHMENT_MIME_TYPE and don't decode the content.
292 |         """
293 |         if isinstance(filename, MIMEBase):
294 |             assert content is None
295 |             assert mimetype is None
296 |             self.attachments.append(filename)
297 |         else:
298 |             assert content is not None
299 |             mimetype = mimetype or mimetypes.guess_type(filename)[0] or DEFAULT_ATTACHMENT_MIME_TYPE
300 |             basetype, subtype = mimetype.split('/', 1)
301 | 
302 |             if basetype == 'text':
303 |                 if isinstance(content, bytes):
304 |                     try:
305 |                         content = content.decode()
306 |                     except UnicodeDecodeError:
307 |                         # If mimetype suggests the file is text but it's
308 |                         # actually binary, read() raises a UnicodeDecodeError.
309 |                         mimetype = DEFAULT_ATTACHMENT_MIME_TYPE
310 | 
311 |             self.attachments.append((filename, content, mimetype))
312 | 
313 |     def attach_file(self, path, mimetype=None):
314 |         """
315 |         Attach a file from the filesystem.
316 | 
317 |         Set the mimetype to DEFAULT_ATTACHMENT_MIME_TYPE if it isn't specified
318 |         and cannot be guessed.
319 | 
320 |         For a text/* mimetype (guessed or specified), decode the file's content
321 |         as UTF-8. If that fails, set the mimetype to
322 |         DEFAULT_ATTACHMENT_MIME_TYPE and don't decode the content.
323 |         """
324 |         path = Path(path)
325 |         with path.open('rb') as file:
326 |             content = file.read()
327 |             self.attach(path.name, content, mimetype)
328 | 
329 |     def _create_message(self, msg):
330 |         return self._create_attachments(msg)
331 | 
332 |     def _create_attachments(self, msg):
333 |         if self.attachments:
334 |             encoding = self.encoding or settings.DEFAULT_CHARSET
335 |             body_msg = msg
336 |             msg = SafeMIMEMultipart(_subtype=self.mixed_subtype, encoding=encoding)
337 |             if self.body or body_msg.is_multipart():
338 |                 msg.attach(body_msg)
339 |             for attachment in self.attachments:
340 |                 if isinstance(attachment, MIMEBase):
341 |                     msg.attach(attachment)
342 |                 else:
343 |                     msg.attach(self._create_attachment(*attachment))
344 |         return msg
345 | 
346 |     def _create_mime_attachment(self, content, mimetype):
347 |         """
348 |         Convert the content, mimetype pair into a MIME attachment object.
349 | 
350 |         If the mimetype is message/rfc822, content may be an
351 |         email.Message or EmailMessage object, as well as a str.
352 |         """
353 |         basetype, subtype = mimetype.split('/', 1)
354 |         if basetype == 'text':
355 |             encoding = self.encoding or settings.DEFAULT_CHARSET
356 |             attachment = SafeMIMEText(content, subtype, encoding)
357 |         elif basetype == 'message' and subtype == 'rfc822':
358 |             # Bug #18967: per RFC2046 s5.2.1, message/rfc822 attachments
359 |             # must not be base64 encoded.
360 |             if isinstance(content, EmailMessage):
361 |                 # convert content into an email.Message first
362 |                 content = content.message()
363 |             elif not isinstance(content, Message):
364 |                 # For compatibility with existing code, parse the message
365 |                 # into an email.Message object if it is not one already.
366 |                 content = message_from_string(force_str(content))
367 | 
368 |             attachment = SafeMIMEMessage(content, subtype)
369 |         else:
370 |             # Encode non-text attachments with base64.
371 |             attachment = MIMEBase(basetype, subtype)
372 |             attachment.set_payload(content)
373 |             Encoders.encode_base64(attachment)
374 |         return attachment
375 | 
376 |     def _create_attachment(self, filename, content, mimetype=None):
377 |         """
378 |         Convert the filename, content, mimetype triple into a MIME attachment
379 |         object.
380 |         """
381 |         attachment = self._create_mime_attachment(content, mimetype)
382 |         if filename:
383 |             try:
384 |                 filename.encode('ascii')
385 |             except UnicodeEncodeError:
386 |                 filename = ('utf-8', '', filename)
387 |             attachment.add_header('Content-Disposition', 'attachment', filename=filename)
388 |         return attachment
389 | 
390 |     def _set_list_header_if_not_empty(self, msg, header, values):
391 |         """
392 |         Set msg's header, either from self.extra_headers, if present, or from
393 |         the values argument.
394 |         """
395 |         if values:
396 |             try:
397 |                 value = self.extra_headers[header]
398 |             except KeyError:
399 |                 value = ', '.join(str(v) for v in values)
400 |             msg[header] = value

  => DIFF (50.0s) awaiting Docker eval

[54/500] django__django-11551

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

admin.E108 is raised on fields accessible only via instance.
Description
	 
		(last modified by ajcsimons)
	 
As part of startup django validates the ModelAdmin's list_display list/tuple for correctness (django.admin.contrib.checks._check_list_display). Having upgraded django from 2.07 to 2.2.1 I found that a ModelAdmin with a list display that used to pass the checks and work fine in admin now fails validation, preventing django from starting. A PositionField from the django-positions library triggers this bug, explanation why follows.
from django.db import models
from position.Fields import PositionField
class Thing(models.Model)
 number = models.IntegerField(default=0)
 order = PositionField()
from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Thing
@admin.register(Thing)
class ThingAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin)
 list_display = ['number', 'order']
Under 2.2.1 this raises an incorrect admin.E108 message saying "The value of list_display[1] refers to 'order' which is not a callable...".
Under 2.0.7 django starts up successfully.
If you change 'number' to 'no_number' or 'order' to 'no_order' then the validation correctly complains about those.
The reason for this bug is commit ​https://github.com/django/django/commit/47016adbf54b54143d4cf052eeb29fc72d27e6b1 which was proposed and accepted as a fix for bug https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/28490. The problem is while it fixed that bug it broke the functionality of _check_list_display_item in other cases. The rationale for that change was that after field=getattr(model, item) field could be None if item was a descriptor returning None, but subsequent code incorrectly interpreted field being None as meaning getattr raised an AttributeError. As this was done after trying field = model._meta.get_field(item) and that failing that meant the validation error should be returned. However, after the above change if hasattr(model, item) is false then we no longer even try field = model._meta.get_field(item) before returning an error. The reason hasattr(model, item) is false in the case of a PositionField is its get method throws an exception if called on an instance of the PositionField class on the Thing model class, rather than a Thing instance.
For clarity, here are the various logical tests that _check_list_display_item needs to deal with and the behaviour before the above change, after it, and the correct behaviour (which my suggested patch exhibits). Note this is assuming the first 2 tests callable(item) and hasattr(obj, item) are both false (corresponding to item is an actual function/lambda rather than string or an attribute of ThingAdmin).
hasattr(model, item) returns True or False (which is the same as seeing if getattr(model, item) raises AttributeError)
model._meta.get_field(item) returns a field or raises FieldDoesNotExist
Get a field from somewhere, could either be from getattr(model,item) if hasattr was True or from get_field.
Is that field an instance of ManyToManyField?
Is that field None? (True in case of bug 28490)
 hasattr get_field field is None? field ManyToMany? 2.0 returns 2.2 returns Correct behaviour Comments 
 True ok False False [] [] [] - 
 True ok False True E109 E109 E109 - 
 True ok True False E108 [] [] good bit of 28490 fix, 2.0 was wrong 
 True raises False False [] [] [] - 
 True raises False True E109 [] E109 Another bug introduced by 28490 fix, fails to check if ManyToMany in get_field raise case 
 True raises True False E108 [] [] good bit of 28490 fix, 2.0 was wrong 
 False ok False False [] E108 [] bad bit of 28490 fix, bug hit with PositionField 
 False ok False True [] E108 E109 both 2.0 and 2.2 wrong 
 False ok True False [] E108 [] bad 28490 fix 
 False raises False False E108 E108 E108 - 
 False raises False True E108 E108 E108 impossible condition, we got no field assigned to be a ManyToMany 
 False raises True False E108 E108 E108 impossible condition, we got no field assigned to be None 
The following code exhibits the correct behaviour in all cases. The key changes are there is no longer a check for hasattr(model, item), as that being false should not prevent us form attempting to get the field via get_field, and only return an E108 in the case both of them fail. If either of those means or procuring it are successful then we need to check if it's a ManyToMany. Whether or not the field is None is irrelevant, and behaviour is contained within the exception catching blocks that should cause it instead of signalled through a variable being set to None which is a source of conflation of different cases.
def _check_list_display_item(self, obj, item, label):
	if callable(item):
		return []
	elif hasattr(obj, item):
		return []
	else:
		try:
			field = obj.model._meta.get_field(item)
		except FieldDoesNotExist:
			try:
				field = getattr(obj.model, item)
			except AttributeError:
				return [
					checks.Error(
						"The value of '%s' refers to '%s', which is not a callable, "
						"an attribute of '%s', or an attribute or method on '%s.%s'." % (
							label, item, obj.__class__.__name__,
							obj.model._meta.app_label, obj.model._meta.object_name,
						),
						obj=obj.__class__,
						id='admin.E108',
					)
				]
		if isinstance(field, models.ManyToManyField):
			return [
				checks.Error(
					"The value of '%s' must not be a ManyToManyField." % label,
					obj=obj.__class__,
					id='admin.E109',
				)
			]
		return []



Hints:
fix
Fix is quite simple but a regression test can be tricky.
Hi felixxm, I also just made a ticket #30545 with more details. Working through all the logical combinations I think both the old code and new code have other bugs and I've posted a suggested fix there.
Updated description with detail from #30545
I think there is a potential simplification of my solution: if get_field raises the FieldDoesNotExist exception then I suspect getattr(obj.model, item) might always fail too (so that test could be omitted and go straight to returning the E108 error), but I'm not sure because the inner workings of get_field are rather complicated.
My proposed changes as pull request: ​PR

## Issue

order_by() a parent model crash when Meta.ordering contains expressions.
Description
	 
		(last modified by Jonny Fuller)
	 
Hi friends,
During testing I discovered a strange bug when using a query expression for ordering during multi-table inheritance. You can find the full write up as well as reproducible test repository ​https://github.com/JonnyWaffles/djangoordermetabug. The bug occurs because the field is an OrderBy object, not a string, during get_order_dir. The linked stacktrace should make the issue obvious, but what I don't understand is why it only fails during test db setup, not during repl or script use. I wish I could help more and come up with a real solution. Hopefully, this is enough for someone wiser to find the culprit.



Hints:
Thanks for the report. I attached a regression test. Reproduced at c498f088c584ec3aff97409fdc11b39b28240de9.
Regression test.

Yes that's the same issue, thanks for another scenario to reproduce it.
​https://github.com/django/django/pull/11489

2273 |     def update_join_types(self, query):
2274 |         """
2275 |         Change join types so that the generated query is as efficient as
2276 |         possible, but still correct. So, change as many joins as possible
2277 |         to INNER, but don't make OUTER joins INNER if that could remove
2278 |         results from the query.
2279 |         """
2280 |         to_promote = set()
2281 |         to_demote = set()
2282 |         # The effective_connector is used so that NOT (a AND b) is treated
2283 |         # similarly to (a OR b) for join promotion.
2284 |         for table, votes in self.votes.items():
2285 |             # We must use outer joins in OR case when the join isn't contained
2286 |             # in all of the joins. Otherwise the INNER JOIN itself could remove
2287 |             # valid results. Consider the case where a model with rel_a and

2289 |             # if rel_a join doesn't produce any results is null (for example
2290 |             # reverse foreign key or null value in direct foreign key), and
2291 |             # there is a matching row in rel_b with col=2, then an INNER join
2292 |             # to rel_a would remove a valid match from the query. So, we need
2293 |             # to promote any existing INNER to LOUTER (it is possible this
2294 |             # promotion in turn will be demoted later on).

2298 |             # when there is a joinable row, then use INNER. For example, in

700 |     def find_ordering_name(self, name, opts, alias=None, default_order='ASC',
701 |                            already_seen=None):
702 |         """
703 |         Return the table alias (the name might be ambiguous, the alias will
704 |         not be) and column name for ordering by the given 'name' parameter.
705 |         The 'name' is of the form 'field1__field2__...__fieldN'.
706 |         """
707 |         name, order = get_order_dir(name, default_order)
708 |         descending = order == 'DESC'
709 |         pieces = name.split(LOOKUP_SEP)
710 |         field, targets, alias, joins, path, opts, transform_function = self._setup_joins(pieces, opts, alias)

712 |         # If we get to this point and the field is a relation to another model,
713 |         # append the default ordering for that model unless the attribute name
714 |         # of the field is specified.
715 |         if field.is_relation and opts.ordering and getattr(field, 'attname', None) != name:
716 |             # Firstly, avoid infinite loops.
717 |             already_seen = already_seen or set()
718 |             join_tuple = tuple(getattr(self.query.alias_map[j], 'join_cols', None) for j in joins)
719 |             if join_tuple in already_seen:
720 |                 raise FieldError('Infinite loop caused by ordering.')
721 |             already_seen.add(join_tuple)
722 | 
723 |             results = []

725 |                 results.extend(self.find_ordering_name(item, opts, alias,
726 |                                                        order, already_seen))

729 |         return [(OrderBy(transform_function(t, alias), descending=descending), False) for t in targets]
730 | 
731 |     def _setup_joins(self, pieces, opts, alias):
732 |         """
733 |         Helper method for get_order_by() and get_distinct().
734 | 
735 |         get_ordering() and get_distinct() must produce same target columns on
736 |         same input, as the prefixes of get_ordering() and get_distinct() must
737 |         match. Executing SQL where this is not true is an error.
738 |         """
739 |         alias = alias or self.query.get_initial_alias()
740 |         field, targets, opts, joins, path, transform_function = self.query.setup_joins(pieces, opts, alias)
741 |         alias = joins[-1]
742 |         return field, targets, alias, joins, path, opts, transform_function

744 |     def get_from_clause(self):
745 |         """
746 |         Return a list of strings that are joined together to go after the
747 |         "FROM" part of the query, as well as a list any extra parameters that
748 |         need to be included. Subclasses, can override this to create a
749 |         from-clause via a "select".
750 | 
751 |         This should only be called after any SQL construction methods that
752 |         might change the tables that are needed. This means the select columns,
753 |         ordering, and distinct must be done first.
754 |         """
755 |         result = []
756 |         params = []
757 |         for alias in tuple(self.query.alias_map):
758 |             if not self.query.alias_refcount[alias]:
759 |                 continue
760 |             try:
761 |                 from_clause = self.query.alias_map[alias]
762 |             except KeyError:
763 |                 # Extra tables can end up in self.tables, but not in the
764 |                 # alias_map if they aren't in a join. That's OK. We skip them.
765 |                 continue
766 |             clause_sql, clause_params = self.compile(from_clause)
767 |             result.append(clause_sql)
768 |             params.extend(clause_params)
769 |         for t in self.query.extra_tables:
770 |             alias, _ = self.query.table_alias(t)
771 |             # Only add the alias if it's not already present (the table_alias()
772 |             # call increments the refcount, so an alias refcount of one means
773 |             # this is the only reference).
774 |             if alias not in self.query.alias_map or self.query.alias_refcount[alias] == 1:
775 |                 result.append(', %s' % self.quote_name_unless_alias(alias))
776 |         return result, params

## Issue

Add DISTINCT support for Avg and Sum aggregates.
Description
	
As an extension of #28658, aggregates should be supported for other general aggregates such as Avg and Sum. Before 2.2, these aggregations just ignored the parameter, but now throw an exception.
This change would just involve setting these classes as allowing DISTINCT, and could also be applied to Min and Max (although pointless).



Hints:
​PR

[57/500] django__django-11728

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

simplify_regexp() doesn't replace trailing groups.
Description
	
replace_named_groups() fails to replace the final named group if the urlpattern passed in is missing a trailing '/'.
For example, with input r'entries/(?P<pk>[^/.]+)/relationships/(?P<related_field>\w+)' the "related_field" does not get properly replaced. A workaround is to tack on a '/' at the end and then it works.
Code that reproduces this is attached. 
This function is used downstream in Django REST Framework. See issue ​6888



Hints:
Here's execution of the example code: (env) django-example$ python demo.py path: entries/(?P<pk>[^/.]+)/relationships/(?P<related_field>\w+) expected: entries/<pk>/relationships/<related_field> got: entries/<pk>/relationships/(?P<related_field>\w+) path_trailing: entries/(?P<pk>[^/.]+)/relationships/(?P<related_field>\w+)/ expected: entries/<pk>/relationships/<related_field>/ got: entries/<pk>/relationships/<related_field>/ Traceback (most recent call last): File "demo.py", line 21, in <module> assert path == expected_path, "path without trailing slash didn't match expected" AssertionError: path without trailing slash didn't match expected (env) django-example$
Thanks for the ticket, trailing slash is not necessary regexp patterns could be also enclosed by $, by I agree that this could be easily fix by: diff --git a/django/contrib/admindocs/utils.py b/django/contrib/admindocs/utils.py index 1ce4594501..db27f82deb 100644 --- a/django/contrib/admindocs/utils.py +++ b/django/contrib/admindocs/utils.py @@ -167,12 +167,6 @@ def replace_named_groups(pattern): # Handle nested parentheses, e.g. '^(?P<a>(x|y))/b'. unmatched_open_brackets, prev_char = 1, None for idx, val in enumerate(pattern[end:]): - # If brackets are balanced, the end of the string for the current - # named capture group pattern has been reached. - if unmatched_open_brackets == 0: - group_pattern_and_name.append((pattern[start:end + idx], group_name)) - break - # Check for unescaped `(` and `)`.  26 | def trim_docstring(docstring):
 27 |     """
 28 |     Uniformly trim leading/trailing whitespace from docstrings.
 29 | 
 30 |     Based on https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0257/#handling-docstring-indentation
 31 |     """
 32 |     if not docstring or not docstring.strip():
 33 |         return ''
 34 |     # Convert tabs to spaces and split into lines
 35 |     lines = docstring.expandtabs().splitlines()
 36 |     indent = min(len(line) - len(line.lstrip()) for line in lines if line.lstrip())
 37 |     trimmed = [lines[0].lstrip()] + [line[indent:].rstrip() for line in lines[1:]]
 38 |     return "\n".join(trimmed).strip()
 39 | 
 40 | 
 41 | def parse_docstring(docstring):
 42 |     """
 43 |     Parse out the parts of a docstring.  Return (title, body, metadata).
 44 |     """
 45 |     docstring = trim_docstring(docstring)
 46 |     parts = re.split(r'\n{2,}', docstring)
 47 |     title = parts[0]
 48 |     if len(parts) == 1:
 49 |         body = ''
 50 |         metadata = {}
 51 |     else:
 52 |         parser = HeaderParser()
 53 |         try:
 54 |             metadata = parser.parsestr(parts[-1])
 55 |         except HeaderParseError:
 56 |             metadata = {}
 57 |             body = "\n\n".join(parts[1:])
 58 |         else:
 59 |             metadata = dict(metadata.items())
 60 |             if metadata:
 61 |                 body = "\n\n".join(parts[1:-1])
 62 |             else:
 63 |                 body = "\n\n".join(parts[1:])
 64 |     return title, body, metadata

148 | # Match the beginning of a named or unnamed group.
149 | named_group_matcher = re.compile(r'\(\?P(<\w+>)')
150 | unnamed_group_matcher = re.compile(r'\(')
151 | 
152 | 
153 | def replace_named_groups(pattern):
154 |     r"""
155 |     Find named groups in `pattern` and replace them with the group name. E.g.,
156 |     1. ^(?P<a>\w+)/b/(\w+)$ ==> ^<a>/b/(\w+)$
157 |     2. ^(?P<a>\w+)/b/(?P<c>\w+)/$ ==> ^<a>/b/<c>/$
158 |     """
159 |     named_group_indices = [
160 |         (m.start(0), m.end(0), m.group(1))
161 |         for m in named_group_matcher.finditer(pattern)
162 |     ]
163 |     # Tuples of (named capture group pattern, group name).
164 |     group_pattern_and_name = []
165 |     # Loop over the groups and their start and end indices.
166 |     for start, end, group_name in named_group_indices:
167 |         # Handle nested parentheses, e.g. '^(?P<a>(x|y))/b'.
168 |         unmatched_open_brackets, prev_char = 1, None
169 |         for idx, val in enumerate(pattern[end:]):
170 |             # If brackets are balanced, the end of the string for the current
171 |             # named capture group pattern has been reached.
172 |             if unmatched_open_brackets == 0:
173 |                 group_pattern_and_name.append((pattern[start:end + idx], group_name))
174 |                 break
175 | 
176 |             # Check for unescaped `(` and `)`. They mark the start and end of a
177 |             # nested group.
178 |             if val == '(' and prev_char != '\\':
179 |                 unmatched_open_brackets += 1
180 |             elif val == ')' and prev_char != '\\':
181 |                 unmatched_open_brackets -= 1
182 |             prev_char = val
183 | 
184 |     # Replace the string for named capture groups with their group names.
185 |     for group_pattern, group_name in group_pattern_and_name:
186 |         pattern = pattern.replace(group_pattern, group_name)
187 |     return pattern
188 | 
189 | 
190 | def replace_unnamed_groups(pattern):
191 |     r"""
192 |     Find unnamed groups in `pattern` and replace them with '<var>'. E.g.,
193 |     1. ^(?P<a>\w+)/b/(\w+)$ ==> ^(?P<a>\w+)/b/<var>$
194 |     2. ^(?P<a>\w+)/b/((x|y)\w+)$ ==> ^(?P<a>\w+)/b/<var>$
195 |     """
196 |     unnamed_group_indices = [m.start(0) for m in unnamed_group_matcher.finditer(pattern)]
197 |     # Indices of the start of unnamed capture groups.
198 |     group_indices = []
199 |     # Loop over the start indices of the groups.
200 |     for start in unnamed_group_indices:
201 |         # Handle nested parentheses, e.g. '^b/((x|y)\w+)$'.
202 |         unmatched_open_brackets, prev_char = 1, None
203 |         for idx, val in enumerate(pattern[start + 1:]):
204 |             if unmatched_open_brackets == 0:
205 |                 group_indices.append((start, start + 1 + idx))
206 |                 break
207 | 
208 |             # Check for unescaped `(` and `)`. They mark the start and end of
209 |             # a nested group.
210 |             if val == '(' and prev_char != '\\':
211 |                 unmatched_open_brackets += 1
212 |             elif val == ')' and prev_char != '\\':
213 |                 unmatched_open_brackets -= 1
214 |             prev_char = val
215 | 
216 |     # Remove unnamed group matches inside other unnamed capture groups.
217 |     group_start_end_indices = []
218 |     prev_end = None
219 |     for start, end in group_indices:
220 |         if prev_end and start > prev_end or not prev_end:
221 |             group_start_end_indices.append((start, end))
222 |         prev_end = end
223 | 
224 |     if group_start_end_indices:
225 |         # Replace unnamed groups with <var>. Handle the fact that replacing the
226 |         # string between indices will change string length and thus indices
227 |         # will point to the wrong substring if not corrected.
228 |         final_pattern, prev_end = [], None
229 |         for start, end in group_start_end_indices:
230 |             if prev_end:
231 |                 final_pattern.append(pattern[prev_end:start])
232 |             final_pattern.append(pattern[:start] + '<var>')
233 |             prev_end = end
234 |         final_pattern.append(pattern[prev_end:])
235 |         return ''.join(final_pattern)
236 |     else:
237 |         return pattern

--- Step 3 ---
🔧 Thought: Reasoning: Looking at the `replace_named_groups` function (lines 153-187), I can see the bug:

The function iterates through `pattern[end:]` starting from the position after the `(?P<name>` prefix. It tracks unmatched open brackets to handle nested groups.

The problem is in the loop at lines 169-182. The check for `unmatched_open_brackets == 0` happens BEFORE processing the current character. This means:
1. When we reach the closing `)` of the last group, `unmatched_open_brackets` becomes 0
2. But then the loop continues to the next iteration
3. In the next iteration, the check `if unmatched_open_brackets == 0` triggers, but we've already moved past the closing `)`
4. So we append `pattern[start:end + idx]` which doesn't include the closing `)`

The fix suggested in the issue is to move the check for `unmatched_open_brackets == 0` to AFTER we've processed the character that made it 0. This way, when we encounter the closing `)` that balances the brackets, we include it in the group pattern.

The same issue exists in `replace_unnamed_groups` function (lines 190-237).

Let me also check the test file to understand the expected test format.

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

OuterRef in exclude() or ~Q() uses wrong model.
Description
	
The following test (added to tests/queries/test_qs_combinators) fails when trying to exclude results using OuterRef()
def test_exists_exclude(self):
	# filter()
	qs = Number.objects.annotate(
		foo=Exists(
			Item.objects.filter(tags__category_id=OuterRef('pk'))
		)
	).filter(foo=True)
	print(qs) # works
	# exclude()
	qs = Number.objects.annotate(
		foo =Exists(
			Item.objects.exclude(tags__category_id=OuterRef('pk'))
		)
	).filter(foo=True)
	print(qs) # crashes
	# filter(~Q())
	qs = Number.objects.annotate(
		foo =Exists(
			Item.objects.filter(~Q(tags__category_id=OuterRef('pk')))
		)
	).filter(foo=True)
	print(qs) # crashes
It results in the following error
ValueError: This queryset contains a reference to an outer query and may only be used in a subquery



Hints:
Thanks for the report. ValueError was fixed in 35431298226165986ad07e91f9d3aca721ff38ec. Provided test fails on master with django.db.utils.ProgrammingError: missing FROM-clause entry for table "V0" LINE 1: ...(U1."tag_id" = U2."id") WHERE U2."category_id" = ("V0"."id")... It looks that OuterRef resolves to "V0"."id" instead of "queries_number"."id" SELECT ... FROM "queries_number" WHERE EXISTS( SELECT ... FROM "queries_item" V0 WHERE NOT (V0."id" IN ( SELECT U1."item_id" FROM "queries_item_tags" U1 INNER JOIN "queries_tag" U2 ON (U1."tag_id" = U2."id") WHERE U2."category_id" = "V0"."id" )) Issue can be reproduce also without annotation Number.objects.filter(Exists(Item.objects.exclude(tags__category_id=OuterRef('pk'))))

## Issue

Change uuid field to FK does not create dependency
Description
	 
		(last modified by Simon Charette)
	 
Hi! I am new in django community, so please help me, because i really dont know is it really "bug".
I have a django project named "testproject" and two apps: testapp1, testapp2.
It will be simpler to understand, with this example:
# TestApp1(models.py):
class App1(models.Model):
	id = models.UUIDField(primary_key=True, unique=True, default=uuid.uuid4, editable=False, verbose_name=_('identifier'))
	text = models.CharField(max_length=100, verbose_name=_('text'))
	another_app = models.UUIDField(null=True, blank=True, verbose_name=_('another app'))
# TestApp2(models.py):
class App2(models.Model):
	id = models.UUIDField(primary_key=True, unique=True, default=uuid.uuid4, editable=False, verbose_name=_('identifier'))
	text = models.CharField(max_length=100, verbose_name=_('text'))
First model named "App1" has UUID field named "another_app":
 another_app = models.UUIDField(null=True, blank=True, verbose_name=_('another app'))
After some time i change field from UUID to FK, like this: 
another_app = models.ForeignKey(App2, null=True, blank=True, on_delete=models.SET_NULL, verbose_name=_('another app'))
And as result i create new migration, but Migration class was unexpected result, because it does not create any "dependencies" for App2, because of FK.
I think the correct solution will be create dependency for App2.
This project use django version 2.2 and postgresql. Attach archive with sources. Project contains small test, after running him, you will get exception like this: ValueError: Related model 'testapp2.App2' cannot be resolved.
So is it problem in django or maybe i dont understand something ?
Here is my post in django users:
​https://groups.google.com/forum/#!searchin/django-users/Django$20bug$3A$20change$20uuid$20field$20to$20FK$20does$20not$20create$20dependency%7Csort:date/django-users/-h9LZxFomLU/yz-NLi1cDgAJ
Regards, Viktor Lomakin



Hints:
Django project which show the bug
There's a lot of history in here, so it may turn out this is a duplicate, but I'll accept this for now, as there's certainly a bug. makemigrations generats this: dependencies = [ ('testapp1', '0002_app1_another_app'), ] operations = [ migrations.AlterField( model_name='app1', name='another_app', field=models.ForeignKey(blank=True, null=True, on_delete=django.db.models.deletion.SET_NULL, to='testapp2.App2', verbose_name='another app'), ), ] But that gives the ValueError as you say. Adjusting dependencies allows the test to pass: dependencies = [ ('testapp1', '0002_app1_another_app'), ('testapp2', '0001_initial'), ] Thus, AlterField should pick this up. (Note for later: SQLite will require disabling constraint checking for this, ref #30023.)
That's probably a bug in django.db.migrations.autodetector.MigrationAutodetector.generate_altered_fields's ​call to add_operation which doesn't pass any dependencies when new_field.is_relation and not old_field.is_relation. The dependencies can be retrieved by using self._get_dependencies_for_foreign_key(new_field). We want to depend on the latest migration of the referenced app instead of the initial one though as the model we're referencing might have been altered since then but that's something the aforementioned method should handle. Tests should be added to django/tests/migrations/test_autodetector.py.
Hello Dart, Are you still planing to work on this? Do you need any assistance submitting a patch?
I'm pretty sure your db change interfere with migration best practice. You are changing field type, which have to be done in two steps. And your migrations are not telling django what should be done with data in uuid field contains when migrate.
Replying to Simon Charette: Hello Dart, Are you still planing to work on this? Do you need any assistance submitting a patch? Yes i'm work with this patch and planning to send this. Yes, it will be good, if you help me.
Replying to Simon Charette: Hello Dart, Are you still planing to work on this? Do you need any assistance submitting a patch? Hi! Today i will send a patch. I found a problem and resolve it. Regards
