163 | class ManagementUtility:
164 |     """
165 |     Encapsulate the logic of the django-admin and manage.py utilities.
166 |     """
167 |     def __init__(self, argv=None):
168 |         self.argv = argv or sys.argv[:]
169 |         self.prog_name = os.path.basename(self.argv[0])
170 |         if self.prog_name == '__main__.py':
171 |             self.prog_name = 'python -m django'
172 |         self.settings_exception = None
173 | 
174 |     def main_help_text(self, commands_only=False):
175 |         """Return the script's main help text, as a string."""
176 |         if commands_only:
177 |             usage = sorted(get_commands())
178 |         else:
179 |             usage = [
180 |                 "",
181 |                 "Type '%s help <subcommand>' for help on a specific subcommand." % self.prog_name,
182 |                 "",
183 |                 "Available subcommands:",
184 |             ]
185 |             commands_dict = defaultdict(lambda: [])
186 |             for name, app in get_commands().items():
187 |                 if app == 'django.core':
188 |                     app = 'django'
189 |                 else:
190 |                     app = app.rpartition('.')[-1]
191 |                 commands_dict[app].append(name)
192 |             style = color_style()
193 |             for app in sorted(commands_dict):
194 |                 usage.append("")
195 |                 usage.append(style.NOTICE("[%s]" % app))
196 |                 for name in sorted(commands_dict[app]):
197 |                     usage.append("    %s" % name)
198 |             # Output an extra note if settings are not properly configured
199 |             if self.settings_exception is not None:
200 |                 usage.append(style.NOTICE(
201 |                     "Note that only Django core commands are listed "
202 |                     "as settings are not properly configured (error: %s)."
203 |                     % self.settings_exception))
204 | 
205 |         return '\n'.join(usage)
206 | 
207 |     def fetch_command(self, subcommand):
208 |         """
209 |         Try to fetch the given subcommand, printing a message with the
210 |         appropriate command called from the command line (usually
211 |         "django-admin" or "manage.py") if it can't be found.
212 |         """
213 |         # Get commands outside of try block to prevent swallowing exceptions
214 |         commands = get_commands()
215 |         try:
216 |             app_name = commands[subcommand]
217 |         except KeyError:
218 |             if os.environ.get('DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE'):
219 |                 # If `subcommand` is missing due to misconfigured settings, the
220 |                 # following line will retrigger an ImproperlyConfigured exception
221 |                 # (get_commands() swallows the original one) so the user is
222 |                 # informed about it.
223 |                 settings.INSTALLED_APPS
224 |             else:
225 |                 sys.stderr.write("No Django settings specified.\n")
226 |             possible_matches = get_close_matches(subcommand, commands)
227 |             sys.stderr.write('Unknown command: %r' % subcommand)
228 |             if possible_matches:
229 |                 sys.stderr.write('. Did you mean %s?' % possible_matches[0])
230 |             sys.stderr.write("\nType '%s help' for usage.\n" % self.prog_name)
231 |             sys.exit(1)
232 |         if isinstance(app_name, BaseCommand):
233 |             # If the command is already loaded, use it directly.
234 |             klass = app_name
235 |         else:
236 |             klass = load_command_class(app_name, subcommand)
237 |         return klass
238 | 
239 |     def autocomplete(self):
240 |         """
241 |         Output completion suggestions for BASH.
242 | 
243 |         The output of this function is passed to BASH's `COMREPLY` variable and
244 |         treated as completion suggestions. `COMREPLY` expects a space
245 |         separated string as the result.
246 | 
247 |         The `COMP_WORDS` and `COMP_CWORD` BASH environment variables are used
248 |         to get information about the cli input. Please refer to the BASH
249 |         man-page for more information about this variables.
250 | 
251 |         Subcommand options are saved as pairs. A pair consists of
252 |         the long option string (e.g. '--exclude') and a boolean
253 |         value indicating if the option requires arguments. When printing to
254 |         stdout, an equal sign is appended to options which require arguments.
255 | 
256 |         Note: If debugging this function, it is recommended to write the debug
257 |         output in a separate file. Otherwise the debug output will be treated
258 |         and formatted as potential completion suggestions.
259 |         """
260 |         # Don't complete if user hasn't sourced bash_completion file.
261 |         if 'DJANGO_AUTO_COMPLETE' not in os.environ:
262 |             return
263 | 
264 |         cwords = os.environ['COMP_WORDS'].split()[1:]
265 |         cword = int(os.environ['COMP_CWORD'])
266 | 
267 |         try:
268 |             curr = cwords[cword - 1]
269 |         except IndexError:
270 |             curr = ''
271 | 
272 |         subcommands = [*get_commands(), 'help']
273 |         options = [('--help', False)]
274 | 
275 |         # subcommand
276 |         if cword == 1:
277 |             print(' '.join(sorted(filter(lambda x: x.startswith(curr), subcommands))))
278 |         # subcommand options
279 |         # special case: the 'help' subcommand has no options
280 |         elif cwords[0] in subcommands and cwords[0] != 'help':
281 |             subcommand_cls = self.fetch_command(cwords[0])
282 |             # special case: add the names of installed apps to options
283 |             if cwords[0] in ('dumpdata', 'sqlmigrate', 'sqlsequencereset', 'test'):
284 |                 try:
285 |                     app_configs = apps.get_app_configs()
286 |                     # Get the last part of the dotted path as the app name.
287 |                     options.extend((app_config.label, 0) for app_config in app_configs)
288 |                 except ImportError:
289 |                     # Fail silently if DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE isn't set. The
290 |                     # user will find out once they execute the command.
291 |                     pass
292 |             parser = subcommand_cls.create_parser('', cwords[0])
293 |             options.extend(
294 |                 (min(s_opt.option_strings), s_opt.nargs != 0)
295 |                 for s_opt in parser._actions if s_opt.option_strings
296 |             )
297 |             # filter out previously specified options from available options
298 |             prev_opts = {x.split('=')[0] for x in cwords[1:cword - 1]}
299 |             options = (opt for opt in options if opt[0] not in prev_opts)
300 | 
301 |             # filter options by current input
302 |             options = sorted((k, v) for k, v in options if k.startswith(curr))
303 |             for opt_label, require_arg in options:
304 |                 # append '=' to options which require args
305 |                 if require_arg:
306 |                     opt_label += '='
307 |                 print(opt_label)
308 |         # Exit code of the bash completion function is never passed back to
309 |         # the user, so it's safe to always exit with 0.
310 |         # For more details see #25420.
311 |         sys.exit(0)
312 | 
313 |     def execute(self):
314 |         """
315 |         Given the command-line arguments, figure out which subcommand is being
316 |         run, create a parser appropriate to that command, and run it.
317 |         """
318 |         try:
319 |             subcommand = self.argv[1]
320 |         except IndexError:
321 |             subcommand = 'help'  # Display help if no arguments were given.
322 | 
323 |         # Preprocess options to extract --settings and --pythonpath.
324 |         # These options could affect the commands that are available, so they
325 |         # must be processed early.
326 |         parser = CommandParser(usage='%(prog)s subcommand [options] [args]', add_help=False, allow_abbrev=False)
327 |         parser.add_argument('--settings')
328 |         parser.add_argument('--pythonpath')
329 |         parser.add_argument('args', nargs='*')  # catch-all
330 |         try:
331 |             options, args = parser.parse_known_args(self.argv[2:])
332 |             handle_default_options(options)
333 |         except CommandError:
334 |             pass  # Ignore any option errors at this point.
335 | 
336 |         try:
337 |             settings.INSTALLED_APPS
338 |         except ImproperlyConfigured as exc:
339 |             self.settings_exception = exc
340 |         except ImportError as exc:
341 |             self.settings_exception = exc
342 | 
343 |         if settings.configured:
344 |             # Start the auto-reloading dev server even if the code is broken.
345 |             # The hardcoded condition is a code smell but we can't rely on a
346 |             # flag on the command class because we haven't located it yet.
347 |             if subcommand == 'runserver' and '--noreload' not in self.argv:
348 |                 try:
349 |                     autoreload.check_errors(django.setup)()
350 |                 except Exception:
351 |                     # The exception will be raised later in the child process
352 |                     # started by the autoreloader. Pretend it didn't happen by
353 |                     # loading an empty list of applications.
354 |                     apps.all_models = defaultdict(dict)
355 |                     apps.app_configs = {}
356 |                     apps.apps_ready = apps.models_ready = apps.ready = True
357 | 
358 |                     # Remove options not compatible with the built-in runserver
359 |                     # (e.g. options for the contrib.staticfiles' runserver).
360 |                     # Changes here require manually testing as described in
361 |                     # #27522.
362 |                     _parser = self.fetch_command('runserver').create_parser('django', 'runserver')
363 |                     _options, _args = _parser.parse_known_args(self.argv[2:])
364 |                     for _arg in _args:
365 |                         self.argv.remove(_arg)
366 | 
367 |             # In all other cases, django.setup() is required to succeed.
368 |             else:
369 |                 django.setup()
370 | 
371 |         self.autocomplete()
372 | 
373 |         if subcommand == 'help':
374 |             if '--commands' in args:
375 |                 sys.stdout.write(self.main_help_text(commands_only=True) + '\n')
376 |             elif not options.args:
377 |                 sys.stdout.write(self.main_help_text() + '\n')
378 |             else:
379 |                 self.fetch_command(options.args[0]).print_help(self.prog_name, options.args[0])
380 |         # Special-cases: We want 'django-admin --version' and
381 |         # 'django-admin --help' to work, for backwards compatibility.
382 |         elif subcommand == 'version' or self.argv[1:] == ['--version']:
383 |             sys.stdout.write(django.get_version() + '\n')
384 |         elif self.argv[1:] in (['--help'], ['-h']):
385 |             sys.stdout.write(self.main_help_text() + '\n')
386 |         else:
387 |             self.fetch_command(subcommand).run_from_argv(self.argv)
388 |

390 | def execute_from_command_line(argv=None):
391 |     """Run a ManagementUtility."""
392 |     utility = ManagementUtility(argv)
393 |     utility.execute()

 27 |     def get_context(self, name, value, attrs):
 28 |         context = super().get_context(name, value, attrs)
 29 |         summary = []

198 |     def clean(self):
199 |         username = self.cleaned_data.get('username')
200 |         password = self.cleaned_data.get('password')
201 | 
202 |         if username is not None and password:
203 |             self.user_cache = authenticate(self.request, username=username, password=password)
204 |             if self.user_cache is None:
205 |                 raise self.get_invalid_login_error()
206 |             else:
207 |                 self.confirm_login_allowed(self.user_cache)
208 |

211 |     def confirm_login_allowed(self, user):
212 |         """
213 |         Controls whether the given User may log in. This is a policy setting,
214 |         independent of end-user authentication. This default behavior is to
215 |         allow login by active users, and reject login by inactive users.
216 | 
217 |         If the given user cannot log in, this method should raise a
218 |         ``forms.ValidationError``.
219 | 
220 |         If the given user may log in, this method should return None.
221 |         """
222 |         if not user.is_active:
223 |             raise forms.ValidationError(
224 |                 self.error_messages['inactive'],
225 |                 code='inactive',
226 |             )

236 |         )

246 |     def send_mail(self, subject_template_name, email_template_name,
247 |                   context, from_email, to_email, html_email_template_name=None):
248 |         """
249 |         Send a django.core.mail.EmailMultiAlternatives to `to_email`.
250 |         """
251 |         subject = loader.render_to_string(subject_template_name, context)
252 |         # Email subject *must not* contain newlines
253 |         subject = ''.join(subject.splitlines())
254 |         body = loader.render_to_string(email_template_name, context)
255 | 
256 |         email_message = EmailMultiAlternatives(subject, body, from_email, [to_email])
257 |         if html_email_template_name is not None:

259 |             email_message.attach_alternative(html_email, 'text/html')

263 |     def get_users(self, email):
264 |         """Given an email, return matching user(s) who should receive a reset.
265 | 
266 |         This allows subclasses to more easily customize the default policies
267 |         that prevent inactive users and users with unusable passwords from
268 |         resetting their password.
269 |         """
270 |         active_users = UserModel._default_manager.filter(**{
271 |             '%s__iexact' % UserModel.get_email_field_name(): email,
272 |             'is_active': True,
273 |         })
274 |         return (u for u in active_users if u.has_usable_password())

166 |     def widget_attrs(self, widget):
167 |         """
168 |         Given a Widget instance (*not* a Widget class), return a dictionary of
169 |         any HTML attributes that should be added to the Widget, based on this
170 |         Field.
171 |         """
172 |         return {}
173 | 
174 |     def has_changed(self, initial, data):
175 |         """Return True if data differs from initial."""
176 |         # Always return False if the field is disabled since self.bound_data
177 |         # always uses the initial value in this case.
178 |         if self.disabled:
179 |             return False
180 |         try:
181 |             data = self.to_python(data)
182 |             if hasattr(self, '_coerce'):
183 |                 return self._coerce(data) != self._coerce(initial)
184 |         except ValidationError:
185 |             return True
186 |         # For purposes of seeing whether something has changed, None is
187 |         # the same as an empty string, if the data or initial value we get
188 |         # is None, replace it with ''.
189 |         initial_value = initial if initial is not None else ''
190 |         data_value = data if data is not None else ''
191 |         return initial_value != data_value
192 | 
193 |     def get_bound_field(self, form, field_name):
194 |         """
195 |         Return a BoundField instance that will be used when accessing the form
196 |         field in a template.
197 |         """
198 |         return BoundField(form, self, field_name)
199 | 
200 |     def __deepcopy__(self, memo):
201 |         result = copy.copy(self)
202 |         memo[id(self)] = result
203 |         result.widget = copy.deepcopy(self.widget, memo)
204 |         result.validators = self.validators[:]
205 |         return result
206 |

208 | class CharField(Field):
209 |     def __init__(self, *, max_length=None, min_length=None, strip=True, empty_value='', **kwargs):
210 |         self.max_length = max_length
211 |         self.min_length = min_length
212 |         self.strip = strip
213 |         self.empty_value = empty_value
214 |         super().__init__(**kwargs)
215 |         if min_length is not None:
216 |             self.validators.append(validators.MinLengthValidator(int(min_length)))
217 |         if max_length is not None:
218 |             self.validators.append(validators.MaxLengthValidator(int(max_length)))
219 |         self.validators.append(validators.ProhibitNullCharactersValidator())
220 | 
221 |     def to_python(self, value):
222 |         """Return a string."""
223 |         if value not in self.empty_values:
224 |             value = str(value)
225 |             if self.strip:
226 |                 value = value.strip()
227 |         if value in self.empty_values:
228 |             return self.empty_value
229 |         return value
230 | 
231 |     def widget_attrs(self, widget):
232 |         attrs = super().widget_attrs(widget)
233 |         if self.max_length is not None and not widget.is_hidden:
234 |             # The HTML attribute is maxlength, not max_length.
235 |             attrs['maxlength'] = str(self.max_length)
236 |         if self.min_length is not None and not widget.is_hidden:
237 |             # The HTML attribute is minlength, not min_length.
238 |             attrs['minlength'] = str(self.min_length)
239 |         return attrs

💬 Response: Sorry, I cannot answer this question.
  => NO_DIFF (19.1s) Agent produced no changes

[62/500] django__django-11815

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Migrations uses value of enum object instead of its name.
Description
	 
		(last modified by oasl)
	 
When using Enum object as a default value for a CharField, the generated migration file uses the value of the Enum object instead of the its name. This causes a problem when using Django translation on the value of the Enum object. 
The problem is that, when the Enum object value get translated to the users language, the old migration files raise an error stating that the Enum does not have the corresponding value. (because the Enum value is translated to another language)
Example:
Let say we have this code in models.py:
from enum import Enum
from django.utils.translation import gettext_lazy as _
from django.db import models
class Status(Enum):
	GOOD = _('Good') # 'Good' will be translated
	BAD = _('Bad') # 'Bad' will be translated
	def __str__(self):
		return self.name
class Item(models.Model):
	status = models.CharField(default=Status.GOOD, max_length=128)
In the generated migration file, the code will be:
...
('status', models.CharField(default=Status('Good'), max_length=128))
...
After the translation, 'Good' will be translated to another word and it will not be part of the Status Enum class any more, so the migration file will raise the error on the previous line:
ValueError: 'Good' is not a valid Status
Shouldn't the code generated by the migration uses the name of the Status Enum 'GOOD', not the value of it, since it is changeable?
It should be:
('status', models.CharField(default=Status['GOOD'], max_length=128))
This will be correct regardless of the translated word



Hints:
Thanks for this report, however I'm not sure how translated values can brake migrations. Can you provide a sample project to reproduce this issue? Migrations with translatable strings works fine for me: >>> class TextEnum(enum.Enum): ... C = _('translatable value') ... >>> TextEnum(_('translatable value')) <TextEnum.C: 'translatable value'> >>> TextEnum('translatable value') <TextEnum.C: 'translatable value'>
To experience the bug: In any Django project, set the default value of a CharField as an enum object: class EnumClass(Enum): VALUE = _('Value') where: VALUE: is the constant enum object name 'Value': is the translatable enum object value In the model: field = models.CharField(default=EnumClass.VALUE, max_length=128) then run: python manage.py makemigrations In the generated migration file, you will notice that the default value of the field is set to: EnumClass('Value'), so it calls the enum object by its translatable value not it is constant name. (This is exactly the BUG, you can think of it without even continue) run: python manage.py migrate In the settings.py file: LANGUAGE_CODE = 'fr-FR' # set it to any language code other than English Run the project after generating, translating, and compiling the messages file (see: ​message-files) The project will raise the error: ValueError: 'Value' is not a valid EnumClass , on the generated migration file.
This use case looks quite niche for me, i.e. I would expect to store a unified values (the same for all languages) and translate only labels visible for users, however I agree that we can fix this.
Here is the diff based on the @oasl solution Shouldn't the code generated by the migration uses the name of the Status Enum 'GOOD', not the value of it, since it is changeable? It should be: ('status', models.CharField(default=Status['GOOD'], max_length=128)) diff --git a/django/db/migrations/serializer.py b/django/db/migrations/serializer.py index 27b5cbd379..b00c6f0df2 100644 --- a/django/db/migrations/serializer.py +++ b/django/db/migrations/serializer.py @@ -120,9 +120,9 @@ class EnumSerializer(BaseSerializer): def serialize(self): enum_class = self.value.__class__ module = enum_class.__module__ - v_string, v_imports = serializer_factory(self.value.value).serialize() + _, v_imports = serializer_factory(self.value.value).serialize() imports = {'import %s' % module, *v_imports} - return "%s.%s(%s)" % (module, enum_class.__name__, v_string), imports + return "%s.%s['%s']" % (module, enum_class.__name__, self.value), imports @felixxm, what do you think?
You cannot use a string representation of self.value i.e. 'EnumClass.GOOD', IMO we should use a name property: return "%s.%s[%r]" % (module, enum_class.__name__, self.value.name), imports

280 |     _registry = {

283 |         list: SequenceSerializer,
284 |         set: SetSerializer,

298 |         (COMPILED_REGEX_TYPE, RegexObject): RegexSerializer,
299 |         uuid.UUID: UUIDSerializer,

301 | 
302 |     @classmethod
303 |     def register(cls, type_, serializer):
304 |         if not issubclass(serializer, BaseSerializer):
305 |             raise ValueError("'%s' must inherit from 'BaseSerializer'." % serializer.__name__)
306 |         cls._registry[type_] = serializer
307 | 
308 |     @classmethod
309 |     def unregister(cls, type_):
310 |         cls._registry.pop(type_)

313 | def serializer_factory(value):
314 |     if isinstance(value, Promise):
315 |         value = str(value)
316 |     elif isinstance(value, LazyObject):
317 |         # The unwrapped value is returned as the first item of the arguments
318 |         # tuple.
319 |         value = value.__reduce__()[1][0]
320 | 
321 |     if isinstance(value, models.Field):
322 |         return ModelFieldSerializer(value)
323 |     if isinstance(value, models.manager.BaseManager):
324 |         return ModelManagerSerializer(value)
325 |     if isinstance(value, Operation):
326 |         return OperationSerializer(value)
327 |     if isinstance(value, type):
328 |         return TypeSerializer(value)
329 |     # Anything that knows how to deconstruct itself.
330 |     if hasattr(value, 'deconstruct'):
331 |         return DeconstructableSerializer(value)
332 |     for type_, serializer_cls in Serializer._registry.items():
333 |         if isinstance(value, type_):
334 |             return serializer_cls(value)
335 |     raise ValueError(
336 |         "Cannot serialize: %r\nThere are some values Django cannot serialize into "
337 |         "migration files.\nFor more, see https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/%s/"
338 |         "topics/migrations/#migration-serializing" % (value, get_docs_version())

--- Step 3 ---
🔧 Thought: Reasoning: Looking at the `EnumSerializer` class (lines 119-125), I can see the problem:

```python
class EnumSerializer(BaseSerializer):
    def serialize(self):
        enum_class = self.value.__class__
        module = enum_class.__module__
        v_string, v_imports = serializer_factory(self.value.value).serialize()
        imports = {'import %s' % module, *v_imports}
        return "%s.%s(%s)" % (module, enum_class.__name__, v_string), imports
```

The issue is that it serializes the enum using `self.value.value` (the value of the enum member), which can be a translatable string. When the translation changes, the migration file will try to access `EnumClass('translated_value')` which fails because the enum member was defined with the original value.

The fix suggested in the issue is to use the enum member's name instead of its value. The correct approach is to use `self.value.name` (the enum member name like 'GOOD') and access it via dictionary-style indexing `EnumClass['GOOD']` instead of calling it with the value `EnumClass('Good')`.

According to the hint, we should use:
```python
return "%s.%s[%r]" % (module, enum_class.__name__, self.value.name), imports
```

This will generate code like `Status['GOOD']` which is independent of the translated value.

Let me implement this fix.

--- Step 4 ---
🎬 Tool call: Edit({'path': 'django/db/migrations/serializer.py', 'old_string': 'class EnumSerializer(BaseSerializer):\n    def serialize(self):\n        enum_class = self.value.__class__\n        module = enum_class.__module__\n        v_string, v_imports = serializer_factory(self.value.value).serialize()\n        imports = {\'import %s\' % module, *v_imports}\n        return "%s.%s(%s)" % (module, enum_class.__name__, v_string), imports', 'new_string': 'class EnumSerializer(BaseSerializer):\n    def serialize(self):\n        enum_class = self.value.__class__\n        module = enum_class.__module__\n        imports = {\'import %s\' % module}\n        return "%s.%s[%r]" % (module, enum_class.__name__, self.value.name), imports'})

[64/500] django__django-11848

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

django.utils.http.parse_http_date two digit year check is incorrect
Description
	 
		(last modified by Ad Timmering)
	 
RFC 850 does not mention this, but in RFC 7231 (and there's something similar in RFC 2822), there's the following quote:
Recipients of a timestamp value in rfc850-date format, which uses a
two-digit year, MUST interpret a timestamp that appears to be more
than 50 years in the future as representing the most recent year in
the past that had the same last two digits.
Current logic is hard coded to consider 0-69 to be in 2000-2069, and 70-99 to be 1970-1999, instead of comparing versus the current year.



Hints:
Accepted, however I don't think your patch is correct. The check should be relative to the current year, if I read the RFC quote correctly.
Created a pull request: Created a pull request: ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/9214
Still some suggested edits on the PR.
I added regression test that fails with old code (test_parsing_rfc850_year_69), updated commit message to hopefully follow the guidelines, and added additional comments about the change. Squashed commits as well. Could you review the pull request again?
sent new pull request
This is awaiting for changes from Tim's feedback on PR. (Please uncheck "Patch needs improvement" again when that's done. 🙂)
As this issue hasn't received any updates in the last 8 months, may I work on this ticket?
Go for it, I don't think I will have time to finish it.

103 | def urlencode(query, doseq=False):
104 |     """
105 |     A version of Python's urllib.parse.urlencode() function that can operate on
106 |     MultiValueDict and non-string values.
107 |     """
108 |     if isinstance(query, MultiValueDict):
109 |         query = query.lists()
110 |     elif hasattr(query, 'items'):
111 |         query = query.items()
112 |     query_params = []
113 |     for key, value in query:
114 |         if value is None:
115 |             raise TypeError(
116 |                 "Cannot encode None for key '%s' in a query string. Did you "
117 |                 "mean to pass an empty string or omit the value?" % key
118 |             )
119 |         elif not doseq or isinstance(value, (str, bytes)):
120 |             query_val = value
121 |         else:
122 |             try:
123 |                 itr = iter(value)
124 |             except TypeError:
125 |                 query_val = value
126 |             else:
127 |                 # Consume generators and iterators, when doseq=True, to
128 |                 # work around https://bugs.python.org/issue31706.
129 |                 query_val = []
130 |                 for item in itr:
131 |                     if item is None:
132 |                         raise TypeError(
133 |                             "Cannot encode None for key '%s' in a query "
134 |                             "string. Did you mean to pass an empty string or "
135 |                             "omit the value?" % key
136 |                         )
137 |                     elif not isinstance(item, bytes):
138 |                         item = str(item)
139 |                     query_val.append(item)
140 |         query_params.append((key, query_val))
141 |     return original_urlencode(query_params, doseq)
142 | 
143 | 
144 | def http_date(epoch_seconds=None):
145 |     """
146 |     Format the time to match the RFC1123 date format as specified by HTTP
147 |     RFC7231 section 7.1.1.1.
148 | 
149 |     `epoch_seconds` is a floating point number expressed in seconds since the
150 |     epoch, in UTC - such as that outputted by time.time(). If set to None, it
151 |     defaults to the current time.
152 | 
153 |     Output a string in the format 'Wdy, DD Mon YYYY HH:MM:SS GMT'.
154 |     """
155 |     return formatdate(epoch_seconds, usegmt=True)
156 | 
157 | 
158 | def parse_http_date(date):
159 |     """
160 |     Parse a date format as specified by HTTP RFC7231 section 7.1.1.1.
161 | 
162 |     The three formats allowed by the RFC are accepted, even if only the first
163 |     one is still in widespread use.
164 | 
165 |     Return an integer expressed in seconds since the epoch, in UTC.
166 |     """
167 |     # email.utils.parsedate() does the job for RFC1123 dates; unfortunately
168 |     # RFC7231 makes it mandatory to support RFC850 dates too. So we roll
169 |     # our own RFC-compliant parsing.
170 |     for regex in RFC1123_DATE, RFC850_DATE, ASCTIME_DATE:
171 |         m = regex.match(date)
172 |         if m is not None:
173 |             break
174 |     else:
175 |         raise ValueError("%r is not in a valid HTTP date format" % date)
176 |     try:
177 |         year = int(m.group('year'))
178 |         if year < 100:
179 |             if year < 70:
180 |                 year += 2000
181 |             else:
182 |                 year += 1900
183 |         month = MONTHS.index(m.group('mon').lower()) + 1
184 |         day = int(m.group('day'))
185 |         hour = int(m.group('hour'))
186 |         min = int(m.group('min'))
187 |         sec = int(m.group('sec'))
188 |         result = datetime.datetime(year, month, day, hour, min, sec)
189 |         return calendar.timegm(result.utctimetuple())
190 |     except Exception as exc:
191 |         raise ValueError("%r is not a valid date" % date) from exc
192 | 
193 | 
194 | def parse_http_date_safe(date):
195 |     """
196 |     Same as parse_http_date, but return None if the input is invalid.
197 |     """
198 |     try:
199 |         return parse_http_date(date)
200 |     except Exception:
201 |         pass
202 | 
203 | 
204 | # Base 36 functions: useful for generating compact URLs
205 | 
206 | def base36_to_int(s):
207 |     """
208 |     Convert a base 36 string to an int. Raise ValueError if the input won't fit
209 |     into an int.
210 |     """
211 |     # To prevent overconsumption of server resources, reject any
212 |     # base36 string that is longer than 13 base36 digits (13 digits
213 |     # is sufficient to base36-encode any 64-bit integer)
214 |     if len(s) > 13:
215 |         raise ValueError("Base36 input too large")
216 |     return int(s, 36)
217 | 
218 | 
219 | def int_to_base36(i):
220 |     """Convert an integer to a base36 string."""
221 |     char_set = '0123456789abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'
222 |     if i < 0:
223 |         raise ValueError("Negative base36 conversion input.")
224 |     if i < 36:
225 |         return char_set[i]
226 |     b36 = ''
227 |     while i != 0:
228 |         i, n = divmod(i, 36)
229 |         b36 = char_set[n] + b36
230 |     return b36
231 | 
232 | 
233 | def urlsafe_base64_encode(s):
234 |     """
235 |     Encode a bytestring to a base64 string for use in URLs. Strip any trailing
236 |     equal signs.
237 |     """
238 |     return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(s).rstrip(b'\n=').decode('ascii')
239 | 
240 | 
241 | def urlsafe_base64_decode(s):
242 |     """
243 |     Decode a base64 encoded string. Add back any trailing equal signs that
244 |     might have been stripped.
245 |     """
246 |     s = s.encode()
247 |     try:
248 |         return base64.urlsafe_b64decode(s.ljust(len(s) + len(s) % 4, b'='))
249 |     except (LookupError, BinasciiError) as e:
250 |         raise ValueError(e)
251 | 
252 | 
253 | def parse_etags(etag_str):
254 |     """
255 |     Parse a string of ETags given in an If-None-Match or If-Match header as
256 |     defined by RFC 7232. Return a list of quoted ETags, or ['*'] if all ETags
257 |     should be matched.
258 |     """
259 |     if etag_str.strip() == '*':
260 |         return ['*']
261 |     else:
262 |         # Parse each ETag individually, and return any that are valid.
263 |         etag_matches = (ETAG_MATCH.match(etag.strip()) for etag in etag_str.split(','))
264 |         return [match.group(1) for match in etag_matches if match]
265 | 
266 | 
267 | def quote_etag(etag_str):
268 |     """
269 |     If the provided string is already a quoted ETag, return it. Otherwise, wrap
270 |     the string in quotes, making it a strong ETag.
271 |     """
272 |     if ETAG_MATCH.match(etag_str):
273 |         return etag_str
274 |     else:
275 |         return '"%s"' % etag_str
276 | 
277 | 
278 | def is_same_domain(host, pattern):
279 |     """
280 |     Return ``True`` if the host is either an exact match or a match
281 |     to the wildcard pattern.
282 | 
283 |     Any pattern beginning with a period matches a domain and all of its
284 |     subdomains. (e.g. ``.example.com`` matches ``example.com`` and
285 |     ``foo.example.com``). Anything else is an exact string match.
286 |     """
287 |     if not pattern:
288 |         return False
289 | 
290 |     pattern = pattern.lower()
291 |     return (
292 |         pattern[0] == '.' and (host.endswith(pattern) or host == pattern[1:]) or
293 |         pattern == host
294 |     )
295 | 
296 | 
297 | def url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme(url, allowed_hosts, require_https=False):
298 |     """
299 |     Return ``True`` if the url uses an allowed host and a safe scheme.
300 | 
301 |     Always return ``False`` on an empty url.
302 | 
303 |     If ``require_https`` is ``True``, only 'https' will be considered a valid
304 |     scheme, as opposed to 'http' and 'https' with the default, ``False``.
305 | 
306 |     Note: "True" doesn't entail that a URL is "safe". It may still be e.g.
307 |     quoted incorrectly. Ensure to also use django.utils.encoding.iri_to_uri()
308 |     on the path component of untrusted URLs.
309 |     """
310 |     if url is not None:
311 |         url = url.strip()
312 |     if not url:
313 |         return False
314 |     if allowed_hosts is None:
315 |         allowed_hosts = set()
316 |     elif isinstance(allowed_hosts, str):
317 |         allowed_hosts = {allowed_hosts}
318 |     # Chrome treats \ completely as / in paths but it could be part of some
319 |     # basic auth credentials so we need to check both URLs.
320 |     return (
321 |         _url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme(url, allowed_hosts, require_https=require_https) and
322 |         _url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme(url.replace('\\', '/'), allowed_hosts, require_https=require_https)
323 |     )
324 | 
325 | 
326 | def is_safe_url(url, allowed_hosts, require_https=False):
327 |     warnings.warn(
328 |         'django.utils.http.is_safe_url() is deprecated in favor of '
329 |         'url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme().',
330 |         RemovedInDjango40Warning, stacklevel=2,
331 |     )
332 |     return url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme(url, allowed_hosts, require_https)
333 | 
334 | 
335 | # Copied from urllib.parse.urlparse() but uses fixed urlsplit() function.
336 | def _urlparse(url, scheme='', allow_fragments=True):
337 |     """Parse a URL into 6 components:
338 |     <scheme>://<netloc>/<path>;<params>?<query>#<fragment>
339 |     Return a 6-tuple: (scheme, netloc, path, params, query, fragment).
340 |     Note that we don't break the components up in smaller bits
341 |     (e.g. netloc is a single string) and we don't expand % escapes."""
342 |     url, scheme, _coerce_result = _coerce_args(url, scheme)
343 |     splitresult = _urlsplit(url, scheme, allow_fragments)
344 |     scheme, netloc, url, query, fragment = splitresult
345 |     if scheme in uses_params and ';' in url:
346 |         url, params = _splitparams(url)
347 |     else:
348 |         params = ''
349 |     result = ParseResult(scheme, netloc, url, params, query, fragment)
350 |     return _coerce_result(result)
351 | 
352 | 
353 | # Copied from urllib.parse.urlsplit() with
354 | # https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/661 applied.
355 | def _urlsplit(url, scheme='', allow_fragments=True):
356 |     """Parse a URL into 5 components:
357 |     <scheme>://<netloc>/<path>?<query>#<fragment>
358 |     Return a 5-tuple: (scheme, netloc, path, query, fragment).
359 |     Note that we don't break the components up in smaller bits
360 |     (e.g. netloc is a single string) and we don't expand % escapes."""
361 |     url, scheme, _coerce_result = _coerce_args(url, scheme)
362 |     netloc = query = fragment = ''
363 |     i = url.find(':')
364 |     if i > 0:
365 |         for c in url[:i]:
366 |             if c not in scheme_chars:
367 |                 break
368 |         else:
369 |             scheme, url = url[:i].lower(), url[i + 1:]
370 | 
371 |     if url[:2] == '//':
372 |         netloc, url = _splitnetloc(url, 2)
373 |         if (('[' in netloc and ']' not in netloc) or
374 |                 (']' in netloc and '[' not in netloc)):
375 |             raise ValueError("Invalid IPv6 URL")
376 |     if allow_fragments and '#' in url:
377 |         url, fragment = url.split('#', 1)
378 |     if '?' in url:
379 |         url, query = url.split('?', 1)
380 |     v = SplitResult(scheme, netloc, url, query, fragment)
381 |     return _coerce_result(v)
382 | 
383 | 
384 | def _url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme(url, allowed_hosts, require_https=False):
385 |     # Chrome considers any URL with more than two slashes to be absolute, but
386 |     # urlparse is not so flexible. Treat any url with three slashes as unsafe.
387 |     if url.startswith('///'):
388 |         return False
389 |     try:
390 |         url_info = _urlparse(url)
391 |     except ValueError:  # e.g. invalid IPv6 addresses
392 |         return False
393 |     # Forbid URLs like http:///example.com - with a scheme, but without a hostname.
394 |     # In that URL, example.com is not the hostname but, a path component. However,
395 |     # Chrome will still consider example.com to be the hostname, so we must not
396 |     # allow this syntax.
397 |     if not url_info.netloc and url_info.scheme:
398 |         return False
399 |     # Forbid URLs that start with control characters. Some browsers (like
400 |     # Chrome) ignore quite a few control characters at the start of a
401 |     # URL and might consider the URL as scheme relative.
402 |     if unicodedata.category(url[0])[0] == 'C':
403 |         return False
404 |     scheme = url_info.scheme
405 |     # Consider URLs without a scheme (e.g. //example.com/p) to be http.
406 |     if not url_info.scheme and url_info.netloc:
407 |         scheme = 'http'
408 |     valid_schemes = ['https'] if require_https else ['http', 'https']
409 |     return ((not url_info.netloc or url_info.netloc in allowed_hosts) and
410 |             (not scheme or scheme in valid_schemes))
411 | 
412 | 
413 | def limited_parse_qsl(qs, keep_blank_values=False, encoding='utf-8',
414 |                       errors='replace', fields_limit=None):
415 |     """
416 |     Return a list of key/value tuples parsed from query string.
417 | 
418 |     Copied from urlparse with an additional "fields_limit" argument.
419 |     Copyright (C) 2013 Python Software Foundation (see LICENSE.python).
420 | 
421 |     Arguments:
422 | 
423 |     qs: percent-encoded query string to be parsed
424 | 
425 |     keep_blank_values: flag indicating whether blank values in
426 |         percent-encoded queries should be treated as blank strings. A
427 |         true value indicates that blanks should be retained as blank
428 |         strings. The default false value indicates that blank values
429 |         are to be ignored and treated as if they were  not included.
430 | 
431 |     encoding and errors: specify how to decode percent-encoded sequences
432 |         into Unicode characters, as accepted by the bytes.decode() method.
433 | 
434 |     fields_limit: maximum number of fields parsed or an exception
435 |         is raised. None means no limit and is the default.
436 |     """
437 |     if fields_limit:
438 |         pairs = FIELDS_MATCH.split(qs, fields_limit)
439 |         if len(pairs) > fields_limit:
440 |             raise TooManyFieldsSent(
441 |                 'The number of GET/POST parameters exceeded '
442 |                 'settings.DATA_UPLOAD_MAX_NUMBER_FIELDS.'
443 |             )
444 |     else:
445 |         pairs = FIELDS_MATCH.split(qs)
446 |     r = []
447 |     for name_value in pairs:
448 |         if not name_value:
449 |             continue
450 |         nv = name_value.split('=', 1)
451 |         if len(nv) != 2:
452 |             # Handle case of a control-name with no equal sign
453 |             if keep_blank_values:
454 |                 nv.append('')
455 |             else:
456 |                 continue
457 |         if nv[1] or keep_blank_values:
458 |             name = nv[0].replace('+', ' ')
459 |             name = unquote(name, encoding=encoding, errors=errors)
460 |             value = nv[1].replace('+', ' ')
461 |             value = unquote(value, encoding=encoding, errors=errors)
462 |             r.append((name, value))
463 |     return r
464 | 
465 | 
466 | def escape_leading_slashes(url):
467 |     """
468 |     If redirecting to an absolute path (two leading slashes), a slash must be
469 |     escaped to prevent browsers from handling the path as schemaless and
470 |     redirecting to another host.
471 |     """
472 |     if url.startswith('//'):
473 |         url = '/%2F{}'.format(url[2:])
474 |     return url

[65/500] django__django-11880

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Form Field’s __deepcopy__ does not (deep)copy the error messages.
Description
	
The __deepcopy__ method defined for the formfields (​https://github.com/django/django/blob/146086f219d01dbb1cd8c089b5a5667e396e1cc4/django/forms/fields.py#L200) performs a shallow copy of self and does not include additional treatment for the error_messages dictionary. As a result, all copies of the same field share the same dictionary and any modification of either the dictionary or the error message itself for one formfield is immediately reflected on all other formfiels.
This is relevant for Forms and ModelForms that modify the error messages of their fields dynamically: while each instance of the specific form (e.g., ProfileForm) is expected to have a set of fields “sealed” away from other instances of the same ProfileForm (​https://github.com/django/django/blob/146086f219d01dbb1cd8c089b5a5667e396e1cc4/django/forms/forms.py#L95), in fact all these instances share the same error messages, resulting in incorrectly raised errors.
Confirmed for versions of Django going back to 1.11.



Hints:
Thanks for this report. I attached a simple test. Reproduced at f52022ad96356d4b1061610f2b74ea4d1956a608.
Simple test.
​PR

212 |         super().__init__(**kwargs)
213 |         if min_length is not None:
214 |             self.validators.append(validators.MinLengthValidator(int(min_length)))
215 |         if max_length is not None:
216 |             self.validators.append(validators.MaxLengthValidator(int(max_length)))
217 |         self.validators.append(validators.ProhibitNullCharactersValidator())
218 | 
219 |     def to_python(self, value):
220 |         """Return a string."""
221 |         if value not in self.empty_values:
222 |             value = str(value)
223 |             if self.strip:
224 |                 value = value.strip()
225 |         if value in self.empty_values:
226 |             return self.empty_value
227 |         return value
228 | 
229 |     def widget_attrs(self, widget):
230 |         attrs = super().widget_attrs(widget)

[66/500] django__django-11885

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Combine fast delete queries
Description
	
When emulating ON DELETE CASCADE via on_delete=models.CASCADE the deletion.Collector will try to perform fast queries which are DELETE FROM table WHERE table.pk IN .... There's a few conditions required for this fast path to be taken but when this happens the collection logic should combine such queries by table to reduce the number of roundtrips to the database.
For example, given the following models
class Person(models.Model):
	friends = models.ManyToManyField('self')
class User(models.Model):
	pass
class Entry(models.Model):
	created_by = models.ForeignKey(User)
	updated_by = models.ForeignKey(User)
Issuing a person.delete() or user.delete() will result in 3 queries of the form
DELETE FROM person_friends WHERE from_id = :id
DELETE FROM person_friends WHERE to_id = :id
DELETE FROM person WHERE id = :id
DELETE FROM entry WHERE created_by_id = :id
DELETE FROM entry WHERE updated_by = :id
DELETRE FROM user WHERE id = :id
But both queries (or N queries depending on the number of foreign relationships) can be combined into a single one by using OR
DELETE FROM person_friends WHERE from_id = :id OR to_id = :id
DELETE FROM person WHERE id = :id
DELETE FROM entry WHERE created_by_id = :id OR updated_by = :id
DELETE FROM user WHERE id = :id

  9 | class ProtectedError(IntegrityError):
 10 |     def __init__(self, msg, protected_objects):
 11 |         self.protected_objects = protected_objects
 12 |         super().__init__(msg, protected_objects)
 13 | 
 14 | 
 15 | def CASCADE(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 16 |     collector.collect(sub_objs, source=field.remote_field.model,
 17 |                       source_attr=field.name, nullable=field.null)
 18 |     if field.null and not connections[using].features.can_defer_constraint_checks:
 19 |         collector.add_field_update(field, None, sub_objs)
 20 | 
 21 | 
 22 | def PROTECT(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 23 |     raise ProtectedError(
 24 |         "Cannot delete some instances of model '%s' because they are "
 25 |         "referenced through a protected foreign key: '%s.%s'" % (
 26 |             field.remote_field.model.__name__, sub_objs[0].__class__.__name__, field.name
 27 |         ),
 28 |         sub_objs
 29 |     )
 30 | 
 31 | 
 32 | def SET(value):
 33 |     if callable(value):
 34 |         def set_on_delete(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 35 |             collector.add_field_update(field, value(), sub_objs)
 36 |     else:
 37 |         def set_on_delete(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 38 |             collector.add_field_update(field, value, sub_objs)
 39 |     set_on_delete.deconstruct = lambda: ('django.db.models.SET', (value,), {})
 40 |     return set_on_delete
 41 | 
 42 | 
 43 | def SET_NULL(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 44 |     collector.add_field_update(field, None, sub_objs)
 45 | 
 46 | 
 47 | def SET_DEFAULT(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 48 |     collector.add_field_update(field, field.get_default(), sub_objs)
 49 | 
 50 | 
 51 | def DO_NOTHING(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 52 |     pass
 53 | 
 54 | 
 55 | def get_candidate_relations_to_delete(opts):
 56 |     # The candidate relations are the ones that come from N-1 and 1-1 relations.
 57 |     # N-N  (i.e., many-to-many) relations aren't candidates for deletion.
 58 |     return (
 59 |         f for f in opts.get_fields(include_hidden=True)
 60 |         if f.auto_created and not f.concrete and (f.one_to_one or f.one_to_many)
 61 |     )
 62 | 
 63 | 
 64 | class Collector:
 65 |     def __init__(self, using):
 66 |         self.using = using
 67 |         # Initially, {model: {instances}}, later values become lists.
 68 |         self.data = {}
 69 |         self.field_updates = {}  # {model: {(field, value): {instances}}}
 70 |         # fast_deletes is a list of queryset-likes that can be deleted without
 71 |         # fetching the objects into memory.
 72 |         self.fast_deletes = []
 73 | 
 74 |         # Tracks deletion-order dependency for databases without transactions
 75 |         # or ability to defer constraint checks. Only concrete model classes
 76 |         # should be included, as the dependencies exist only between actual
 77 |         # database tables; proxy models are represented here by their concrete
 78 |         # parent.
 79 |         self.dependencies = {}  # {model: {models}}
 80 | 
 81 |     def add(self, objs, source=None, nullable=False, reverse_dependency=False):
 82 |         """
 83 |         Add 'objs' to the collection of objects to be deleted.  If the call is
 84 |         the result of a cascade, 'source' should be the model that caused it,
 85 |         and 'nullable' should be set to True if the relation can be null.
 86 | 
 87 |         Return a list of all objects that were not already collected.
 88 |         """
 89 |         if not objs:
 90 |             return []
 91 |         new_objs = []
 92 |         model = objs[0].__class__
 93 |         instances = self.data.setdefault(model, set())
 94 |         for obj in objs:
 95 |             if obj not in instances:
 96 |                 new_objs.append(obj)
 97 |         instances.update(new_objs)
 98 |         # Nullable relationships can be ignored -- they are nulled out before
 99 |         # deleting, and therefore do not affect the order in which objects have
100 |         # to be deleted.
101 |         if source is not None and not nullable:
102 |             if reverse_dependency:
103 |                 source, model = model, source
104 |             self.dependencies.setdefault(
105 |                 source._meta.concrete_model, set()).add(model._meta.concrete_model)
106 |         return new_objs
107 | 
108 |     def add_field_update(self, field, value, objs):
109 |         """
110 |         Schedule a field update. 'objs' must be a homogeneous iterable
111 |         collection of model instances (e.g. a QuerySet).
112 |         """
113 |         if not objs:
114 |             return
115 |         model = objs[0].__class__
116 |         self.field_updates.setdefault(
117 |             model, {}).setdefault(
118 |             (field, value), set()).update(objs)
119 | 
120 |     def _has_signal_listeners(self, model):
121 |         return (
122 |             signals.pre_delete.has_listeners(model) or
123 |             signals.post_delete.has_listeners(model)
124 |         )
125 | 
126 |     def can_fast_delete(self, objs, from_field=None):
127 |         """
128 |         Determine if the objects in the given queryset-like or single object
129 |         can be fast-deleted. This can be done if there are no cascades, no
130 |         parents and no signal listeners for the object class.
131 | 
132 |         The 'from_field' tells where we are coming from - we need this to
133 |         determine if the objects are in fact to be deleted. Allow also
134 |         skipping parent -> child -> parent chain preventing fast delete of
135 |         the child.
136 |         """
137 |         if from_field and from_field.remote_field.on_delete is not CASCADE:
138 |             return False
139 |         if hasattr(objs, '_meta'):
140 |             model = type(objs)
141 |         elif hasattr(objs, 'model') and hasattr(objs, '_raw_delete'):
142 |             model = objs.model
143 |         else:
144 |             return False
145 |         if self._has_signal_listeners(model):
146 |             return False
147 |         # The use of from_field comes from the need to avoid cascade back to
148 |         # parent when parent delete is cascading to child.
149 |         opts = model._meta
150 |         return (
151 |             all(link == from_field for link in opts.concrete_model._meta.parents.values()) and
152 |             # Foreign keys pointing to this model.
153 |             all(
154 |                 related.field.remote_field.on_delete is DO_NOTHING
155 |                 for related in get_candidate_relations_to_delete(opts)
156 |             ) and (
157 |                 # Something like generic foreign key.
158 |                 not any(hasattr(field, 'bulk_related_objects') for field in opts.private_fields)
159 |             )
160 |         )
161 | 
162 |     def get_del_batches(self, objs, field):
163 |         """
164 |         Return the objs in suitably sized batches for the used connection.
165 |         """
166 |         conn_batch_size = max(
167 |             connections[self.using].ops.bulk_batch_size([field.name], objs), 1)
168 |         if len(objs) > conn_batch_size:
169 |             return [objs[i:i + conn_batch_size]
170 |                     for i in range(0, len(objs), conn_batch_size)]
171 |         else:
172 |             return [objs]
173 | 
174 |     def collect(self, objs, source=None, nullable=False, collect_related=True,
175 |                 source_attr=None, reverse_dependency=False, keep_parents=False):
176 |         """
177 |         Add 'objs' to the collection of objects to be deleted as well as all
178 |         parent instances.  'objs' must be a homogeneous iterable collection of
179 |         model instances (e.g. a QuerySet).  If 'collect_related' is True,
180 |         related objects will be handled by their respective on_delete handler.
181 | 
182 |         If the call is the result of a cascade, 'source' should be the model
183 |         that caused it and 'nullable' should be set to True, if the relation
184 |         can be null.
185 | 
186 |         If 'reverse_dependency' is True, 'source' will be deleted before the
187 |         current model, rather than after. (Needed for cascading to parent
188 |         models, the one case in which the cascade follows the forwards
189 |         direction of an FK rather than the reverse direction.)
190 | 
191 |         If 'keep_parents' is True, data of parent model's will be not deleted.
192 |         """
193 |         if self.can_fast_delete(objs):
194 |             self.fast_deletes.append(objs)
195 |             return
196 |         new_objs = self.add(objs, source, nullable,
197 |                             reverse_dependency=reverse_dependency)
198 |         if not new_objs:
199 |             return
200 | 
201 |         model = new_objs[0].__class__
202 | 
203 |         if not keep_parents:
204 |             # Recursively collect concrete model's parent models, but not their
205 |             # related objects. These will be found by meta.get_fields()
206 |             concrete_model = model._meta.concrete_model
207 |             for ptr in concrete_model._meta.parents.values():
208 |                 if ptr:
209 |                     parent_objs = [getattr(obj, ptr.name) for obj in new_objs]
210 |                     self.collect(parent_objs, source=model,
211 |                                  source_attr=ptr.remote_field.related_name,
212 |                                  collect_related=False,
213 |                                  reverse_dependency=True)
214 |         if collect_related:
215 |             if keep_parents:
216 |                 parents = set(model._meta.get_parent_list())
217 |             for related in get_candidate_relations_to_delete(model._meta):
218 |                 # Preserve parent reverse relationships if keep_parents=True.
219 |                 if keep_parents and related.model in parents:
220 |                     continue
221 |                 field = related.field
222 |                 if field.remote_field.on_delete == DO_NOTHING:
223 |                     continue
224 |                 batches = self.get_del_batches(new_objs, field)
225 |                 for batch in batches:
226 |                     sub_objs = self.related_objects(related, batch)
227 |                     if self.can_fast_delete(sub_objs, from_field=field):
228 |                         self.fast_deletes.append(sub_objs)
229 |                     else:
230 |                         related_model = related.related_model
231 |                         # Non-referenced fields can be deferred if no signal
232 |                         # receivers are connected for the related model as
233 |                         # they'll never be exposed to the user. Skip field
234 |                         # deferring when some relationships are select_related
235 |                         # as interactions between both features are hard to
236 |                         # get right. This should only happen in the rare
237 |                         # cases where .related_objects is overridden anyway.
238 |                         if not (sub_objs.query.select_related or self._has_signal_listeners(related_model)):
239 |                             referenced_fields = set(chain.from_iterable(
240 |                                 (rf.attname for rf in rel.field.foreign_related_fields)
241 |                                 for rel in get_candidate_relations_to_delete(related_model._meta)
242 |                             ))
243 |                             sub_objs = sub_objs.only(*tuple(referenced_fields))
244 |                         if sub_objs:
245 |                             field.remote_field.on_delete(self, field, sub_objs, self.using)
246 |             for field in model._meta.private_fields:
247 |                 if hasattr(field, 'bulk_related_objects'):
248 |                     # It's something like generic foreign key.
249 |                     sub_objs = field.bulk_related_objects(new_objs, self.using)
250 |                     self.collect(sub_objs, source=model, nullable=True)
251 | 
252 |     def related_objects(self, related, objs):
253 |         """
254 |         Get a QuerySet of objects related to `objs` via the relation `related`.
255 |         """
256 |         return related.related_model._base_manager.using(self.using).filter(
257 |             **{"%s__in" % related.field.name: objs}
258 |         )
259 | 
260 |     def instances_with_model(self):
261 |         for model, instances in self.data.items():
262 |             for obj in instances:
263 |                 yield model, obj
264 | 
265 |     def sort(self):
266 |         sorted_models = []
267 |         concrete_models = set()
268 |         models = list(self.data)
269 |         while len(sorted_models) < len(models):
270 |             found = False
271 |             for model in models:
272 |                 if model in sorted_models:
273 |                     continue
274 |                 dependencies = self.dependencies.get(model._meta.concrete_model)
275 |                 if not (dependencies and dependencies.difference(concrete_models)):
276 |                     sorted_models.append(model)
277 |                     concrete_models.add(model._meta.concrete_model)
278 |                     found = True
279 |             if not found:
280 |                 return
281 |         self.data = {model: self.data[model] for model in sorted_models}
282 | 
283 |     def delete(self):
284 |         # sort instance collections
285 |         for model, instances in self.data.items():
286 |             self.data[model] = sorted(instances, key=attrgetter("pk"))
287 | 
288 |         # if possible, bring the models in an order suitable for databases that
289 |         # don't support transactions or cannot defer constraint checks until the
290 |         # end of a transaction.
291 |         self.sort()
292 |         # number of objects deleted for each model label
293 |         deleted_counter = Counter()
294 | 
295 |         # Optimize for the case with a single obj and no dependencies
296 |         if len(self.data) == 1 and len(instances) == 1:
297 |             instance = list(instances)[0]
298 |             if self.can_fast_delete(instance):
299 |                 with transaction.mark_for_rollback_on_error():
300 |                     count = sql.DeleteQuery(model).delete_batch([instance.pk], self.using)
301 |                 setattr(instance, model._meta.pk.attname, None)
302 |                 return count, {model._meta.label: count}
303 | 
304 |         with transaction.atomic(using=self.using, savepoint=False):
305 |             # send pre_delete signals
306 |             for model, obj in self.instances_with_model():
307 |                 if not model._meta.auto_created:
308 |                     signals.pre_delete.send(
309 |                         sender=model, instance=obj, using=self.using
310 |                     )
311 | 
312 |             # fast deletes
313 |             for qs in self.fast_deletes:
314 |                 count = qs._raw_delete(using=self.using)
315 |                 deleted_counter[qs.model._meta.label] += count
316 | 
317 |             # update fields
318 |             for model, instances_for_fieldvalues in self.field_updates.items():
319 |                 for (field, value), instances in instances_for_fieldvalues.items():
320 |                     query = sql.UpdateQuery(model)
321 |                     query.update_batch([obj.pk for obj in instances],
322 |                                        {field.name: value}, self.using)
323 | 
324 |             # reverse instance collections
325 |             for instances in self.data.values():
326 |                 instances.reverse()
327 | 
328 |             # delete instances
329 |             for model, instances in self.data.items():
330 |                 query = sql.DeleteQuery(model)
331 |                 pk_list = [obj.pk for obj in instances]
332 |                 count = query.delete_batch(pk_list, self.using)
333 |                 deleted_counter[model._meta.label] += count
334 | 
335 |                 if not model._meta.auto_created:
336 |                     for obj in instances:
337 |                         signals.post_delete.send(
338 |                             sender=model, instance=obj, using=self.using
339 |                         )
340 | 
341 |         # update collected instances
342 |         for instances_for_fieldvalues in self.field_updates.values():
343 |             for (field, value), instances in instances_for_fieldvalues.items():
344 |                 for obj in instances:
345 |                     setattr(obj, field.attname, value)
346 |         for model, instances in self.data.items():
347 |             for instance in instances:
348 |                 setattr(instance, model._meta.pk.attname, None)
349 |         return sum(deleted_counter.values()), dict(deleted_counter)

--- Step 3 ---
🔧 Thought: Reasoning: 
Looking at the `Collector` class in `django/db/models/deletion.py`:

1. The `collect` method (line 174-250) adds objects to be deleted. When `can_fast_delete` returns True, it adds the queryset to `self.fast_deletes` (line 194).

2. The `delete` method (line 283-349) processes these fast deletes. At line 313-315, it iterates through `self.fast_deletes` and calls `_raw_delete` on each queryset individually:
```python
for qs in self.fast_deletes:
    count = qs._raw_delete(using=self.using)
    deleted_counter[qs.model._meta.label] += count
```

The issue is that when there are multiple queries for the same table (e.g., `person_friends` with `from_id` and `to_id` conditions), they are executed separately instead of being combined into a single query with OR conditions.

For example, with the models in the issue:
- `Person.friends` (ManyToMany to self) creates two queries: `DELETE FROM person_friends WHERE from_id = :id` and `DELETE FROM person_friends WHERE to_id = :id`
- `Entry` has two foreign keys to `User`: `created_by` and `updated_by`, creating two separate queries

The fix should combine fast delete queries by table. When multiple querysets target the same model, they should be combined into a single query using OR conditions.

Let me look at how `fast_deletes` is populated and understand the structure better.

 15 | def CASCADE(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 16 |     collector.collect(sub_objs, source=field.remote_field.model,
 17 |                       source_attr=field.name, nullable=field.null)
 18 |     if field.null and not connections[using].features.can_defer_constraint_checks:

 32 | def SET(value):
 33 |     if callable(value):
 34 |         def set_on_delete(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 35 |             collector.add_field_update(field, value(), sub_objs)
 36 |     else:
 37 |         def set_on_delete(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 38 |             collector.add_field_update(field, value, sub_objs)
 39 |     set_on_delete.deconstruct = lambda: ('django.db.models.SET', (value,), {})
 40 |     return set_on_delete

 43 | def SET_NULL(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 44 |     collector.add_field_update(field, None, sub_objs)

 47 | def SET_DEFAULT(collector, field, sub_objs, using):
 48 |     collector.add_field_update(field, field.get_default(), sub_objs)

 51 | def DO_NOTHING(collector, field, sub_objs, using):

 54 | 
 55 | def get_candidate_relations_to_delete(opts):
 56 |     # The candidate relations are the ones that come from N-1 and 1-1 relations.
 57 |     # N-N  (i.e., many-to-many) relations aren't candidates for deletion.
 58 |     return (
 59 |         f for f in opts.get_fields(include_hidden=True)
 60 |         if f.auto_created and not f.concrete and (f.one_to_one or f.one_to_many)
 61 |     )
 62 | 
 63 | 
 64 | class Collector:
 65 |     def __init__(self, using):
 66 |         self.using = using
 67 |         # Initially, {model: {instances}}, later values become lists.
 68 |         self.data = {}
 69 |         self.field_updates = {}  # {model: {(field, value): {instances}}}
 70 |         # fast_deletes is a list of queryset-likes that can be deleted without
 71 |         # fetching the objects into memory.
 72 |         self.fast_deletes = []
 73 | 
 74 |         # Tracks deletion-order dependency for databases without transactions
 75 |         # or ability to defer constraint checks. Only concrete model classes
 76 |         # should be included, as the dependencies exist only between actual
 77 |         # database tables; proxy models are represented here by their concrete
 78 |         # parent.
 79 |         self.dependencies = {}  # {model: {models}}
 80 | 
 81 |     def add(self, objs, source=None, nullable=False, reverse_dependency=False):
 82 |         """
 83 |         Add 'objs' to the collection of objects to be deleted.  If the call is
 84 |         the result of a cascade, 'source' should be the model that caused it,
 85 |         and 'nullable' should be set to True if the relation can be null.
 86 | 
 87 |         Return a list of all objects that were not already collected.
 88 |         """
 89 |         if not objs:
 90 |             return []
 91 |         new_objs = []
 92 |         model = objs[0].__class__
 93 |         instances = self.data.setdefault(model, set())
 94 |         for obj in objs:
 95 |             if obj not in instances:
 96 |                 new_objs.append(obj)
 97 |         instances.update(new_objs)
 98 |         # Nullable relationships can be ignored -- they are nulled out before
 99 |         # deleting, and therefore do not affect the order in which objects have
100 |         # to be deleted.

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

bulk_create batch_size param overrides the compatible batch size calculation
Description
	 
		(last modified by Ahmet Kucuk)
	 
At this line: ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/stable/2.2.x/django/db/models/query.py#L1197
batch_size param overrides compatible batch size calculation. This looks like a bug as bulk_update properly picks the minimum of two:
​https://github.com/django/django/blob/stable/2.2.x/django/db/models/query.py#L504
I suggest using similar
 batch_size = min(batch_size, max_batch_size) if batch_size else max_batch_size
logic in bulk_create as well. I am happy to open a PR for it.

  38 | class BaseIterable:
  39 |     def __init__(self, queryset, chunked_fetch=False, chunk_size=GET_ITERATOR_CHUNK_SIZE):
  40 |         self.queryset = queryset
  41 |         self.chunked_fetch = chunked_fetch
  42 |         self.chunk_size = chunk_size

  45 | class ModelIterable(BaseIterable):
  46 |     """Iterable that yields a model instance for each row."""
  47 | 
  48 |     def __iter__(self):
  49 |         queryset = self.queryset
  50 |         db = queryset.db
  51 |         compiler = queryset.query.get_compiler(using=db)
  52 |         # Execute the query. This will also fill compiler.select, klass_info,
  53 |         # and annotations.
  54 |         results = compiler.execute_sql(chunked_fetch=self.chunked_fetch, chunk_size=self.chunk_size)
  55 |         select, klass_info, annotation_col_map = (compiler.select, compiler.klass_info,
  56 |                                                   compiler.annotation_col_map)
  57 |         model_cls = klass_info['model']
  58 |         select_fields = klass_info['select_fields']
  59 |         model_fields_start, model_fields_end = select_fields[0], select_fields[-1] + 1
  60 |         init_list = [f[0].target.attname
  61 |                      for f in select[model_fields_start:model_fields_end]]
  62 |         related_populators = get_related_populators(klass_info, select, db)
  63 |         known_related_objects = [
  64 |             (field, related_objs, operator.attrgetter(*[
  65 |                 field.attname
  66 |                 if from_field == 'self' else
  67 |                 queryset.model._meta.get_field(from_field).attname
  68 |                 for from_field in field.from_fields
  69 |             ])) for field, related_objs in queryset._known_related_objects.items()
  70 |         ]
  71 |         for row in compiler.results_iter(results):
  72 |             obj = model_cls.from_db(db, init_list, row[model_fields_start:model_fields_end])
  73 |             for rel_populator in related_populators:
  74 |                 rel_populator.populate(row, obj)
  75 |             if annotation_col_map:
  76 |                 for attr_name, col_pos in annotation_col_map.items():
  77 |                     setattr(obj, attr_name, row[col_pos])
  78 | 
  79 |             # Add the known related objects to the model.
  80 |             for field, rel_objs, rel_getter in known_related_objects:
  81 |                 # Avoid overwriting objects loaded by, e.g., select_related().
  82 |                 if field.is_cached(obj):
  83 |                     continue
  84 |                 rel_obj_id = rel_getter(obj)
  85 |                 try:
  86 |                     rel_obj = rel_objs[rel_obj_id]
  87 |                 except KeyError:
  88 |                     pass  # May happen in qs1 | qs2 scenarios.
  89 |                 else:
  90 |                     setattr(obj, field.name, rel_obj)
  91 | 
  92 |             yield obj
  93 |

  95 | class ValuesIterable(BaseIterable):
  96 |     """
  97 |     Iterable returned by QuerySet.values() that yields a dict for each row.
  98 |     """
  99 | 
 100 |     def __iter__(self):
 101 |         queryset = self.queryset
 102 |         query = queryset.query
 103 |         compiler = query.get_compiler(queryset.db)
 104 | 
 105 |         # extra(select=...) cols are always at the start of the row.
 106 |         names = [
 107 |             *query.extra_select,
 108 |             *query.values_select,
 109 |             *query.annotation_select,
 110 |         ]
 111 |         indexes = range(len(names))
 112 |         for row in compiler.results_iter(chunked_fetch=self.chunked_fetch, chunk_size=self.chunk_size):
 113 |             yield {names[i]: row[i] for i in indexes}

 116 | class ValuesListIterable(BaseIterable):
 117 |     """
 118 |     Iterable returned by QuerySet.values_list(flat=False) that yields a tuple
 119 |     for each row.
 120 |     """
 121 | 
 122 |     def __iter__(self):
 123 |         queryset = self.queryset
 124 |         query = queryset.query
 125 |         compiler = query.get_compiler(queryset.db)
 126 | 
 127 |         if queryset._fields:
 128 |             # extra(select=...) cols are always at the start of the row.
 129 |             names = [
 130 |                 *query.extra_select,
 131 |                 *query.values_select,
 132 |                 *query.annotation_select,
 133 |             ]
 134 |             fields = [*queryset._fields, *(f for f in query.annotation_select if f not in queryset._fields)]
 135 |             if fields != names:
 136 |                 # Reorder according to fields.
 137 |                 index_map = {name: idx for idx, name in enumerate(names)}
 138 |                 rowfactory = operator.itemgetter(*[index_map[f] for f in fields])
 139 |                 return map(
 140 |                     rowfactory,
 141 |                     compiler.results_iter(chunked_fetch=self.chunked_fetch, chunk_size=self.chunk_size)
 142 |                 )
 143 |         return compiler.results_iter(tuple_expected=True, chunked_fetch=self.chunked_fetch, chunk_size=self.chunk_size)

 146 | class NamedValuesListIterable(ValuesListIterable):
 147 |     """
 148 |     Iterable returned by QuerySet.values_list(named=True) that yields a
 149 |     namedtuple for each row.
 150 |     """
 151 | 
 152 |     @staticmethod
 153 |     @lru_cache()
 154 |     def create_namedtuple_class(*names):
 155 |         # Cache namedtuple() with @lru_cache() since it's too slow to be
 156 |         # called for every QuerySet evaluation.
 157 |         return namedtuple('Row', names)
 158 | 
 159 |     def __iter__(self):
 160 |         queryset = self.queryset
 161 |         if queryset._fields:
 162 |             names = queryset._fields
 163 |         else:
 164 |             query = queryset.query
 165 |             names = [*query.extra_select, *query.values_select, *query.annotation_select]
 166 |         tuple_class = self.create_namedtuple_class(*names)
 167 |         new = tuple.__new__
 168 |         for row in super().__iter__():
 169 |             yield new(tuple_class, row)

 185 | class QuerySet:
 186 |     """Represent a lazy database lookup for a set of objects."""
 187 | 
 188 |     def __init__(self, model=None, query=None, using=None, hints=None):
 189 |         self.model = model
 190 |         self._db = using
 191 |         self._hints = hints or {}
 192 |         self.query = query or sql.Query(self.model)
 193 |         self._result_cache = None
 194 |         self._sticky_filter = False
 195 |         self._for_write = False
 196 |         self._prefetch_related_lookups = ()
 197 |         self._prefetch_done = False
 198 |         self._known_related_objects = {}  # {rel_field: {pk: rel_obj}}
 199 |         self._iterable_class = ModelIterable
 200 |         self._fields = None
 201 | 
 202 |     def as_manager(cls):
 203 |         # Address the circular dependency between `Queryset` and `Manager`.
 204 |         from django.db.models.manager import Manager
 205 |         manager = Manager.from_queryset(cls)()
 206 |         manager._built_with_as_manager = True
 207 |         return manager
 208 |     as_manager.queryset_only = True
 209 |     as_manager = classmethod(as_manager)
 210 | 
 211 |     ########################
 212 |     # PYTHON MAGIC METHODS #
 213 |     ########################
 214 | 
 215 |     def __deepcopy__(self, memo):
 216 |         """Don't populate the QuerySet's cache."""
 217 |         obj = self.__class__()
 218 |         for k, v in self.__dict__.items():
 219 |             if k == '_result_cache':
 220 |                 obj.__dict__[k] = None
 221 |             else:
 222 |                 obj.__dict__[k] = copy.deepcopy(v, memo)
 223 |         return obj
 224 | 
 225 |     def __getstate__(self):
 226 |         # Force the cache to be fully populated.
 227 |         self._fetch_all()
 228 |         return {**self.__dict__, DJANGO_VERSION_PICKLE_KEY: get_version()}
 229 | 
 230 |     def __setstate__(self, state):
 231 |         msg = None
 232 |         pickled_version = state.get(DJANGO_VERSION_PICKLE_KEY)
 233 |         if pickled_version:
 234 |             current_version = get_version()
 235 |             if current_version != pickled_version:
 236 |                 msg = (
 237 |                     "Pickled queryset instance's Django version %s does not "
 238 |                     "match the current version %s." % (pickled_version, current_version)
 239 |                 )
 240 |         else:
 241 |             msg = "Pickled queryset instance's Django version is not specified."
 242 | 
 243 |         if msg:
 244 |             warnings.warn(msg, RuntimeWarning, stacklevel=2)
 245 | 
 246 |         self.__dict__.update(state)
 247 | 
 248 |     def __repr__(self):
 249 |         data = list(self[:REPR_OUTPUT_SIZE + 1])
 250 |         if len(data) > REPR_OUTPUT_SIZE:
 251 |             data[-1] = "...(remaining elements truncated)..."
 252 |         return '<%s %r>' % (self.__class__.__name__, data)
 253 | 
 254 |     def __len__(self):
 255 |         self._fetch_all()
 256 |         return len(self._result_cache)
 257 | 
 258 |     def __iter__(self):
 259 |         """
 260 |         The queryset iterator protocol uses three nested iterators in the
 261 |         default case:
 262 |             1. sql.compiler.execute_sql()
 263 |                - Returns 100 rows at time (constants.GET_ITERATOR_CHUNK_SIZE)
 264 |                  using cursor.fetchmany(). This part is responsible for
 265 |                  doing some column masking, and returning the rows in chunks.
 266 |             2. sql.compiler.results_iter()
 267 |                - Returns one row at time. At this point the rows are still just
 268 |                  tuples. In some cases the return values are converted to
 269 |                  Python values at this location.
 270 |             3. self.iterator()
 271 |                - Responsible for turning the rows into model objects.
 272 |         """
