223 | 
224 |     def make_bytes(self, value):
225 |         """Turn a value into a bytestring encoded in the output charset."""
226 |         # Per PEP 3333, this response body must be bytes. To avoid returning
227 |         # an instance of a subclass, this function returns `bytes(value)`.
228 |         # This doesn't make a copy when `value` already contains bytes.
229 | 
230 |         # Handle string types -- we can't rely on force_bytes here because:
231 |         # - Python attempts str conversion first
232 |         # - when self._charset != 'utf-8' it re-encodes the content
233 |         if isinstance(value, (bytes, memoryview)):
234 |             return bytes(value)
235 |         if isinstance(value, str):
236 |             return bytes(value.encode(self.charset))
237 |         # Handle non-string types.
238 |         return str(value).encode(self.charset)
239 | 
240 |     # These methods partially implement the file-like object interface.
241 |     # See https://docs.python.org/library/io.html#io.IOBase
242 | 
243 |     # The WSGI server must call this method upon completion of the request.
244 |     # See http://blog.dscpl.com.au/2012/10/obligations-for-calling-close-on.html
245 |     def close(self):
246 |         for closer in self._resource_closers:
247 |             try:
248 |                 closer()
249 |             except Exception:
250 |                 pass
251 |         # Free resources that were still referenced.
252 |         self._resource_closers.clear()
253 |         self.closed = True
254 |         signals.request_finished.send(sender=self._handler_class)
255 | 
256 |     def write(self, content):
257 |         raise OSError('This %s instance is not writable' % self.__class__.__name__)
258 | 
259 |     def flush(self):
260 |         pass
261 | 
262 |     def tell(self):
263 |         raise OSError('This %s instance cannot tell its position' % self.__class__.__name__)
264 | 
265 |     # These methods partially implement a stream-like object interface.
266 |     # See https://docs.python.org/library/io.html#io.IOBase
267 | 
268 |     def readable(self):
269 |         return False
270 | 
271 |     def seekable(self):
272 |         return False
273 | 
274 |     def writable(self):
275 |         return False
276 | 
277 |     def writelines(self, lines):
278 |         raise OSError('This %s instance is not writable' % self.__class__.__name__)
279 | 
280 | 
281 | class HttpResponse(HttpResponseBase):
282 |     """
283 |     An HTTP response class with a string as content.
284 | 
285 |     This content that can be read, appended to, or replaced.
286 |     """
287 | 
288 |     streaming = False
289 | 
290 |     def __init__(self, content=b'', *args, **kwargs):
291 |         super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
292 |         # Content is a bytestring. See the `content` property methods.
293 |         self.content = content
294 | 
295 |     def __repr__(self):
296 |         return '<%(cls)s status_code=%(status_code)d%(content_type)s>' % {
297 |             'cls': self.__class__.__name__,
298 |             'status_code': self.status_code,
299 |             'content_type': self._content_type_for_repr,
300 |         }
301 | 
302 |     def serialize(self):
303 |         """Full HTTP message, including headers, as a bytestring."""
304 |         return self.serialize_headers() + b'\r\n\r\n' + self.content
305 | 
306 |     __bytes__ = serialize
307 | 
308 |     @property
309 |     def content(self):
310 |         return b''.join(self._container)
311 | 
312 |     @content.setter
313 |     def content(self, value):
314 |         # Consume iterators upon assignment to allow repeated iteration.
315 |         if hasattr(value, '__iter__') and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
316 |             content = b''.join(self.make_bytes(chunk) for chunk in value)
317 |             if hasattr(value, 'close'):
318 |                 try:
319 |                     value.close()
320 |                 except Exception:
321 |                     pass
322 |         else:
323 |             content = self.make_bytes(value)
324 |         # Create a list of properly encoded bytestrings to support write().
325 |         self._container = [content]
326 | 
327 |     def __iter__(self):
328 |         return iter(self._container)
329 | 
330 |     def write(self, content):
331 |         self._container.append(self.make_bytes(content))
332 | 
333 |     def tell(self):
334 |         return len(self.content)
335 | 
336 |     def getvalue(self):
337 |         return self.content
338 | 
339 |     def writable(self):
340 |         return True
341 | 
342 |     def writelines(self, lines):
343 |         for line in lines:
344 |             self.write(line)
345 | 
346 | 
347 | class StreamingHttpResponse(HttpResponseBase):
348 |     """
349 |     A streaming HTTP response class with an iterator as content.
350 | 
351 |     This should only be iterated once, when the response is streamed to the
352 |     client. However, it can be appended to or replaced with a new iterator
353 |     that wraps the original content (or yields entirely new content).
354 |     """
355 | 
356 |     streaming = True
357 | 
358 |     def __init__(self, streaming_content=(), *args, **kwargs):
359 |         super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
360 |         # `streaming_content` should be an iterable of bytestrings.
361 |         # See the `streaming_content` property methods.
362 |         self.streaming_content = streaming_content
363 | 
364 |     @property
365 |     def content(self):
366 |         raise AttributeError(
367 |             "This %s instance has no `content` attribute. Use "
368 |             "`streaming_content` instead." % self.__class__.__name__
369 |         )
370 | 
371 |     @property
372 |     def streaming_content(self):
373 |         return map(self.make_bytes, self._iterator)
374 | 
375 |     @streaming_content.setter
376 |     def streaming_content(self, value):
377 |         self._set_streaming_content(value)
378 | 
379 |     def _set_streaming_content(self, value):
380 |         # Ensure we can never iterate on "value" more than once.
381 |         self._iterator = iter(value)
382 |         if hasattr(value, 'close'):
383 |             self._resource_closers.append(value.close)
384 | 
385 |     def __iter__(self):
386 |         return self.streaming_content
387 | 
388 |     def getvalue(self):
389 |         return b''.join(self.streaming_content)
390 | 
391 | 
392 | class FileResponse(StreamingHttpResponse):
393 |     """
394 |     A streaming HTTP response class optimized for files.
395 |     """
396 |     block_size = 4096
397 | 
398 |     def __init__(self, *args, as_attachment=False, filename='', **kwargs):
399 |         self.as_attachment = as_attachment
400 |         self.filename = filename
401 |         super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
402 | 
403 |     def _set_streaming_content(self, value):
404 |         if not hasattr(value, 'read'):
405 |             self.file_to_stream = None
406 |             return super()._set_streaming_content(value)
407 | 
408 |         self.file_to_stream = filelike = value
409 |         if hasattr(filelike, 'close'):
410 |             self._resource_closers.append(filelike.close)
411 |         value = iter(lambda: filelike.read(self.block_size), b'')
412 |         self.set_headers(filelike)
413 |         super()._set_streaming_content(value)
414 | 
415 |     def set_headers(self, filelike):
416 |         """
417 |         Set some common response headers (Content-Length, Content-Type, and
418 |         Content-Disposition) based on the `filelike` response content.
419 |         """
420 |         encoding_map = {
421 |             'bzip2': 'application/x-bzip',
422 |             'gzip': 'application/gzip',
423 |             'xz': 'application/x-xz',
424 |         }
425 |         filename = getattr(filelike, 'name', None)
426 |         filename = filename if (isinstance(filename, str) and filename) else self.filename
427 |         if os.path.isabs(filename):
428 |             self['Content-Length'] = os.path.getsize(filelike.name)
429 |         elif hasattr(filelike, 'getbuffer'):
430 |             self['Content-Length'] = filelike.getbuffer().nbytes
431 | 
432 |         if self.get('Content-Type', '').startswith('text/html'):
433 |             if filename:
434 |                 content_type, encoding = mimetypes.guess_type(filename)
435 |                 # Encoding isn't set to prevent browsers from automatically
436 |                 # uncompressing files.
437 |                 content_type = encoding_map.get(encoding, content_type)
438 |                 self['Content-Type'] = content_type or 'application/octet-stream'
439 |             else:
440 |                 self['Content-Type'] = 'application/octet-stream'
441 | 
442 |         filename = self.filename or os.path.basename(filename)
443 |         if filename:
444 |             disposition = 'attachment' if self.as_attachment else 'inline'
445 |             try:
446 |                 filename.encode('ascii')
447 |                 file_expr = 'filename="{}"'.format(filename)
448 |             except UnicodeEncodeError:
449 |                 file_expr = "filename*=utf-8''{}".format(quote(filename))
450 |             self['Content-Disposition'] = '{}; {}'.format(disposition, file_expr)
451 |         elif self.as_attachment:
452 |             self['Content-Disposition'] = 'attachment'
453 | 
454 | 
455 | class HttpResponseRedirectBase(HttpResponse):
456 |     allowed_schemes = ['http', 'https', 'ftp']
457 | 
458 |     def __init__(self, redirect_to, *args, **kwargs):
459 |         super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
460 |         self['Location'] = iri_to_uri(redirect_to)
461 |         parsed = urlparse(str(redirect_to))
462 |         if parsed.scheme and parsed.scheme not in self.allowed_schemes:
463 |             raise DisallowedRedirect("Unsafe redirect to URL with protocol '%s'" % parsed.scheme)
464 |

540 |     An HTTP response class that consumes data to be serialized to JSON.
541 | 
542 |     :param data: Data to be dumped into json. By default only ``dict`` objects
543 |       are allowed to be passed due to a security flaw before EcmaScript 5. See
544 |       the ``safe`` parameter for more information.
545 |     :param encoder: Should be a json encoder class. Defaults to
546 |       ``django.core.serializers.json.DjangoJSONEncoder``.
547 |     :param safe: Controls if only ``dict`` objects may be serialized. Defaults
548 |       to ``True``.
549 |     :param json_dumps_params: A dictionary of kwargs passed to json.dumps().
550 |     """

552 |     def __init__(self, data, encoder=DjangoJSONEncoder, safe=True,
553 |                  json_dumps_params=None, **kwargs):
554 |         if safe and not isinstance(data, dict):
555 |             raise TypeError(

557 |                 'safe parameter to False.'
558 |             )
559 |         if json_dumps_params is None:
560 |             json_dumps_params = {}
561 |         kwargs.setdefault('content_type', 'application/json')

  => NO_DIFF (12.8s) Agent produced no changes

[104/500] django__django-13212

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Make validators include the provided value in ValidationError
Description
	
It is sometimes desirable to include the provide value in a custom error message. For example:
“blah” is not a valid email.
By making built-in validators provide value to ValidationError, one can override an error message and use a %(value)s placeholder.
This placeholder value matches an example already in the docs:
​https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/3.0/ref/validators/#writing-validators



Hints:
​https://github.com/django/django/pull/13212
After reconsideration I have some doubts. Do you think it's really useful to include invalid values in error messages? Invalid form is redisplayed with errors in most (all?) of cases, so I'm not sure if it can be useful to display error messages containing invalid values next to form fields with invalid values (see comment). I'm trying to find a use case.
Invalid form is redisplayed with errors in most (all?) of cases This assumes the form system is used only for a narrow set of use cases that involving HTML rendering. This is not always the case, especially in projects I work on. The form system has two major features: input validation and HTML rendering. I often use the validation system without the HTML rendering. When used in this context, yes, being able to include the provided value in custom error messages is quite helpful. Sometimes this error is displayed in a log other times as an alert. I opened this ticket as my application has practical uses for it, not theoretical. I hit a wall when trying to change an error message to meet an internal specification. As a concrete example, my application allows uploading spreadsheets and the data in those spreadsheets is validated using a Django form. This use case has a history of support: see #24229. In the event of invalid data, the precise error can't be displayed next to the originating value. Providing the submitted value as context in the message helps the user locate and correct it. For example Email “blah” in cell A1 is not a valid email address. Beyond my concrete use case, the code change is quite minimal (IMO) and unobtrusive while providing more flexibility and control for users to customize error messages. I think we should give users that power as it comes with a low maintenance cost.
Sounds reasonable, thanks.

 24 |     inverse_match = False

 27 |     def __init__(self, regex=None, message=None, code=None, inverse_match=None, flags=None):
 28 |         if regex is not None:
 29 |             self.regex = regex
 30 |         if message is not None:
 31 |             self.message = message
 32 |         if code is not None:
 33 |             self.code = code
 34 |         if inverse_match is not None:
 35 |             self.inverse_match = inverse_match
 36 |         if flags is not None:
 37 |             self.flags = flags
 38 |         if self.flags and not isinstance(self.regex, str):
 39 |             raise TypeError("If the flags are set, regex must be a regular expression string.")
 40 | 
 41 |         self.regex = _lazy_re_compile(self.regex, self.flags)
 42 | 
 43 |     def __call__(self, value):
 44 |         """
 45 |         Validate that the input contains (or does *not* contain, if
 46 |         inverse_match is True) a match for the regular expression.
 47 |         """
 48 |         regex_matches = self.regex.search(str(value))
 49 |         invalid_input = regex_matches if self.inverse_match else not regex_matches
 50 |         if invalid_input:
 51 |             raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)

 65 | class URLValidator(RegexValidator):
 66 |     ul = '\u00a1-\uffff'  # Unicode letters range (must not be a raw string).
 67 | 
 68 |     # IP patterns
 69 |     ipv4_re = r'(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4]\d|[0-1]?\d?\d)(?:\.(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4]\d|[0-1]?\d?\d)){3}'
 70 |     ipv6_re = r'\[[0-9a-f:.]+\]'  # (simple regex, validated later)
 71 | 
 72 |     # Host patterns
 73 |     hostname_re = r'[a-z' + ul + r'0-9](?:[a-z' + ul + r'0-9-]{0,61}[a-z' + ul + r'0-9])?'
 74 |     # Max length for domain name labels is 63 characters per RFC 1034 sec. 3.1
 75 |     domain_re = r'(?:\.(?!-)[a-z' + ul + r'0-9-]{1,63}(?<!-))*'
 76 |     tld_re = (
 77 |         r'\.'                                # dot
 78 |         r'(?!-)'                             # can't start with a dash
 79 |         r'(?:[a-z' + ul + '-]{2,63}'         # domain label
 80 |         r'|xn--[a-z0-9]{1,59})'              # or punycode label
 81 |         r'(?<!-)'                            # can't end with a dash
 82 |         r'\.?'                               # may have a trailing dot
 83 |     )
 84 |     host_re = '(' + hostname_re + domain_re + tld_re + '|localhost)'
 85 | 
 86 |     regex = _lazy_re_compile(
 87 |         r'^(?:[a-z0-9.+-]*)://'  # scheme is validated separately
 88 |         r'(?:[^\s:@/]+(?::[^\s:@/]*)?@)?'  # user:pass authentication
 89 |         r'(?:' + ipv4_re + '|' + ipv6_re + '|' + host_re + ')'
 90 |         r'(?::\d{2,5})?'  # port
 91 |         r'(?:[/?#][^\s]*)?'  # resource path
 92 |         r'\Z', re.IGNORECASE)
 93 |     message = _('Enter a valid URL.')
 94 |     schemes = ['http', 'https', 'ftp', 'ftps']
 95 | 
 96 |     def __init__(self, schemes=None, **kwargs):
 97 |         super().__init__(**kwargs)
 98 |         if schemes is not None:
 99 |             self.schemes = schemes
100 | 
101 |     def __call__(self, value):
102 |         if not isinstance(value, str):
103 |             raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)
104 |         # Check if the scheme is valid.
105 |         scheme = value.split('://')[0].lower()
106 |         if scheme not in self.schemes:
107 |             raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)
108 | 
109 |         # Then check full URL
110 |         try:
111 |             super().__call__(value)
112 |         except ValidationError as e:
113 |             # Trivial case failed. Try for possible IDN domain
114 |             if value:
115 |                 try:
116 |                     scheme, netloc, path, query, fragment = urlsplit(value)
117 |                 except ValueError:  # for example, "Invalid IPv6 URL"
118 |                     raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)
119 |                 try:
120 |                     netloc = punycode(netloc)  # IDN -> ACE
121 |                 except UnicodeError:  # invalid domain part
122 |                     raise e
123 |                 url = urlunsplit((scheme, netloc, path, query, fragment))
124 |                 super().__call__(url)
125 |             else:
126 |                 raise
127 |         else:
128 |             # Now verify IPv6 in the netloc part
129 |             host_match = re.search(r'^\[(.+)\](?::\d{2,5})?$', urlsplit(value).netloc)
130 |             if host_match:
131 |                 potential_ip = host_match[1]
132 |                 try:
133 |                     validate_ipv6_address(potential_ip)
134 |                 except ValidationError:
135 |                     raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)
136 | 
137 |         # The maximum length of a full host name is 253 characters per RFC 1034
138 |         # section 3.1. It's defined to be 255 bytes or less, but this includes
139 |         # one byte for the length of the name and one byte for the trailing dot
140 |         # that's used to indicate absolute names in DNS.
141 |         if len(urlsplit(value).netloc) > 253:
142 |             raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)
143 | 
144 | 
145 | integer_validator = RegexValidator(
146 |     _lazy_re_compile(r'^-?\d+\Z'),
147 |     message=_('Enter a valid integer.'),
148 |     code='invalid',
149 | )
150 | 
151 | 
152 | def validate_integer(value):
153 |     return integer_validator(value)

156 | @deconstructible
157 | class EmailValidator:
158 |     message = _('Enter a valid email address.')
159 |     code = 'invalid'
160 |     user_regex = _lazy_re_compile(
161 |         r"(^[-!#$%&'*+/=?^_`{}|~0-9A-Z]+(\.[-!#$%&'*+/=?^_`{}|~0-9A-Z]+)*\Z"  # dot-atom
162 |         r'|^"([\001-\010\013\014\016-\037!#-\[\]-\177]|\\[\001-\011\013\014\016-\177])*"\Z)',  # quoted-string
163 |         re.IGNORECASE)
164 |     domain_regex = _lazy_re_compile(
165 |         # max length for domain name labels is 63 characters per RFC 1034
166 |         r'((?:[A-Z0-9](?:[A-Z0-9-]{0,61}[A-Z0-9])?\.)+)(?:[A-Z0-9-]{2,63}(?<!-))\Z',
167 |         re.IGNORECASE)
168 |     literal_regex = _lazy_re_compile(
169 |         # literal form, ipv4 or ipv6 address (SMTP 4.1.3)
170 |         r'\[([A-f0-9:.]+)\]\Z',
171 |         re.IGNORECASE)
172 |     domain_allowlist = ['localhost']
173 |

178 |             'domain_allowlist.',

189 |             RemovedInDjango41Warning,
190 |             stacklevel=2,

194 |     def __init__(self, message=None, code=None, allowlist=None, *, whitelist=None):
195 |         if whitelist is not None:
196 |             allowlist = whitelist
197 |             warnings.warn(
198 |                 'The whitelist argument is deprecated in favor of allowlist.',
199 |                 RemovedInDjango41Warning,
200 |                 stacklevel=2,
201 |             )
202 |         if message is not None:
203 |             self.message = message
204 |         if code is not None:
205 |             self.code = code
206 |         if allowlist is not None:
207 |             self.domain_allowlist = allowlist
208 | 
209 |     def __call__(self, value):
210 |         if not value or '@' not in value:
211 |             raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)
212 | 
213 |         user_part, domain_part = value.rsplit('@', 1)
214 | 
215 |         if not self.user_regex.match(user_part):
216 |             raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)
217 | 
218 |         if (domain_part not in self.domain_allowlist and
219 |                 not self.validate_domain_part(domain_part)):
220 |             # Try for possible IDN domain-part
221 |             try:
222 |                 domain_part = punycode(domain_part)
223 |             except UnicodeError:
224 |                 pass
225 |             else:
226 |                 if self.validate_domain_part(domain_part):
227 |                     return
228 |             raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)
229 | 
230 |     def validate_domain_part(self, domain_part):
231 |         if self.domain_regex.match(domain_part):
232 |             return True
233 | 
234 |         literal_match = self.literal_regex.match(domain_part)
235 |         if literal_match:
236 |             ip_address = literal_match[1]
237 |             try:
238 |                 validate_ipv46_address(ip_address)
239 |                 return True
240 |             except ValidationError:
241 |                 pass
242 |         return False
243 | 
244 |     def __eq__(self, other):
245 |         return (
246 |             isinstance(other, EmailValidator) and
247 |             (self.domain_allowlist == other.domain_allowlist) and
248 |             (self.message == other.message) and
249 |             (self.code == other.code)

328 | @deconstructible
329 | class BaseValidator:
330 |     message = _('Ensure this value is %(limit_value)s (it is %(show_value)s).')
331 |     code = 'limit_value'
332 | 
333 |     def __init__(self, limit_value, message=None):
334 |         self.limit_value = limit_value
335 |         if message:
336 |             self.message = message
337 | 
338 |     def __call__(self, value):
339 |         cleaned = self.clean(value)
340 |         limit_value = self.limit_value() if callable(self.limit_value) else self.limit_value
341 |         params = {'limit_value': limit_value, 'show_value': cleaned, 'value': value}
342 |         if self.compare(cleaned, limit_value):
343 |             raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code, params=params)
344 | 
345 |     def __eq__(self, other):
346 |         if not isinstance(other, self.__class__):
347 |             return NotImplemented
348 |         return (
349 |             self.limit_value == other.limit_value and
350 |             self.message == other.message and
351 |             self.code == other.code
352 |         )

409 | @deconstructible
410 | class DecimalValidator:
411 |     """
412 |     Validate that the input does not exceed the maximum number of digits
413 |     expected, otherwise raise ValidationError.
414 |     """
415 |     messages = {
416 |         'invalid': _('Enter a number.'),
417 |         'max_digits': ngettext_lazy(
418 |             'Ensure that there are no more than %(max)s digit in total.',
419 |             'Ensure that there are no more than %(max)s digits in total.',
420 |             'max'
421 |         ),
422 |         'max_decimal_places': ngettext_lazy(
423 |             'Ensure that there are no more than %(max)s decimal place.',
424 |             'Ensure that there are no more than %(max)s decimal places.',
425 |             'max'
426 |         ),
427 |         'max_whole_digits': ngettext_lazy(
428 |             'Ensure that there are no more than %(max)s digit before the decimal point.',
429 |             'Ensure that there are no more than %(max)s digits before the decimal point.',
430 |             'max'
431 |         ),
432 |     }
433 | 
434 |     def __init__(self, max_digits, decimal_places):
435 |         self.max_digits = max_digits
436 |         self.decimal_places = decimal_places
437 | 
438 |     def __call__(self, value):
439 |         digit_tuple, exponent = value.as_tuple()[1:]
440 |         if exponent in {'F', 'n', 'N'}:
441 |             raise ValidationError(self.messages['invalid'])
442 |         if exponent >= 0:
443 |             # A positive exponent adds that many trailing zeros.
444 |             digits = len(digit_tuple) + exponent
445 |             decimals = 0
446 |         else:
447 |             # If the absolute value of the negative exponent is larger than the
448 |             # number of digits, then it's the same as the number of digits,
449 |             # because it'll consume all of the digits in digit_tuple and then
450 |             # add abs(exponent) - len(digit_tuple) leading zeros after the
451 |             # decimal point.
452 |             if abs(exponent) > len(digit_tuple):
453 |                 digits = decimals = abs(exponent)
454 |             else:
455 |                 digits = len(digit_tuple)
456 |                 decimals = abs(exponent)
457 |         whole_digits = digits - decimals
458 | 
459 |         if self.max_digits is not None and digits > self.max_digits:
460 |             raise ValidationError(
461 |                 self.messages['max_digits'],
462 |                 code='max_digits',
463 |                 params={'max': self.max_digits},
464 |             )
465 |         if self.decimal_places is not None and decimals > self.decimal_places:
466 |             raise ValidationError(
467 |                 self.messages['max_decimal_places'],
468 |                 code='max_decimal_places',
469 |                 params={'max': self.decimal_places},
470 |             )
471 |         if (self.max_digits is not None and self.decimal_places is not None and
472 |                 whole_digits > (self.max_digits - self.decimal_places)):
473 |             raise ValidationError(
474 |                 self.messages['max_whole_digits'],
475 |                 code='max_whole_digits',
476 |                 params={'max': (self.max_digits - self.decimal_places)},
477 |             )
478 | 
479 |     def __eq__(self, other):
480 |         return (
481 |             isinstance(other, self.__class__) and
482 |             self.max_digits == other.max_digits and
483 |             self.decimal_places == other.decimal_places
484 |         )
485 |

492 |     )

 22 | def get_password_validators(validator_config):
 23 |     validators = []
 24 |     for validator in validator_config:
 25 |         try:
 26 |             klass = import_string(validator['NAME'])
 27 |         except ImportError:
 28 |             msg = "The module in NAME could not be imported: %s. Check your AUTH_PASSWORD_VALIDATORS setting."
 29 |             raise ImproperlyConfigured(msg % validator['NAME'])
 30 |         validators.append(klass(**validator.get('OPTIONS', {})))
 31 | 
 32 |     return validators

 34 | 
 35 | def validate_password(password, user=None, password_validators=None):
 36 |     """
 37 |     Validate whether the password meets all validator requirements.
 38 | 
 39 |     If the password is valid, return ``None``.
 40 |     If the password is invalid, raise ValidationError with all error messages.
 41 |     """
 42 |     errors = []
 43 |     if password_validators is None:
 44 |         password_validators = get_default_password_validators()
 45 |     for validator in password_validators:
 46 |         try:
 47 |             validator.validate(password, user)
 48 |         except ValidationError as error:
 49 |             errors.append(error)
 50 |     if errors:
 51 |         raise ValidationError(errors)
 52 |

 54 | def password_changed(password, user=None, password_validators=None):
 55 |     """
 56 |     Inform all validators that have implemented a password_changed() method
 57 |     that the password has been changed.
 58 |     """
 59 |     if password_validators is None:
 60 |         password_validators = get_default_password_validators()
 61 |     for validator in password_validators:
 62 |         password_changed = getattr(validator, 'password_changed', lambda *a: None)
 63 |         password_changed(password, user)

 69 |     """
 70 |     help_texts = []
 71 |     if password_validators is None:
 72 |         password_validators = get_default_password_validators()
 73 |     for validator in password_validators:

118 | class UserAttributeSimilarityValidator:
119 |     """
120 |     Validate whether the password is sufficiently different from the user's
121 |     attributes.
122 | 
123 |     If no specific attributes are provided, look at a sensible list of
124 |     defaults. Attributes that don't exist are ignored. Comparison is made to
125 |     not only the full attribute value, but also its components, so that, for
126 |     example, a password is validated against either part of an email address,
127 |     as well as the full address.
128 |     """
129 |     DEFAULT_USER_ATTRIBUTES = ('username', 'first_name', 'last_name', 'email')
130 | 
131 |     def __init__(self, user_attributes=DEFAULT_USER_ATTRIBUTES, max_similarity=0.7):
132 |         self.user_attributes = user_attributes
133 |         self.max_similarity = max_similarity
134 | 
135 |     def validate(self, password, user=None):
136 |         if not user:
137 |             return
138 | 
139 |         for attribute_name in self.user_attributes:
140 |             value = getattr(user, attribute_name, None)
141 |             if not value or not isinstance(value, str):
142 |                 continue
143 |             value_parts = re.split(r'\W+', value) + [value]
144 |             for value_part in value_parts:
145 |                 if SequenceMatcher(a=password.lower(), b=value_part.lower()).quick_ratio() >= self.max_similarity:
146 |                     try:
147 |                         verbose_name = str(user._meta.get_field(attribute_name).verbose_name)
148 |                     except FieldDoesNotExist:
149 |                         verbose_name = attribute_name
150 |                     raise ValidationError(
151 |                         _("The password is too similar to the %(verbose_name)s."),
152 |                         code='password_too_similar',
153 |                         params={'verbose_name': verbose_name},
154 |                     )
155 | 
156 |     def get_help_text(self):
157 |         return _('Your password can’t be too similar to your other personal information.')
158 | 
159 | 
160 | class CommonPasswordValidator:
161 |     """
162 |     Validate whether the password is a common password.
163 | 
164 |     The password is rejected if it occurs in a provided list of passwords,
165 |     which may be gzipped. The list Django ships with contains 20000 common
166 |     passwords (lowercased and deduplicated), created by Royce Williams:
167 |     https://gist.github.com/roycewilliams/281ce539915a947a23db17137d91aeb7
168 |     The password list must be lowercased to match the comparison in validate().
169 |     """

173 |         try:
174 |             with gzip.open(password_list_path, 'rt', encoding='utf-8') as f:
175 |                 self.passwords = {x.strip() for x in f}
176 |         except OSError:
177 |             with open(password_list_path) as f:
178 |                 self.passwords = {x.strip() for x in f}
179 | 
180 |     def validate(self, password, user=None):

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

Session data cannot be decoded during the transition to Django 3.1.
Description
	
In d4fff711d4c97356bd6ba1273d2a5e349326eb5f (#31274) we've changed format for session data, that's why setting DEFAULT_HASHING_ALGORITHM to 'sha1' is not enough to support running multiple instances of the same project during the transition to Django 3.1.
We could use the legacy encode() when DEFAULT_HASHING_ALGORITHM == 'sha1' (it's a bit hacky).

  1 | """
  2 | Functions for creating and restoring url-safe signed JSON objects.
  3 | 
  4 | The format used looks like this:
  5 | 
  6 | >>> signing.dumps("hello")
  7 | 'ImhlbGxvIg:1QaUZC:YIye-ze3TTx7gtSv422nZA4sgmk'
  8 | 
  9 | There are two components here, separated by a ':'. The first component is a
 10 | URLsafe base64 encoded JSON of the object passed to dumps(). The second
 11 | component is a base64 encoded hmac/SHA1 hash of "$first_component:$secret"
 12 | 
 13 | signing.loads(s) checks the signature and returns the deserialized object.
 14 | If the signature fails, a BadSignature exception is raised.
 15 | 
 16 | >>> signing.loads("ImhlbGxvIg:1QaUZC:YIye-ze3TTx7gtSv422nZA4sgmk")
 17 | 'hello'
 18 | >>> signing.loads("ImhlbGxvIg:1QaUZC:YIye-ze3TTx7gtSv422nZA4sgmk-modified")
 19 | ...
 20 | BadSignature: Signature failed: ImhlbGxvIg:1QaUZC:YIye-ze3TTx7gtSv422nZA4sgmk-modified
 21 | 
 22 | You can optionally compress the JSON prior to base64 encoding it to save
 23 | space, using the compress=True argument. This checks if compression actually
 24 | helps and only applies compression if the result is a shorter string:
 25 | 
 26 | >>> signing.dumps(list(range(1, 20)), compress=True)
 27 | '.eJwFwcERACAIwLCF-rCiILN47r-GyZVJsNgkxaFxoDgxcOHGxMKD_T7vhAml:1QaUaL:BA0thEZrp4FQVXIXuOvYJtLJSrQ'
 28 | 
 29 | The fact that the string is compressed is signalled by the prefixed '.' at the
 30 | start of the base64 JSON.
 31 | 
 32 | There are 65 url-safe characters: the 64 used by url-safe base64 and the ':'.
 33 | These functions make use of all of them.

 63 |     return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(s).strip(b'=')

 82 |     """
 83 |     Simple wrapper around json to be used in signing.dumps and

 86 |     def dumps(self, obj):
 87 |         return json.dumps(obj, separators=(',', ':')).encode('latin-1')

 89 |     def loads(self, data):
 90 |         return json.loads(data.decode('latin-1'))

 92 | 
 93 | def dumps(obj, key=None, salt='django.core.signing', serializer=JSONSerializer, compress=False):
 94 |     """
 95 |     Return URL-safe, hmac signed base64 compressed JSON string. If key is
 96 |     None, use settings.SECRET_KEY instead. The hmac algorithm is the default
 97 |     Signer algorithm.
 98 | 
 99 |     If compress is True (not the default), check if compressing using zlib can
100 |     save some space. Prepend a '.' to signify compression. This is included
101 |     in the signature, to protect against zip bombs.
102 | 
103 |     Salt can be used to namespace the hash, so that a signed string is
104 |     only valid for a given namespace. Leaving this at the default
105 |     value or re-using a salt value across different parts of your
106 |     application without good cause is a security risk.
107 | 
108 |     The serializer is expected to return a bytestring.
109 |     """
110 |     data = serializer().dumps(obj)
111 | 
112 |     # Flag for if it's been compressed or not
113 |     is_compressed = False
114 | 
115 |     if compress:
116 |         # Avoid zlib dependency unless compress is being used
117 |         compressed = zlib.compress(data)
118 |         if len(compressed) < (len(data) - 1):
119 |             data = compressed
120 |             is_compressed = True
121 |     base64d = b64_encode(data).decode()
122 |     if is_compressed:
123 |         base64d = '.' + base64d
124 |     return TimestampSigner(key, salt=salt).sign(base64d)
125 | 
126 | 
127 | def loads(s, key=None, salt='django.core.signing', serializer=JSONSerializer, max_age=None):
128 |     """
129 |     Reverse of dumps(), raise BadSignature if signature fails.
130 | 
131 |     The serializer is expected to accept a bytestring.
132 |     """
133 |     # TimestampSigner.unsign() returns str but base64 and zlib compression
134 |     # operate on bytes.
135 |     base64d = TimestampSigner(key, salt=salt).unsign(s, max_age=max_age).encode()
136 |     decompress = base64d[:1] == b'.'
137 |     if decompress:
138 |         # It's compressed; uncompress it first
139 |         base64d = base64d[1:]
140 |     data = b64_decode(base64d)
141 |     if decompress:
142 |         data = zlib.decompress(data)
143 |     return serializer().loads(data)
144 | 
145 | 
146 | class Signer:
147 |     # RemovedInDjango40Warning.
148 |     legacy_algorithm = 'sha1'
149 | 
150 |     def __init__(self, key=None, sep=':', salt=None, algorithm=None):
151 |         self.key = key or settings.SECRET_KEY
152 |         self.sep = sep
153 |         if _SEP_UNSAFE.match(self.sep):
154 |             raise ValueError(
155 |                 'Unsafe Signer separator: %r (cannot be empty or consist of '
156 |                 'only A-z0-9-_=)' % sep,
157 |             )
158 |         self.salt = salt or '%s.%s' % (self.__class__.__module__, self.__class__.__name__)
159 |         # RemovedInDjango40Warning: when the deprecation ends, replace with:
160 |         # self.algorithm = algorithm or 'sha256'
161 |         self.algorithm = algorithm or settings.DEFAULT_HASHING_ALGORITHM
162 | 
163 |     def signature(self, value):
164 |         return base64_hmac(self.salt + 'signer', value, self.key, algorithm=self.algorithm)
165 | 
166 |     def _legacy_signature(self, value):
167 |         # RemovedInDjango40Warning.
168 |         return base64_hmac(self.salt + 'signer', value, self.key, algorithm=self.legacy_algorithm)
169 | 
170 |     def sign(self, value):
171 |         return '%s%s%s' % (value, self.sep, self.signature(value))
172 | 
173 |     def unsign(self, signed_value):
174 |         if self.sep not in signed_value:
175 |             raise BadSignature('No "%s" found in value' % self.sep)
176 |         value, sig = signed_value.rsplit(self.sep, 1)
177 |         if (
178 |             constant_time_compare(sig, self.signature(value)) or (
179 |                 self.legacy_algorithm and
180 |                 constant_time_compare(sig, self._legacy_signature(value))
181 |             )
182 |         ):
183 |             return value
184 |         raise BadSignature('Signature "%s" does not match' % sig)
185 | 
186 | 
187 | class TimestampSigner(Signer):
188 | 
189 |     def timestamp(self):
190 |         return baseconv.base62.encode(int(time.time()))
191 | 
192 |     def sign(self, value):
193 |         value = '%s%s%s' % (value, self.sep, self.timestamp())
194 |         return super().sign(value)
195 | 
196 |     def unsign(self, value, max_age=None):
197 |         """
198 |         Retrieve original value and check it wasn't signed more
199 |         than max_age seconds ago.
200 |         """
201 |         result = super().unsign(value)
202 |         value, timestamp = result.rsplit(self.sep, 1)
203 |         timestamp = baseconv.base62.decode(timestamp)
204 |         if max_age is not None:
205 |             if isinstance(max_age, datetime.timedelta):
206 |                 max_age = max_age.total_seconds()
207 |             # Check timestamp is not older than max_age
208 |             age = time.time() - timestamp
209 |             if age > max_age:
210 |                 raise SignatureExpired(
211 |                     'Signature age %s > %s seconds' % (age, max_age))
212 |         return value

[106/500] django__django-13297

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

TemplateView.get_context_data()'s kwargs returns SimpleLazyObjects that causes a crash when filtering.
Description
	
Example Code that works in 3.0, but not in 3.1:
class OfferView(TemplateView):
	template_name = "offers/offer.html"
	def get_context_data(self, **kwargs):
		offer_slug = kwargs.get("offer_slug", "")
		offer = get_object_or_404(Account, slug=offer_slug)
		return {"offer": offer, "offer_slug": offer_slug}
In order to make this work in 3.1, you have to explicitly convert the result of kwargs.get() to a string to get the SimpleLazyObject to resolve:
class OfferView(TemplateView):
	template_name = "offers/offer.html"
	def get_context_data(self, **kwargs):
		offer_slug = kwargs.get("offer_slug", "")
		offer = get_object_or_404(Account, slug=str(offer_slug))
		return {"offer": offer, "offer_slug": offer_slug}
The error generated if you don't is:
Error binding parameter 0 - probably unsupported type
from django/db/backends/sqlite3/operations.py, line 144, in _quote_params_for_last_executed_query
In both cases, the urls.py looks like:
path(
		"/offers/<slug:offer_slug>/",
		OfferView.as_view(),
		name="offer_view",
	),
When debugging, I found that offer_slug (coming in from kwargs.get) was of type 'SimpleLazyObject' in Django 3.1, and when I explicitly converted it to a string, get_object_or_404 behaved as expected.
This is using Python 3.7.8 with SQLite.



Hints:
Thanks for the report. get_object_or_404() and QuerySet.filter() with SimpleLazyObject throw the same exception in Django 2.2 or 3.0. TemplateView.get_context_data()'s kwargs returns SimpleLazyObjects in Django 3.1 which causes a crash. Passing URL kwargs into context is deprecated (see #19878) but should still work in Django 3.1 and 3.2. Regression in 4ed534758cb6a11df9f49baddecca5a6cdda9311. Reproduced at 60626162f76f26d32a38d18151700cb041201fb3.

## Instructions

1. Use `Grep` and `Glob` to locate the relevant source files. Start by searching for key class names, function names, or error messages mentioned in the issue.

5. Use `Read` to verify your edit was applied correctly.

 45 |         """
 46 |         # Go through keyword arguments, and either save their values to our
 47 |         # instance, or raise an error.
 48 |         for key, value in kwargs.items():
 49 |             setattr(self, key, value)
 50 | 
 51 |     @classonlymethod
 52 |     def as_view(cls, **initkwargs):
 53 |         """Main entry point for a request-response process."""
 54 |         for key in initkwargs:
 55 |             if key in cls.http_method_names:
 56 |                 raise TypeError(
 57 |                     'The method name %s is not accepted as a keyword argument '
 58 |                     'to %s().' % (key, cls.__name__)
 59 |                 )
 60 |             if not hasattr(cls, key):
 61 |                 raise TypeError("%s() received an invalid keyword %r. as_view "
 62 |                                 "only accepts arguments that are already "
 63 |                                 "attributes of the class." % (cls.__name__, key))
 64 | 
 65 |         def view(request, *args, **kwargs):
 66 |             self = cls(**initkwargs)
 67 |             self.setup(request, *args, **kwargs)
 68 |             if not hasattr(self, 'request'):
 69 |                 raise AttributeError(
 70 |                     "%s instance has no 'request' attribute. Did you override "
 71 |                     "setup() and forget to call super()?" % cls.__name__
 72 |                 )
 73 |             return self.dispatch(request, *args, **kwargs)
 74 |         view.view_class = cls
 75 |         view.view_initkwargs = initkwargs
 76 | 
 77 |         # take name and docstring from class
 78 |         update_wrapper(view, cls, updated=())
 79 | 
 80 |         # and possible attributes set by decorators
 81 |         # like csrf_exempt from dispatch
 82 |         update_wrapper(view, cls.dispatch, assigned=())
 83 |         return view
 84 | 
 85 |     def setup(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
 86 |         """Initialize attributes shared by all view methods."""
 87 |         if hasattr(self, 'get') and not hasattr(self, 'head'):
 88 |             self.head = self.get
 89 |         self.request = request
 90 |         self.args = args
 91 |         self.kwargs = kwargs
 92 | 
 93 |     def dispatch(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
 94 |         # Try to dispatch to the right method; if a method doesn't exist,
 95 |         # defer to the error handler. Also defer to the error handler if the
 96 |         # request method isn't on the approved list.
 97 |         if request.method.lower() in self.http_method_names:
 98 |             handler = getattr(self, request.method.lower(), self.http_method_not_allowed)
 99 |         else:
100 |             handler = self.http_method_not_allowed
101 |         return handler(request, *args, **kwargs)
102 | 
103 |     def http_method_not_allowed(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
104 |         logger.warning(
105 |             'Method Not Allowed (%s): %s', request.method, request.path,
106 |             extra={'status_code': 405, 'request': request}
107 |         )
108 |         return HttpResponseNotAllowed(self._allowed_methods())
109 | 
110 |     def options(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
111 |         """Handle responding to requests for the OPTIONS HTTP verb."""
112 |         response = HttpResponse()
113 |         response['Allow'] = ', '.join(self._allowed_methods())
114 |         response['Content-Length'] = '0'
115 |         return response
116 | 
117 |     def _allowed_methods(self):
118 |         return [m.upper() for m in self.http_method_names if hasattr(self, m)]
119 | 
120 | 
121 | class TemplateResponseMixin:
122 |     """A mixin that can be used to render a template."""
123 |     template_name = None
124 |     template_engine = None
125 |     response_class = TemplateResponse
126 |     content_type = None
127 | 
128 |     def render_to_response(self, context, **response_kwargs):
129 |         """
130 |         Return a response, using the `response_class` for this view, with a
131 |         template rendered with the given context.
132 | 
133 |         Pass response_kwargs to the constructor of the response class.
134 |         """
135 |         response_kwargs.setdefault('content_type', self.content_type)
136 |         return self.response_class(
137 |             request=self.request,
138 |             template=self.get_template_names(),
139 |             context=context,
140 |             using=self.template_engine,
141 |             **response_kwargs
142 |         )
143 | 
144 |     def get_template_names(self):
145 |         """
146 |         Return a list of template names to be used for the request. Must return
147 |         a list. May not be called if render_to_response() is overridden.
148 |         """
149 |         if self.template_name is None:
150 |             raise ImproperlyConfigured(
151 |                 "TemplateResponseMixin requires either a definition of "
152 |                 "'template_name' or an implementation of 'get_template_names()'")
153 |         else:
154 |             return [self.template_name]
155 | 
156 | 
157 | class TemplateView(TemplateResponseMixin, ContextMixin, View):
158 |     """Render a template."""
159 |     def get(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
160 |         # RemovedInDjango40Warning: when the deprecation ends, replace with:
161 |         #   context = self.get_context_data()
162 |         context_kwargs = _wrap_url_kwargs_with_deprecation_warning(kwargs)
163 |         context = self.get_context_data(**context_kwargs)
164 |         return self.render_to_response(context)
165 | 
166 | 
167 | # RemovedInDjango40Warning
168 | def _wrap_url_kwargs_with_deprecation_warning(url_kwargs):
169 |     context_kwargs = {}
170 |     for key, value in url_kwargs.items():
171 |         # Bind into function closure.
172 |         @SimpleLazyObject
173 |         def access_value(key=key, value=value):
174 |             warnings.warn(
175 |                 'TemplateView passing URL kwargs to the context is '
176 |                 'deprecated. Reference %s in your template through '
177 |                 'view.kwargs instead.' % key,
178 |                 RemovedInDjango40Warning, stacklevel=2,
179 |             )
180 |             return value
181 |         context_kwargs[key] = access_value
182 |     return context_kwargs
183 | 
184 | 
185 | class RedirectView(View):
186 |     """Provide a redirect on any GET request."""
187 |     permanent = False
188 |     url = None
189 |     pattern_name = None
190 |     query_string = False
191 | 
192 |     def get_redirect_url(self, *args, **kwargs):
193 |         """
194 |         Return the URL redirect to. Keyword arguments from the URL pattern
195 |         match generating the redirect request are provided as kwargs to this
196 |         method.
197 |         """
198 |         if self.url:
199 |             url = self.url % kwargs
200 |         elif self.pattern_name:
201 |             url = reverse(self.pattern_name, args=args, kwargs=kwargs)
202 |         else:
203 |             return None
204 | 
205 |         args = self.request.META.get('QUERY_STRING', '')
206 |         if args and self.query_string:
207 |             url = "%s?%s" % (url, args)
208 |         return url
209 | 
210 |     def get(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
211 |         url = self.get_redirect_url(*args, **kwargs)
212 |         if url:
213 |             if self.permanent:
214 |                 return HttpResponsePermanentRedirect(url)
215 |             else:
216 |                 return HttpResponseRedirect(url)
217 |         else:
218 |             logger.warning(
219 |                 'Gone: %s', request.path,
220 |                 extra={'status_code': 410, 'request': request}
221 |             )
222 |             return HttpResponseGone()
223 | 
224 |     def head(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
225 |         return self.get(request, *args, **kwargs)

227 |     def post(self, request, *args, **kwargs):

🤖 bench-agent processing: You are a software engineer fixing a bug in the `django/django` repository.

## Issue

limit_choices_to on a ForeignKey can render duplicate options in formfield
Description
	
If you pass a Q object as limit_choices_to on a ForeignKey field involving a join, you may end up with duplicate options in your form.
See regressiontest in patch for a clear view on the problem.

This is nasty because it not only renders duplicates but also blows up when .get() is called on the queryset if you select one of the duplicates (MultipleObjectsReturned).

   2 | Helper functions for creating Form classes from Django models
   3 | and database field objects.
